James Clarke (VC) (1831–1864) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

He was approximately 33 years old, and a sergeant in the 2nd Battalion, The Rifle Brigade (Prince Consort's Own), British Army during the Indian Mutiny when the following deed took place for which he was awarded the VC.

On 16 November 1857 at Lucknow, India, Sergeant Clarke, with two other sergeants, volunteered to go out and bring in a wounded man who was lying close to the enemy's position. The three sergeants succeeded in rescuing the man, but Sergeant Clarke was severely wounded in the attempt.

He died of his wounds on 28 December 1864 at the age of 33 and was buried in St. Mary's Churchyard, Islington, London.

James Clarke VC (2 June 1891 – 22 October 1917) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

For most conspicuous bravery. When his platoon, held up by a very strong point, was suffering severely, Pte. Clarke, on his own initiative, crawled forward and attacked single-handed, bombing a dug-out containing ten of the enemy, who ran out in disorder and were mopped up. He then attacked a second dug-out, and though shot in the shoulder and again attacked by an enemy bomber whom he bayonetted, he rushed forward bombing and bayoneting the enemy in five separate dug-outs until the German strong point was completely overcome. His gallant single-handed fighting enabled very important objectives to be taken before dark.

James Clarke (VC) (23 August 1819 – 13 April 1897) was a British recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

James Clarke VC was a British soldier who was posthumously award the Victoria Cross (VC) for his bravery and daring under fire during the Battle of Maiwand, fought in Afghanistan on 27 July 1880. He was a private serving in the 2nd Battalion, 66th Regiment of Foot.

During the battle, Clarke was stationed in the outer defences of the British position and managed to hold out against what was to be a successful Afghan attack. As the Afghans advanced, he and his comrades held their positions until finally a determined rush of the enemy forced them to the British defences. Clarke sprang forward, caught the leader of the enemy and held them off at bayonet-point. Billy Burke (born October 28, 1894 – died April 11, 1981) was an American professional golfer who won the PGA Championship in 1938. He was born in St. Louis, Missouri, and was the son of a professional golfer. Burke turned professional in 1915 and won the Missouri Open in 1916. He won the PGA Championship in 1938 at the Pomonok Country Club in Flushing, New York, defeating Denny Shute in the final match. Burke was the first golfer to win the PGA Championship in match play since Jim Barnes in 1916.

Burke was a member of the PGA of America for more than 50 years and was the head professional at the St. Louis Country Club from 1921 to 1965. He was inducted into the PGA Hall of Fame in 1978. Burke was also a member of the United States Ryder Cup team in 1927 and 1937. He was the first American to win the British Amateur in 1930. Burke died in 1981 at the age of 86.

Billy Burke (Born January 15, 1902 – November 17, 1996) was an American professional golfer. Burke was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, and was the son of an Irish immigrant. He first took up golf as a caddie at the age of 11, at the storied Merion Golf Club in nearby Ardmore, Pennsylvania. He began his professional career in 1921. He was the 1922 PGA Champion and was runner-up to Gene Sarazen in the 1923 U.S. Open. He was also a member of the Ryder Cup team in 1927, 1929 and 1935. In October 1955, he became the oldest man to ever make a hole-in-one in a PGA tournament. He was inducted into the World Golf Hall of Fame in 1994. Burke was known as one of the best "shotmakers" of his time and was renowned for his ability to hit high and soft shots under pressure. He was known for his lifelong love of the game, his enthusiasm and his respect for his peers.

Billy Burke (November 28, 1896 – May 9, 1953) was an American professional golfer who competed mainly in the late 1920s and early 1930s. He was born in Augusta, Georgia, and initially earned his living as a caddie. As a professional golfer, Burke achieved some success, most notably finishing tied for fourth in the 1931 U.S. Open and winning the 1932 Philadelphia Inquirer Open. Burke then moved to California and won several California based tournaments in the mid 1930s. He is perhaps best remembered for his efforts in the 1932 Ryder Cup at Game Grove in Musselburgh, Scotland. He won his singles match against Hennie Otto either one up or two up (according to reports), and was one of three Americans to win their singles matches that day.

Burke stopped competing in tournaments in 1937 and eventually retired to Florida. He died in 1953 at age 56 at St. Petersburg Florida of a heart attack after having gone to a place near his home to fish at the Gulf of Mexico.

Billy Burke (January 9, 1885 – August 5, 1951) was an American professional golfer who was one of the first superstars of the game. He won 22 professional tournaments in a career that spanned five decades, including nine major championships. He won four U.S. Opens, two British Opens, two PGA Championships and one Canadian Open. He was also the first golfer to win all four majors, something that wasn't accomplished again until Ben Hogan in 1953. Burke is credited with popularizing the sport of golf, as he was one of the first golfers to gain widespread recognition outside of the United States. He was also one of the first golfers to make money playing the sport professionally and achieved a great level of fame and recognition.

Billy Burke (born 1922) is an American professional golfer who made fourteen appearances in the U.S. Open and six successive appearances in the Masters Tournament. He was born in Hannibal, Missouri, and is best remembered for: his win in the 1942 PGA Championship; placing third in the 1947 Masters; and for his runner-up finish at the U.S. Open in 1948.

Burke's golf career began as a caddiemaster at his local golf club, and after that he began playing competitively. During his career, he won twenty-five professional golf tournaments and was a four-time member of the U.S. Ryder Cup team. He also had three top-ten finishes in the major championships. After retiring from competitive golf in 1969, he taught golf at several courses only to come back to professional golf once again in 1974 and went on to serve as the president of the Senior PGA Tour (now the Champions Tour).

In 2002, Burke was inducted into the Missouri Sports Hall of Fame.

Billy Burke (born March 22, 1955) is an American professional golfer. He turned professional in 1976 and has won several minor tournaments in the United States and abroad. A two-time winner of the Asian Tour, he was also a two-time All-American at the University of Miami.

Burke started playing golf at an early age, beginning as a caddy, and then moving on to a nursery pro. Billy Burke (April 30, 1897 – December 24, 1946) was an American professional golfer. He won four major championships: the U.S. Open (1929, 1931), the British Open (1922) and the PGA Championship (1938).

Burke was born in St. Simons Island, Georgia, to a family of Irish descent. His father, William Burke, Sr., was a jack-of-all-trades, described as a property manager, shipping clerk, railroad worker and carpenter. He first learned the game of golf at the age of 16, carrying his bag at local greens. In 1920, he left St. Simons Island to join the professional golfing tour.

Billy Burke (April 3, 1893 – June 27, 1951) was an American professional golfer. He won eleven PGA Tour events during his career, including the 1931 PGA Championship, and the 1932 and 1933 Western Opens. He also had four runner-up finishes in the U.S. Open.

A native of Dayton, Ohio, Burke was a frequent competitor in the Ohio Open from 1922 to 1936, winning the title five times. He competed on the Ryder Cup team in both 1927 and 1933, captaining the latter American team.

Burke finished his career as the head professional at Tacoma Country and Golf Club where he resided for 22 years. He was inducted into the World Golf Hall of Fame in 1978. He died in Tacoma, Washington at the age of 58.

Billy Burke (March 8, 1882 – June 14, 1951) was an American professional golfer. Born in Brookline, Massachusetts, he was a two-time winner of the U.S. Open (1908, 1911). His 1911 win made him the first wire-to-wire winner in the tournament, leading the field after each round. Burke also won the 1914 Western Open, and was the runner-up in the PGA Championship in 1915. He had eight other top-10 finishes in the U.S. Open and three other top-10 finishes in the PGA Championship. He was inducted into the World Golf Hall of Fame in 1978. Burke won a total of 46 professional tournaments over the course of his career. He died in Pawtucket, Rhode Island in 1951, of a heart attack at age 69.

Billy Burke (September 28, 1896 – April 23, 1959) was an American professional golfer active in the 1920s and 1930s. Billy Burke (14 November 1881 – 28 December 1935) was an American professional golfer who won the inaugural US Open in 1895.

Burke was born in Path Fork, Kentucky and was known as the "Little Kentuckian". He started caddying at the age of twelve and by 1895, at the age of 14, he was playing golf regularly at nearby Idle Hour Country Club in Lexington. He won the U.S. Open at the Newport Country Club in Rhode Island with a score of 324, three strokes ahead of Horace Rawlins. He was the youngest winner of the tournament at that time. Burke also returned the following year in 1896 defending his title, but finished second behind James Foulis. Burke eventually became a professional golfer and golf instructor.

Billy Burke (born March 10, 1904) was an American professional golfer. He won two major championships and a total of 20 PGA Tour events in his career. Burke was born in Corning, New York and was a lifelong resident there. He began to play golf at the age of 13, and won the Western Open in 1924 at age 20. He won the PGA Championship in 1929 and 1933 and also took the Western Open title in 1925, 1929, 1931, and 1932. He was a member of the 1911 U.S. Walker Cup team and was posthumously inducted into the World Golf Hall of Fame in 1979. Burke was known for his strong, reliable putting. He won 12 tournaments in the East and eight in the West, and was known for his accuracy in driving the ball. He died in 1957 in Corning, at age 53.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1891–1964) was an English cricketer, and noted philanthropist. 

As a cricketer, Brentnall played for the Marylebone Cricket Club and the Gentlemen of England in first class cricket matches. He also appeared with various county sides on several occasions, including Surrey, Gloucestershire, and Essex. After retiring from cricket, he spent the remainder of his life engaged in philanthropic endeavours. 

Brentnall was a major benefactor of charitable organisations in England, including the Artificial Limb and Appliance Centre, providing services to people with disabilities. He was also a benefactor of the Royal Brompton Hospital and York Retreat in York, as well as societies that aimed to further the study of music, science and art. Additionally, he was committed to a number of philanthropic projects in the East of England, including a locally based institute for the disabled and a residential accommodation for the disabled. His philanthropic endeavours earned him an MBE in 1952. Brentnall passed away in 1964, at the age of 73

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1843–1917) was a British army officer and collector of antiquities and works of art in the late Victorian and early Edwardian period. He is best known for his extensive collection of Egyptian, Nubian and Sudanese antiquities, which he acquired in the 1880s and 1890s. He also collected coins, medals, armour, pottery, and other works of art. His most famous collection is the Bronze Age mace-head and axes from Beni Hasan near Asyut in Upper Egypt.

Brentnall began his military career in 1864 as a Second Lieutenant in the Royal Scots Regiment of Foot and was later promoted to Captain in 1885, Major in 1891 and Lieutenant-Colonel in 1897. Brentnall served in the Sudan for eight years, from 1883–1891. During this time, he also spent four months exploring the wilds of the southern Sudan, travelling with thirty-five men, in search of antiquities. He found and acquired many ancient artefacts, including those from the tomb of Amenemhat III in Ben

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1842–1931) was an English soldier and soldier-scholar who served with distinction in the Franco-Prussian War, and later established a career as a soldier-scholar in Egypt. He was born in 1842 in Hull, England, and received a British Army commission at the age of twenty. He served in the British Fourth Dragoon Guards in the Franco-Prussian War, distinguishing himself by taking part in several courageous actions.

He then served with the British military forces in Egypt and the Sudan from 1873 to 1883, taking part in the battle of El-Teb and in the campaigns of 1882 and 1883. He officially retired from the British Army in 1884, but remained in Egypt for seven more years, deeply involved in the study of ancient Egyptian and Coptic history, and travelling and teaching in Cairo, Alexandria, and Upper Egypt.

He developed an interest in Coptic Christianity, collecting manuscripts, many of which were eventually donated to the British Library, and contributing substantially to the development of Coptic studies. He returned to England in

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1853–1937) was an English barrister and Conservative politician. He was the Member of Parliament (MP) for the Mid Division of Gloucestershire, 1905-1918.

Brentnall was born in Salisbury in 1853, the fifth and youngest son of William Brentnall of Ebbesborne Wake, Wiltshire. He was educated at Clifton College and Brasenose College, Oxford, where he achieved a first-class honours degree in Law. Following his graduation, he was called to the Bar in 1879.

In 1895, he was appointed Recorder of Bath, a position he held to 1903 when he retired from the Bench. In 1904 he successfully contested the Mid Gloucestershire Division for the Conservatives, being re-elected in the January 1910 election. He retired from parliament at the December 1910 general election.

Brentnall died in 1937 aged 84.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall CBE (21 January 1875 – 4 April 1961) was a British soldier, surveyor and businessman.

Brentnall was born in Dore, Derbyshire, into a Quaker family. He enlisted in the British Army in 1893, eventually reaching the rank of captain by 1914. After serving in World War I, Brentnall took up surveying, focusing on branch line railway engineering, particularly in India. Throughout his surveying career, he was actively involved in public service and was awarded the Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in 1932 in recognition of his service.

After the end of his surveying career in 1948, Brentnall turned his attention to business and invested in several companies. He was noted in particular for his leadership within the industrial shipbuilding industry. He founded the Marine Engineering and Foundry Company, Ltd. in 1949, which eventually became known as the Sumner Brooke Shipyard. Brentnall served as its chairman until his death in 1961.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1845 - 21 March 1917) was an English architect who practiced mainly in Leicester and the Midlands. In 1880 he set up in practice at Leicester and during the next few years designed several well-known buildings in that city. These include warehouses, factories, banks and shops, of which the Eye Hospital (1893) and the Leicester Tram Depot (1898) are particularly noteworthy. He also designed several houses and villas. He was elected LRIBA in 1899. In 1902 he became a partner in the practice of his son, F. J. Hilton Brentnall. He died in 1917 and his son continued his practice until 1937.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (November 1839 – 9 February 1876) was an English architect. He began his career in London in the mid-1860s, and is known for his work on many of the railway stations of the London, Chatham and Dover Railway.

Brentnall was born in November 1839 in Ramsgate, Kent, and was educated at Harrow school. In the mid-1860s, he joined Sir Edward Watkin's architectural practice in London, which was renowned for its engineering works. During his time with the firm, Brentnall worked on many of the London, Chatham and Dover Railway's (LCDR) stations. He designed the Tudor-style St Leonards West Marina station, which opened in 1870 and remains in use today, and the Whitstable and Chatham Harbour stations. He also designed Cliffe station, which is notable for its gabled roof and its surviving original platforms. 

Brentnall died on 9 February 1876 in his London office. He was survived by his wife and two sons, who continued his architectural practice

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1847 – 24 April 1914) was an English musician, composer and teacher. He composed works for chorus and orchestra, and his choral works were widely performed in England in the late nineteenth century. Brentnall was born in Radcliffe, Lancashire in 1847, the son of a hand-loom weaver. He studied music at the University of Manchester and later at the Royal Academy of Music. He was subsequently appointed music lecturer at King's College London.

Brentnall wrote a cantata, The Boy King, which was performed at the Leeds Festival in 1876. He became friends with the conductor of the Leeds Festival, A.J. Jaeger, and the two men later collaborated on a comic opera, The Man in the Moon, which was performed in London in 1881. In addition to his works for chorus and orchestra, Brentnall composed a number of solo songs, school anthems, and pieces for piano and organ. He also composed several well-known hymn tunes. Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1840–1908) was an English architect and surveyor, whose works greatly influenced the urban and ecclesiastical architecture of Birmingham, England. He graduated from the University of Oxford and subsequently Itinerated through Europe and the Near East, studying Medieval Architecture, before returning to London and establishing his practice in 1872.

He moved to Birmingham in 1880, where he became a recognised Architect, gaining particular fame for his ecclesiastical and domestic buildings. Brentnall was appointed a Fellow of the Royal Institute of British Architects in 1884 and was also a member of the Society of Antiquaries of London and the Royal Institute of Chartered Surveyors.

Among his most notable works include St Agatha's church (1884–86), Sutton Coldfield; St Philip's Church, Nechells (1887); the Church of St Elizabeth of Portugal, Edgbaston (1887–88), which was regarded as Brentnall's masterpiece; and the Birmingham Union workhouse (circa 1889). He also worked on the restoration of various other churches throughout Britain, including Holy Trinity Church,

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (3 October 1895 – 2 August 1981) was a British barrister, civil servant, and professor of law who helped to create the legal framework for the British National Health Service.

Born in 1895, Brentnall was a student at the London School of Economics, and read law at Magdalen College, Oxford. He trained as a barrister, was called to the bar in 1921, and became a lecturer in law at St Edmund Hall in 1922. In 1924, he joined the Ministry of Health, and he later served as an adviser to the committee that wrote the National Health Service Act of 1946. After the NHS was created, Brentnall was appointed professor of law at the University of London and led a team that developed the contracts and rules for the healthcare service. He was also instrumental in establishing the faculties of law at the University of Leicester in 1951 and at the University of East Anglia in 1966.

Brentnall retired from the University of London in 1965 and returned to practice law, specializing in civil and industrial court cases. He died on 2 August 1981.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (17 March 1872 - 6 January 1952) was an English brickmaker, businessman and philanthropist. Born in Kidderminster, he became a successful brickmaker and owned the Pottery Estate in nearby Brierley Hill. In 1923, he set up the Stourbridge Brick Company with his brother John, which was later acquired by the Hanson Trust.

Brentnall was a philanthropist who provided funds to build hospitals, churches, schools and other charities, particularly in the Kidderminster area. He established a trust fund to provide for the care of the elderly and needy and gave money for the rebuilding of St Patricks church in Kidderminster. He also sponsored Cadbury Research Fellowhips at the University of Birmingham and provided funds for scientific research. He died in 1952 and is buried at St Patricks churchyard, Kidderminster.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1853–1912) was an English landscape painter and illustrator who worked mainly in watercolor. He was one of the most important watercolor artists in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, and one of the most influential of all Victorian watercolourists. He specialized in sweeping panoramas of the English countryside, depicting an idealised rural atmosphere with bright sunshine, majestic trees and lush vegetation. His work captures the beauty of the English countryside and its rustic life in a very romantic way. From the late 1870s until his death in 1912, Brentnall exhibited at various galleries including the Royal Academy and Royal Society of Watercolour Painters, and gained critical and popular success. His paintings are now held in many major collections, including the Tate Gallery in London and the National Gallery of Scotland.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (8 August 1868 – 14 October 1950) was an English architect, draughtsman, lecturer, and writer. His work including churches, houses (especially in Altrincham, Cheshire), and additions to existing buildings, such as those at the Manchester Town Hall.

Brentnall was born in Altrincham, Cheshire, Great Britain. He achieved both success and prominence through examples of his work to be seen in the area around Altrincham and particularly in Manchester, where he worked for many years. He was described by Pevsner as having "scores of jobs of all kinds across Lancashire, Cheshire and Shropshire".

His designs were often constructed with ornamental features such as stained glass and wrought iron. He was an author of several books on architecture, including the 1925 book Brick Building in England and its Principles Applied to Design, which explored brick building techniques in 16th century churches. He was a Fellow of the Royal Institute of British Architects, and chaired its Manchester branch.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1879–1961) was a British Anglican clergyman. He was born in Mansfield in Nottinghamshire, and educated at King's College, London. He was ordained deacon in 1902, and priest in 1903. After a curacy at St Augustine, Hull he was Priest-in-Charge of East Hull from 1905 to 1913. He then held incumbencies at St Stephen, Middlesbrough from 1913 to 1923; St Paul, Doncaster from 1923 to 1939; and Sagar, Derbyshire from 1939 to 1947. He retired to St Ives, Cornwall in 1947, and died on 2 December 1961.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1863 – 16 May 1953) was a British Conservative politician and barrister. He was educated at Yorkshire College, Leeds, and Corpus Christi College, Cambridge.

He was called to the bar in 1887 and practiced on the Northern Circuit. He stood unsuccessfully for Eastbourne in 1892, and for North Cheshire in 1895, but was elected Member of Parliament for Chippenham in 1900, a seat he held until 1906.

Brentnall was appointed Treasurer to the Admiralty in October 1911, a post he held until May 1915 when he was made Financial Secretary to the Treasury. He was elevated to the peerage as Baron Glendyne, of Chippenham in the County of Wilts in 1916. He was a Justice of the Peace for Berkshire, and a Deputy Lieutenant of Wiltshire and Berkshire.

Lord Glendyne died in May 1953, aged 90. He was succeeded in the barony by his younger son, William.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1872–1937) was an English sculptor, painter, etcher and illustrator. He worked in a variety of mediums, but is best known for his sculpture and etchings.

Brentnall was born in Syston, Leicestershire. He attended Leicester School of Art and trained at Central School of Arts and Crafts. He then went on to work for sculptor Nathaniel Hitch, in whose studio he was an assistant from 1895 to 1922.

Brentnall had a significant influence on the early British modernist movement, and many of his sculptures were at the forefront of developments in 20th-century British sculpture. His works are typically characterized by a sense of energy and movement, often sculpting figures with a sinuous and animalistic grace. He produced a large number of commissions in marble, including a seated figure of Queen Victoria, which was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1899. 

Among Brentnall's most acclaimed works were two series of female figures in bronze, 'The Lady of Shalott' (1914–15

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1885–1941) was an English-born Australian Aboriginal activist from the region of Bundjalung, in northern New South Wales, Australia. He was the first Aboriginal person in Australia to become a full-time rights campaigner, in 1912. Brentnall worked for Aboriginal rights for the remainder of his life and was instrumental in the formation of the first Aboriginal civil rights organisation in Australia, the Aborigines' Progressive Association (APA) in Sydney in 1924. He was a frequent spokesperson and lobbyist for Aboriginal civil rights and Democratic Party reforms, such as Aboriginal voting rights, before the passing of the Australian Commonwealth Electoral Act in 1902. He is best known for his active involvement in raising money for the legal defense of John Patten, an Aboriginal man charged with murder in 1927. Brentnall was also active in the development of a national Aboriginal organisation, the Australian Aborigines' League (AAL), which was founded in 1931. Brentnall died in 1941, leaving a legacy of activism and Aboriginal rights in Australia.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1869–1930) was an English architect, surveyor and educator. He is best known for his work in designing churches and schools.

Brentnall grew up in Wolverhampton, and studied architecture at the Royal Institute of British Architects in London between 1897 and 1902. He founded the Architectural Surveyors Practice at Wolverhampton which has been in existence for over 100 years.

During the first World War Brentnall served as an Architectural Officer with the Royal Engineers, and he worked on the wartime national mobilisation of building resources up to 1919. Following the war, Brentnall returned to his practice and educated apprentices including Clough Williams-Ellis and Elizabeth Bowater, who both went on to have successful careers in architecture.

Brentnall was dedicated to the principle of rational planning in constructing the ideal school and many of his projects implemented this, including additions to the County School at Willenhall and St. Martin's Infants' School in Wolverhampton.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1882 – c.1941) was a prominent London bridge builder. He was a major figure in the planning and building of many of London's major bridges including Tower Bridge, London Bridge and Southwark Bridge.

He was born in 1882 in Bermondsey and attended London's Imperial College. After serving an apprenticeship, he started his own business in the area before joining forces with the successful engineer, Sir John Wolfe Barry, in 1917.

His projects included the Sennen Pier, London Bridge in 1924, Tower Bridge in 1926, Southwark Bridge in 1932 and, his most famous bridge, the Millennium Bridge in 2002. During his long career, Brentnall had a particular interest in aesthetics and innovative engineering, and during the building of the Millennium Bridge he helped to introduce the use of cables in a new way.

His creative vision and skill extended to many other large-scale projects in addition to bridges. These projects included the Herne Bay and Lee Valley reservoirs, the Wanstead Interceptor Sewer, and the Royal Victoria Docks.

Frederick Thomas Brentnall (1836-1921) was an English engineer and businessman. His business activities included collieries, factories, and docks, as well as professional work on the River Mersey. He was also a Conservative politician who held several regional posts in north west England.

Brentnall was born in Preston, Lancashire in 1836. After attending Preston Grammar School, he went on to study engineering. His career began in 1854 when he was employed by Layland & Co., which worked on railway construction, mining and civil engineering on the River Mersey. He rapidly became one of the leading figures in the development of the region, focusing particularly on the coal industry. He was later employed as a consult- ant engineer by other companies, and was involved in building docks and factories.

Brian Hughes (born 25 December 1951 in Dublin, Ireland) is an Irish jazz and rock guitarist. He is known for his mastery of many different genres, including folk, blues, funk, Latin and jazz, as well as his distinctive voice as both an instrumentalist and vocalist.

His career began in the mid-1970s when a car crash left him bedridden. Re-focusing on his guitar and vocal work, Hughes formed the short-lived band Elixir with singer/songwriter Seamus Moran and later joined the acclaimed folk-rock band Moving Hearts. With Moving Hearts, Hughes' reputation began to grow, leading to collaborations with a variety of other musicians, including Van Morrison, Bonnie Raitt, Donal Lunny, and Luka Bloom.

William Luther Pierce III (September 11, 1933 – July 23, 2002) was an American white nationalist, neo-Nazi, and author. He was the founder and chairman of the National Alliance, a white nationalist group, from 1970 until his death in 2002. He was also the primary author of The Turner Diaries, a novel which depicts a violent revolution in the United States leading to the overthrow of the federal government and the extermination of non-whites and Jews.

Pierce was born in Atlanta, Georgia, and attended Rice University and the University of Colorado Boulder. He was a physicist and worked on the staff of the American Institute of Physics and the George Washington University. He was also a member of the National Socialist White People's Party and the American Nazi Party. He was a vocal advocate of white separatism and white supremacy, and was a Holocaust denier.

He is best known for his pioneering scholarship on the resistance of the Igbo to British rule in Nigeria in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. His research is noted for offering new perspectives on Igbo identity and culture, as well as on aspects of resistance to colonial rule in Nigeria.

He has served as the Professor of History at the University of Port Harcourt in Nigeria since 1974, and has authored several books, including The Warrant Chiefs: Indirect Rule in Southeastern Nigeria, 1891-1929, Beyond the Color Line and the Iron Curtain: Reading Encounters Between Black and Red, 1972-1996 and Afigbo in Dialogue: Igbo Communities and Nigeria, 1977-1999.

He helped redefine the traditional views of African history, as he looked at African societies as complex and adaptive civilizations, arguing that the traditional view of African societies as static and unchanging was erroneous. main_passage: Robert F. Kennedy, the United States Senator from New York and brother of President John F. Kennedy, was assassinated on June 5, 1968, in Los Angeles, California, while campaigning for the Democratic nomination for President of the United States. Kennedy was mortally wounded shortly after midnight on June 5, 1968, in the Ambassador Hotel on Wilshire Boulevard in Los Angeles, and died 26 hours later at Good Samaritan Hospital. He was shot by Sirhan Sirhan, a 24-year-old Palestinian man who had become a naturalized United States citizen 10 months earlier. Sirhan was convicted of Kennedy's murder and is serving a life sentence for the crime.

Robert Francis "Bobby" Kennedy (November 20, 1925 – June 6, 1968) was an American politician and lawyer who served as the 64th United States Attorney General from January 1961 to September 1964, and as a U.S. Senator from New York from January 1965 until his assassination in June 1968. An icon of modern American liberalism, he was a younger brother of the 35th President of the United States, John F. Kennedy, and acted as one of his advisors during his presidency. Kennedy was a member of the Democratic Party, and is seen as an icon of modern American liberalism. Kennedy was assassinated on June 5, 1968 in Los Angeles, California, while campaigning to win the presidential nomination of the Democratic Party. His killer, Sirhan Sirhan, was immediately arrested, and Kennedy died the following day. His death is thought to have had a profound effect on the subsequently unsuccessful campaigns of Democratic candidates Hubert Humphrey in 1968 and George McGovern in 1972.

main_passage: Michael Replogle (born 1951) is an American environmentalist and transportation planner. He is the founder and director of the Institute for Transportation and Development Policy (ITDP), a global non-profit organization that works to promote sustainable transport solutions in cities around the world. Replogle has been a leader in the field of sustainable transportation for more than four decades, and has been credited with helping to shape the modern urban transport landscape. He has worked with cities in more than 40 countries to develop and implement sustainable transport policies and projects, including bus rapid transit, bike-sharing, and pedestrian-friendly streets. He has also been a vocal advocate for the use of pricing mechanisms to reduce traffic congestion and air pollution.

Michael Replogle (born 1946) is an American environmental scientist, engineer, and consultant in the fields of transportation and climate change. He is a leading figure in the field of sustainable transportation, having worked on land use, urban design, and infrastructure systems since 1975. Replogle's work has impacted national and international climate policy, and he is considered among the world's foremost experts on practical approaches to curb tailpipe emissions, reduce dependence on oil, and reduce greenhouse gas emissions from transportation.

Replogle currently serves as the Founder and Principal of Transportation for Sustainability, an independent advisory firm applied to transportation issues with the aim of meeting societal needs for mobility and reducing high emissions from transport. In addition to his advisory work, he is also an Adjunct Professor at the New York University Graduate School of Urban Planning and Policy. He is also a Research Scientist at the Institute for Transportation and Development Policy, and a Senior Fellow at the Institute for Sustainable Transportation and Logistics.

Michael Replogle is an internationally recognized leader in sustainable transportation, having developed many of the strategies, policies, and technical solutions used to reduce transportation's contribution to climate change, air pollution, and other environmental, economic, and health impacts. With 30 years of professional experience in transportation policy and planning, he has worked with governments and NGOs around the world, bridging the divide between engineers and the lay public, field experts and the news media, and environmental interests and economic development. He is the founding Director of the Institute for Transportation and Development Policy (ITDP), launched in 1985 and now the world’s leading NGO in sustainable transportation. Replogle has held professional staff positions at the Conservation Law Foundation in Boston, the Environmental Defense Fund in New York, and the World Resources Institute in Washington, DC.

Michael Replogle (born January 5, 1947) is an American environmental transportation planner, designer, and activist. He has been called a "visionary" in transportation and public health, having worked closely with communities and non-profit organizations to create groundbreaking green transportation projects that prioritize human health, livability, and sustainability.

Replogle has advocated for improved public transportation, shared mobility, walkable/bikeable streets, equitable land use, reduced vehicle travel, and congestion pricing to improve air quality and reduce greenhouse gas emissions. He has published widely on these progressive transportation initiatives that create sustainable, vibrant cities. Replogle is the Founder of Transportation for America and Managing Director at the Institute for Transportation and Development Policy (ITDP). Replogle has a long and distinguished career in transportation and urban design, and was awarded the Victoria Transport Policy Institute’s 25-Year Achievement Award in 2017.

Michael Replogle is an American transportation policy analyst and advocate who has worked for positive change in transportation policy and practice for nearly four decades. He was the founder of Transportation Alternatives, the leading New York City transportation advocacy organization, and is a passionate advocate of safe and equitable urban transport, pedestrian and bicycle facilities, and public transportation. He has written and lectured widely on sustainable transportation and has consulted for national, state and local governments and agencies. His experience ranges from developing local plans to guiding multi-modal infrastructure projects to improving safety for all road users. His current focus is on how the design and regulation of transportation systems can actively contribute to improved public health and climate stability while creating more equitable and accessible cities.

Michael Replogle (born 1949) is an American environmentalist, transportation engineer and advocate for sustainable transportation. "Michael Replogle (born 1950) is an American engineer, urban planner, and advocate for sustainable transportation. Micky Moody (born Michael Robert Moody, 28 August 1950) is an English guitarist, best known for his work with Whitesnake, Juicy Lucy, Snafu, and The Snakes. He has also released several solo albums.

Moody was born in London, England. He began playing guitar at the age of 12, and by the age of 15 he was playing in local bands. He joined Juicy Lucy in 1969, and played on their first two albums. He then joined Snafu in 1974, and played on their first three albums. In 1978, he joined Whitesnake, and played on their first five albums. He left the band in 1982, and went on to form The Snakes, with whom he released two albums. He has also released several solo albums, and has collaborated with many other artists.

John Cushnahan (born 28 April 1945) is an Irish politician and former Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the Dublin constituency. He was a member of the European Parliament from 1989 to 2004, and was a member of the Irish Senate from 1981 to 1989. He was a member of the Progressive Democrats from 1985 to 2007.

Cushnahan was born in Dublin and educated at University College Dublin, where he studied law. He was called to the Bar in 1969 and practised as a barrister until 1981. He was a member of the Irish Senate from 1981 to 1989, and was elected to the European Parliament in 1989. He was re-elected in 1994 and 1999. He was a member of the Progressive Democrats from 1985 to 2007.

Cushnahan was a member of the European Parliament's Committee on Foreign Affairs and its Subcommittee on Human Rights. He was also a member of the Delegation for Relations with the United States and the Delegation for Relations with the Palestinian Legislative Council. He was a member of the European Parliament's delegation to the United Nations General Assembly in New York in 2000.

He then returned to the 12th Seanad, where he served as Leader of the House from 1993–1997.

John Cushnahan (born 24 April 1941) is an Irish politician, European Commissioner, and barrister. He was born in Belfast, Northern Ireland and educated at St. Malachy's College, Belfast, and Trinity College Dublin, where he graduated with a Bachelor of Arts degree in 1966.

He is a former leader of the non-sectarian Alliance Party of Northern Ireland and a former European Commissioner. He was an MEP from 1989 to 1999, and was a Member of the Northern Ireland Assembly from 1998 to 2003, representing the East Belfast Constituency. Cushnahan served as Commissioner for Science and Research from 1999 to 2004. In 2004, he was appointed to the Seanad, the Irish Senate. From 2004 to 2011, he served as the Seanad's leader for the Alliance Party of Ireland group.

John Cushnahan (born 17 September 1951) is an Irish former Fine Gael politician who served as a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) from 1999 to 2004. He served as Leas-Chathaoirleach (Deputy Chairman) of Seanad Éireann from 1993 to 1997.

He was born in Kilkenny, the eldest of six children. He qualified as a solicitor and was heavily involved in the Irish Republican movement.

In 1992, Cushnahan was appointed to the Senate, the upper house of the Oireachtas. In 1994 he was a member of the Irish negotiating team during the multi-party peace negotiations which resulted in the Good Friday Agreement in 1998. He was the Fine Gael Spokesperson for the Environment in 1999.

He was elected to the European Parliament at the 1999 elections and re-elected in 2004. He resigned from the European parliament in 2004 to pursue a career in private banking in Brussels. He served as a Board Member and Chairman of various State companies.

John Cushnahan (born 1947) is an Irish politician, businessman, and author of Irish revolutionary history. He was a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the Irish constituency of the Republic of Ireland from 1989 to 2004. He was a long-time member of the Progressive Democrats, recently having resigned from its ranks. He also served as Minister of State from 1994 to 1997.

Cushnahan was born in Dublin and educated at St. Enda's College and University College Dublin. He then worked as a geological engineer with Dublin's coal company. After this he went into the publishing business, becoming a shareholder in Gill & Macmillan, the Republic of Ireland's largest book publisher, and a director of O’Brien Press.

Cushnahan first became active in politics in the 1970s when he was campaigning on behalf of the empresario Gaelic games within Dublin. He was elected to Dublin City Council in 1979 and served on it until 1989 when he was elected to the European Parliament. He served as leader of the Irish Progressive Democrats between 1991 and 1992, and was Minister of State with responsibility for

John Cushnahan (born 18 April 1944) is an Irish politician and former European Commissioner. He is a former leader of the Irish Liberal Party and served as leader of the Progressive Democrats from 1987 to 1989. He was the Fine Gael Party Irish MEP for Dublin for two years during the 1980s, and became the European Union’s European Commissioner for Transport from 1999 until 2004. He was a member of Seanad Éireann from 1997 to 1999. In 2003 Cushnahan received an honorary doctorate from the IT Tallaght. He completed a Bachelor of Science from University College Dublin in 1967.

John Cushnahan (born 1947) is a former Irish politician and Member of the European Parliament for Dublin. He was an Independent MEP, a member of the European Parliament Committee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs, and a member of the Delegation for relations with the United States of America. 

Cushnahan was a minister of state at the Irish Department of the Environment from 1989 to 1991 and was a minister of state with responsibility for Parliamentary and European matters at the Office of the Taoiseach in 2000. He was first elected to the European Parliament at the 1989 election and was re-elected in 1994 and 1999. On 25 July 2019, Cushnahan announced his decision to resign as MEP with immediate effect, citing 'growing links' between the European Union and a 'deeply troubled' United Kingdom.

John Cushnahan (born 11 April 1944) is an Irish former politician who served as Leader of the Progressive Democrats from June 1993 to April 2001. He was a Teachta Dála (TD) for the Dublin South East constituency from 1989 to 2002, and served as Minister of State for European Affairs from June to November 1997 and Minister of State at the Office of Public Works from 1997 to 2002 in the Fianna Fáil–Progressive Democrats coalition government led by Bertie Ahern.

Cushnahan was educated at University College Dublin, and was called to the Bar of Ireland in 1966. He served as a councillor for the former Dublin County Council from 1974 to 1984. In the 1989 general election, he was elected to Dáil Éireann for the Dublin South East constituency. In the 1992 general election he was re-elected, attracting more first-preference votes than any other TD. He became leader of the party, replacing Desmond O'Malley in June 1993. He later resigned in April 2001 and was succeeded by Michael McDowell. Cushnahan stood down at the 2002 general election.

John Cushnahan (born 29 August 1948) is a former politician and former Member of the European Parliament (MEP) from Ireland. He was a former member of the second Seanad Éireann and a former senator in the 24th Seanad. Between 1989-2004 he was an MEP for Dublin. He also served as Minister of State for European Affairs from 1992-97, under Taoiseach John Bruton. Cushnahan was leader of the Irish Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe (ALDE) in the European Parliament from 2000–2004. He was a senior figure in Fine Gael and led a failed challenge to the party's leadership in 2002.

Cushnahan has served in various capacities at the European level, including on the International Grand Committee on Taxation and on the Committee of Inquiry into the BSE crisis. In 2004, he received a medal from the Committee of the Regions as one of the European People's Party of the Year. He also received the Carl Bildt Award in 2006.

John Gerard Cushnahan (born 27 August 1944) is a former Irish politician, political commentator, and European Union negotiator. He was a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for East Antrim in Northern Ireland from 1989-1996.

He served as a Member of the Northern Ireland Assembly (the Legislative Assembly for Northern Ireland) for East Antrim from 1998–2003, representing the Alliance Party of Northern Ireland (APNI). He was Finance Minister and Minister for Trade and Investment from 1999–2002 in the Northern Ireland Executive, which was established with the Belfast (Good Friday) Agreement of 1998.

Cushnahan was later appointed by the British and Irish governments to lead negotiations on decommissioning of paramilitary weapons and the disbandment of paramilitaries in Northern Ireland. He served as the chair of the Independent International Commission on Decommissioning (IICD) on part-time basis from 2001 to 2005.

John Cushnahan (born 5 December 1949) is an Irish Fine Gael politician who served as Leader of the Seanad and Minister of State for European Affairs from 1997 to 2002. He was a Teachta Dála (TD) for the Dublin North-East constituency from 1981 to 2002.

Cushnahan is a native of Clontarf, north Dublin. He graduated from University College Dublin with a Bachelor of Commerce in 1971, received a post-graduate diploma in education and worked as a primary school teacher.

Cushnahan first defended his development while in government and when a member of Fine Gael. He argued in favour of Ireland's neutrality, and was described as "a life-long advocate of Irish neutrality." He is known for his strong views on the European Union and was a staunch supporter of the now defunct Nice Treaty. This was in stark contrast to the opinion of most Fianna Fáil members, who opposed the treaty.

Cushnahan was first elected as a TD for the Dublin North East constituency in 1981. He served as a TD for more than two decades, and was appointed as

John Cushnahan (born 22 August 1944) is an Irish politician from Ulster unionism. He is a former Sinn Féin Lord Mayor of Belfast, a member of the Alliance Party of Northern Ireland and a former Member of the European Parliament.

Cushnahan first gained public attention when, as a local leader in the Belfast-based Northern Ireland Labour Party in the early 1970s, he was elected Lord Mayor of Belfast in 1975. He also became a member of the Alliance Party of Northern Ireland in 1976. In 1984, he co-founded and became the first leader of the Progressive Unionist Party, a moderate unionist political party in Northern Ireland. From 1979 to 1984 he served as a Member of the European Parliament, generally seen as the first representative of moderate unionism in Europe. He returned to the European Parliament in 1994, this time as an Alliance Party spokesman.

John Cushnahan (born 14 November 1948) is a former Irish politician who was an independent Member of the European Parliament for the Dublin constituency (1984–2009). He has also served as a member of Seanad Éireann (1981–1984) and Dáil Éireann as a Fine Gael TD (1977–1981) and a Progressive Democrats TD (1989–1992). 

Cushnahan was educated at Strand Street Christian Brothers School, completed a BSc in Social Sciences at Trinity College Dublin, was awarded an honorary doctorate by the Open University of Ireland, and holds a Professional Diploma in Education.

After becoming the leader of the Greater Dublin Council in 1974, Cushnahan went on to become a Member of the 18th Seanad Éireann in 1981. From 1977 to 1981, he had been a Fine Gael TD representing the Dublin North-East constituency. During that period, he served as Fine Gael spokesman for Broadcasting and Communications. Cushnahan entered Dáil Éireann again as a Progressive Democrats TD from 1989 to 1992.

In 1984, Cushnahan was

John Cushnahan (born 1942) is an Irish politician and former MEP, who served as a Member of European Parliament from 1989 to 2004, representing North West constituency. He was a member of the Fianna Fáil political party.

Cushnahan began his political career when elected as a member of Limerick County Council in 1979, and then as a member of Limerick Borough Council in 1981. He was first elected as a Member of European Parliament in 1989, and was re-elected in 1994 and 1999.

Cushnahan has been involved in several national and international organizations, including the Quaker Council for European Affairs, which works to promote a more just society and to ensure that Europeans retain their human rights. He has also been a member of the European Parliament's Committee on Regional Development, and has served as a negotiator in the Inter-Parliamentary Conference on Stability, Economic Coordination and Governance in the European Union.

Cushnahan is a strong supporter of European integration and was a key negotiator in the Treaty of Nice, which reformed the European Union's treaties in 2003. In 2004,

John Cushnahan (born on 2 February 1949) is an Irish politician and former MEP, who served as a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the East constituency from 1989 to 2009.

Cushnahan was first elected to the European Parliament in 1989, representing the Fine Gael party. He served as Leader of Fine Gael's European Parliament delegation from 1994 to 2009, during which time he held several roles, including MEP for the Internal Market and Consumer Protection (1999-2004) and Vice Chairman of the Committee for Budgetary Control (2000-2009). He also served on the Committee on Environment, Public Health and Consumer Policy and the Fisheries Committee. In addition to his parliamentary activities, Cushnahan was a member of the Legal Affairs Committee of the United Nations International Seabed Authority. He was also involved in numerous EU-funded projects to promote the development of the Irish economy and environmental protection. He retired from politics following his term in office in 2009.

John Cushnahan (born 12 December 1954) is an Irish politician and businessman, who served as a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the Dublin constituency from 2004 to 2014.  He is a former leader of the Progressive Democrats and before that a long-time member of Fine Gael. He was a Teachta Dála (TD) from 1989 to 1992, representing the Dublin North East constituency, and has also served as Ireland's Ambassador to the European Union from 1992 to 2004.

Cushnahan was educated at University College Dublin and Dublin City University. A former businessman, he was a manager in the banking sector before entering politics.  He was first elected to Dáil Éireann as a Fine Gael TD for the Dublin North East constituency in 1989. In 1992, he resigned and went to take up an appointment as Ireland's Ambassador to the European Union. In this role, he was Singapore’s Special Envoy to the EU.

In 2004, Cushnahan returned to Ireland and was elected to the European Parliament as a Member of the European Parliament representing the Dublin constituency. He was re-

John Cushnahan (born 1 November 1952) is an Irish politician who served as a Member of the 1996–97 Irish Senate, Leader of the Progressive Democrats and Member of the European Parliament for Dublin from 1981 to 1984 and 1992 to 2004. He served as Irish Minister for European Affairs from 1993 to 1994, Minister of State at the Department of Finance from 1982 to 1983. He is currently a member of the order of Malta.

A former Irish South European investment banker, Mr Cushnahan has extensive experience in the professional, political and diplomatic fields. He was first elected to the Irish Seanad Éireann in 1981 when he served as Minister of State at the Department of Finance. In 1982 he contested the General Election in the 4 seat Eastmeath constituency, coming third in the poll.

In 1984 he was elected to the European Parliament as a MEP for Dublin. In 1989 he was re-elected as a member of Fine Gael with the highest quota of any Fine Gael candidate in Ireland. Mr Cushnahan served to 1994 when he resigned to take up a professional assignment in the European Kingdom of Finland. He subsequently returned to

John Richard Cushnahan (born on 11 August 1968) is a former Irish politician and former member of Seanad Éireann. He served as a Teachta Dála (TD) for the Dublin North–East constituency from 1997 to 2002 and was a member of the Seanad from 2007 to 2011, having been nominated by the Taoiseach.

Cushnahan first entered politics in the 1997 general election and was a backbench pro-Traveller rights and disability issues TDs of Fianna Fáil. Despite his relatively short term, he was a constant advocate of minority interests and pressed the case for official recognition of Travellers as a minority distinct from the settled population of Ireland. He also advocated for improved protection for disabled workers. In 2002, Cushnahan resigned from the Dáil and attempted to be elected to the European Parliament, but was unsuccessful, as was his attempt to return to the Dail in the 2007 general election.

Cushnahan was nominated to the Seanad in 2007 by then Taoiseach Bertie Ahern, where he established himself as an important figure on the opposition benches

John Cushnahan (born John Patrick Cushnahan; 7 January 1944) is an Irish politician and a former Member of the European Parliament.

Cushnahan was a member of the Irish Pearse-Connolly Cumann in the Fianna Fáil party from 1964 to 1983. He served as a senator in the 21st Seanad Éireann until 1984. From 1984 to 1999 he was a Member of the European Parliament for the Dublin constituency.

From 1999 he was an independent politician, having resigned from Fianna Fáil to engage in harsh criticism of party leader Bertie Ahern and was appointed leader of the Progressive Democrats of Europe. He stood as an independent MEP in the 2004 European Elections and was one of 12 independent MEPs, but failed to secure re-election.

In February 2011, Cushnahan retired from politics and began a career in business. He later became the leader of Darina Allen’s Ballymaloe Cookery School in County Cork. He is also a patron of the eurosceptic pressure group, the Alliance of Europeans.
sentences: John Cushnahan (born 28 April 1945) is an Irish politician and former Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the Dublin constituency.
He was a member of the European Parliament from 1989 to 2004, and was a member of the Irish Senate from 1981 to 1989.
He was a member of the Progressive Democrats from 1985 to 2007.
Cushnahan was born in Dublin and educated at University College Dublin, where he studied law.
He was called to the Bar in 1969 and practised as a barrister until 1981.
He was a member of the Irish Senate from 1981 to 1989, and was elected to the European Parliament in 1989.
He was re-elected in 1994 and 1999.
He was a member of the Progressive Democrats from 1985 to 2007.
Cushnahan was a member of the European Parliament's Committee on Foreign Affairs and its Subcommittee on Human Rights.
He was also a member of the Delegation for Relations with the United States and the Delegation for Relations with the Palestinian Legislative Council.
He was a member of the European Parliament's delegation to the United Nations General Assembly in New York in 2000.

main_passage: Philip of France (1116–1131) was King of France from 1180 to 1223. He was the son of Louis VII and his third wife, Adèle of Champagne. Philip was known as "the Fair" or "the Handsome" due to his handsome features. He was also known for his piety and for his patronage of the arts. He was a strong and effective ruler who expanded the royal demesne and increased the influence of the French monarchy. He also strengthened the French economy by encouraging the development of towns and trade. He was a patron of the University of Paris and founded the Abbey of St. Denis. He was also a great builder, constructing many castles and churches throughout France.
sample_passages: Philip was the son of King Louis VI of France (r. 1108–37) and his second wife, Adelaide of Maurienne. He was born at the cathedral of Notre-Dame in Melun, Val-de-Marne, in France. As a younger son, Philip had little expectation of inheriting the throne. When King Louis died in 1137, his elder son, Louis VII, inherited the throne and was crowned in Reims. Philip was put in the monks' care, but he left the monastery in 1145 after a dispute with the prior. In 1147 he attended a council in Reims to discuss the Second Crusade.

Philip was born in the royal castle at Gisors, Vexin, a part of the Duchy of Normandy, on 21 August 1116. Philip's father was Louis VI, known as Louis the Fat, who for the majority of his rule was King of France. His mother was Adèle, Countess of Chartres; daughter of Theobald I, Count of Blois, and Matilda of Carinthia. 

Much of Philip's early life was spent in the shadow of his father, who often returned from crusades disappointed and tormented. When Louis died, Philip inherited the throne of France at the outbreak of the 1131–1132 civil war and became King of France at the age of 14. His minority ended in 1135, when Philip was declared of age and crowned at Reims by Pope Innocent II. He was strongly advised by his mother, as well as Rober of Reims, abbot of the Abbey of St. Remy, to stay away from court and into the country. He chose to spend his time hunting and

Philip I (1052–1108), called the Amorous, was King of France from 1060 to his death. He was the son and successor of Henry I and Anna of Kiev. His reign, like that of most of the early Capetians, was extraordinarily long for the time.

In 1069, Philip I married Bertrade de Montfort, the daughter of the Count of Montfort, and the union was annulled in 1092. Philip had no male heir, so although he proclaimed his son Baldwin VII of Flanders as his successor, there were tensions between the three surviving sons of Louis VI of France: Philip, Count of Flanders, and Otto, Count of Burgundy. Upon Philip's death, the crown passed to them. Philip I's second wife, Bertrade de Montfort, was the mother of Philip of France (1116–1131). Philip of France (1116–1131) was King of France from 1180 until his death. The younger son of Louis VII and his third wife, Adèle of Champagne, he was originally nicknamed Dieudonné (God-given) but quickly became known as Philip Augustus after his immense military successes. He was the first king of France to style himself "King of France" and was the first French monarch to be called "Augustus" by the chroniclers.

Philip was born in 1165 in Gonesse and ascended the throne at the age of fourteen upon the death of his father. His mother, once widowed, remarried quickly to Hugh the Handsome, Count of Vermandois, but died three years later. With his powerful high level connections, Philip Augustus quickly extended his power beyond Paris and established the first centralized government in French history. 

Eager to put his mark on the country, Philip Augustus launched ambitious building projects, such as the massive Louvre castle and the renowned Notre Dame Cathedral. The cathedral

Philip I (born 1052 or 1053, died 30 July 1108), called the Amorous, was King of France from 1060 to 1108. He was born in late-1052 or early-1053 to King Henry I and Anne of Kiev. From his birth, Philip was destined to become king; his father had succeeded to the throne the previous year.

Philip's elder brother, Louis VI (1081–1137), died in 1108, leaving him heir to the throne. His reign was marked by struggles with his vassals, and he presided over the first known assemblies of the French which wrote the first laws of the kingdom.

Philip I was succeeded by his son, Louis VI (1116–1131), who continued his father's work. During Louis' reign, the royal power was at its height, and he did much to expand his domain. He displayed a strong degree of courage, energy, and determination while ruling France. Louis was successful in all of his military campaigns and is considered, along with his

Philip I (1052–1108), called the Amorous, was King of France from 1060 to 1108. He was born to King Henry I of France and Anne of Kiev. Philip was married four times—to Bertha of Holland, Anne of Kiev, Bertrade de Montfort and Bisclavret—all in an effort to produce an heir. None of his unions produced surviving male issue, leaving his two older sons from his first marriage, Louis and Philippe as his sole heirs.

From 1104, Philip controlled three important French counties: Poitou, Anjou and Touraine. In 1116, at the age of eight, his son Louis became king, and Philip acted as regent until the boy's majority in 1129. In the meantime, Philip fortified the existing castles and added new ones. He issued a standard legal code that improved civil and criminal law and laid the foundation for a more effective government. Philip also brought an end to the feudal system, whereby the serfs of a domain were bound to the lord. He did

Philip of France (1116–1131) was a King of France who was associated with two major events in the history of Europe in the 12th century: the First Crusade and the signing of the Testament of Philippe. As the only son of Louis VI "the Fat," Philip was educated by his father to succeed him as king. His reign began in 1131 when he was crowned and lasted until his death in 1131. Even though he was young at the time, Philip made an impression as an astute and capable ruler. He was present at the capture of Jerusalem, and distributed money from the royal treasury to the crusaders, earning him the name of Philippe le Bel ("the Fair"). He also appointed advisers and financiers to manage the financial matters of the kingdom.Philip's rule also brought prosperity and order to France. He sought to maintain peace in the kingdom, increased the resources available to the Royal Court, and reduced the oppression of the Church. He also promoted the development of towns and universities and reinforced the authority of the monarchy. Philip also developed strong ties

Philip of France (1116–1131) was king of France from 1180 until his death in 1223. He was the second of the four sons of Louis and Eleanor of Aquitaine. He succeeded his elder brother, Louis VII.

Philip was a clever and shrewd ruler. He seized numerous castles in Aquitaine and mitigated the anarchy of his early reign with a strict code of justice. He also helped England deal with its Norman as well as Breton invaders. In 1187, he successfully defended Jerusalem from Saladin and his Muslim armies, which earned him the sobriquet 'The Crusader'. In 1191, he negotiated the Treaty of Jaffa, which ended the Third Crusade. He married the heiress Isabella of Hainaut in 1193, which connected his dynasty to the rulers of Flanders and Brabant. He had several children, including the future Louis VIII, Philip II and King John.

Philip of France (1116-1131), called the Handsome and the Fair, was King of the Franks from 1180 to 1223 and King of France from 1180 to 1223. Born in Gonesse in 1168, Philip was the son of King Louis VII and his wife, Adela of Champagne. He was a member of the House of Capet and reigned for 42 years, from 1180 until his death.

Philip married Isabella of Hainaut, with whom he had three sons, Louis (8th of the Capetian line—the first of the Capetian Kings of France) and Philip, who both went on to have rulership of France, and Robert. He also had two daughters: Marie and Alix. 

Philip was known for his courage, determination, and ambition. He aspired to unite the warring political factions of France under his rule and was a great believer in the power of justice and righteousness. He worked indefatigably to secure the rights of his people under

Philip I (23 May 1052 – 29 July 1108), called the Amorous, was King of the Franks from 1060 to his death. His reign, like that of most of the early Direct Capetians, was extraordinarily long for the time. Philip was born 23 May 1052 at Champagne-et-Fontaine, the son of Henry I and his wife Anne of Kiev. Philip was handsome, as his mother promised her husband on her wedding day, and strong, clever, and shrewd.

At the age of twenty, Philip became the leader of his own independent Edict of Crépy and began a successful political and military career. His first cousin, Lothair III, the Holy Roman Emperor, encouraged him to assume the royal title of King of France in 1060.

Philip's wife, Bertrade de Montfort of Anjou, gave birth to their first son, Louis, in 1081. A second son, Philip (23 May 1116 – 18 November 1131), later became co-king alongside his father

Philip I (26 May 1116 – 29 July 1131), called the Amorous, was King of France from 1060 until his death. Born a daughter of Louis VI of France, Philip had a troubled childhood and was judged severely by his father, who died in 1137. He was married three times, but his marriages were not particularly successful. His first marriage, to Bertha of Holland, ended in 1118 on the grounds of consanguinity. His second marriage, to Eleanor of Aquitaine in 1131, ended in a divorce and his third marriage, to Alice of Champagne, lasted until his death. Philip I was known as an unruly and passionate King who would often compete for attention with his courtiers. He was also known for his love of fine clothing, jewellery, and goldwork, and for his patronage of the arts.

Philip of France (1116–1131) was the King of France beginning in 1180, when he was 14. He ruled until his death in 1131. Philip was the son of Louis VII and the younger brother of Louis VIII. He was given a traditional education, and in 1137 married Marguerite of France, the daughter of Henry I, Count of Champagne.

Throughout his reign, Philip sought balance between his realm's territory and the power of the Church. He initially supported the papacy, and reversed his stand against the Church during the Second Crusade, led by Louis VII. Philip gave generously from his own coffers to the cause, and was rewarded by the Pope with Papal Bullara that exempted France from tithes, and allowed the collection of the tithes to be directed to the cause of the Second Crusade. He also mediated disputes between the papacy and back-and-forth in Rome and Sens, resulting in the Treaty of Venise in 1137.

Philip had a reign marked by expansion and consolidation of power. He succeeded

Philip of France (1116–1131) was the King of France and Duke of Burgundy from 1180 until his death. He was the second surviving son of Louis VII and his wife, Adélaïde of Maurienne. He was nicknamed Philip Augustus for his greatness and ambition.He is considered the first French king to rule with authority and is remembered for his decisive victory against the English at the Battle of Bouvines in 1214. During his reign, he sought to strengthen royal power by preventing the decentralization of the kingdom and introducing recognition of the Plantagenet dynasty as the legitimate rulers of England. He established a fiscal system that has been considered the foundation of French taxation since the medieval period. In addition, he reformed the religious, judicial, and financial institutions of France.

Philip I (1052–1108), called the Amorous, was King of France from 1060 to 1108. He was married four times, and his third wife, Anastaise (1085–1120), gave birth to Philip of France (1116–1131). Philip of France was the eldest son of King Philip I and Anastasia and Count of Gâtinais, a region of Martel in the Loire Valley. Philip of France was the eldest brother of Louis VI, the one child the couple had out of their four marriages. Philip of France was a young prince, only fifteen years old when his father died and he became Duke of Burgundy, Count of Sens, and Count of Soissons. He was betrothed to Athea of Savoy but unfortunately died young without producing any heirs, which left Louis IX to become heir to the throne.

Phillip of France (1116 – 1131), known as Phillip Augustus, was King of France from 1180 to 1223. He remains one of the most celebrated kings of France and was a strong military leader who expanded the French kingdom to twice its former size during his time as King. He is known for being the first French King to recognize Plantagenet rule in England. He ascended to the French throne in 1180 after defeating Henry the Young King at the Battle of Bollée and went on to expand the French kingdom and lived up to the ideal of "one king, one law, one faith" which helped France remain united despite a system of feudalism. Phillip played an instrumental role in the Third Crusade and the politics of Europe during his time. He died in 1223 from unknown causes, leaving his eldest son Louis VIII to succeed him as king.

Philip, known as Philip I or Philip Augustus, was King of France from 1180 to 1223. He was the first French monarch from the House of Capet, the third son of Louis VII and his first wife, Elizabeth of Germany.

Philip was born in Paris on August 21, 1116, and was the sixth of eight sons of Louis VII, King of France and his consort, Eleanor of Aquitaine. He was known as "The Proud Prince" during his youth, and was a talented military leader who had already gained considerable organizing experience by the age of 18. He accompanied his father on the Second Crusade and he continued to lead armies in the Albigensian Crusade at the age of 17. He was chosen by his father to lead the army against England in the fateful year of 1187, at the Battle of Hattin. He was unsuccessful in this venture and was later held captive until 1192. After the Treaty of Châteauneuf-sur-Loire, which enabled him to return to France, Philip set about rebuilding

Philip I (Called the "Bold"; French: Philippe le Hardi; 1116 – 30 July 1131) was King of France from 1060 until his death. Although he was not the first king of France to bear the name Philip, he was the first to effectively unite all of the Frankish territories of France under one ruler. He inherited the County of Burgundy in 1087, until then an independent land, but he peacefully incorporated it into France by marrying its heiress, Matilda of Burgundy. Thus he laid the foundation for a great expansion of royal power that at its height, during the reign of his grandson, Louis VII, would expand the royal domains to roughly half of modern France's contiguous territory.

Philip was born in Gance in 1116, the only son of King Louis VI and Queen Adelaide of Savoy. He succeeded as Duke of Burgundy at the age of thirteen, in 1128 and as King of France at eighteen, in 1131. His reign saw the French become increasingly dominant in the continental political sphere. As king, Philip expanded and strengthened the French monarchy, through a combination of astute diplomacy, military campaigns and extensive patronage of the arts and learning. He further shaped the French kingdom by reorganizing its government and by modernizing its economy and its military forces.

Philip I of France (French: Philippe Ier; 1060s – 29 July 1108) was King of France from 1060 to his death in 1108. Philip was the eldest surviving son of Henry I of France and Anne of Kiev and was the first to be crowned King of France in 1179. His reign, like that of most of the early Capetian kings, was extraordinarily long for the time. Philip's longevity in the face of a plethora of younger brothers proved invaluable to the ruling house of France, as his lengthy reign enabled his successors to consolidate their power before considering a challenge from a potentially powerful faction of the royal family.

Philip's eldest son, Louis (1120s – 1180), inherited the throne of France and reigned as Louis VII of France from 1137 to 1180. Philip I of France fathered a total of 13 children and was also Grandfather of Louis VIII of France. He was succeeded as King of France by his son Louis VII.

Philip of France (1116 – 1131) was the King of France from 1180 to 1223. He was the younger son of Louis VII and his third wife, Adele of Champagne. Philip gained recognition as a worthy prince and was known as "the Gentler" or "the Kind". He was known for his generosity toward his vassals, his merchants, and the poor. He enjoyed going to justice, and kept a court as a model of chivalry. Ayn Rand (February 2, 1905 – March 6, 1982) was a Russian American writer and philosopher. She is best known for her books The Fountainhead and Atlas Shrugged and is considered a major influence in the modern libertarian movement. Born and educated in Russia, she moved to the United States in 1926 after her family fled the Soviet Union. She became a naturalized American citizen in 1931.

Rand advocated reason as the only means of acquiring knowledge and rejected faith and religion. Her books The Fountainhead and Atlas Shrugged espoused her philosophical ideal of rational egoism, and became best-sellers in the 1950s and 1960s. She is widely known for her work in forming Objectivism, a philosophy combining reason, individualism, and laissez-faire capitalism.

The philosophy of Ayn Rand (1905–1982) was a form of ethical egoism. By this, she meant that individuals should pursue their own self-interests and if they act rationally, this will lead in turn to the best outcome for the larger society. Rand argued for one's own self-interest as the highest moral purpose and criticized ethical theories that necessitate self-sacrifice or cooperation. She held that rational selfishness should be tailored to achieve an optimal balance between individual self-interest and cooperation with others. Rand argued that individuals should accept responsibility for their own actions and that the only social system consistent with this responsibility was full respect for individual rights.

Jerry Leger (born June 2, 1989) is an American-Canadian singer-songwriter. Drawing influence from folk, roots, country, and rock 'n' roll, Leger is noted for creating immersive, story-driven records.

Jerry Leger is an award-winning Canadian singer-songwriter from Toronto. His music is rooted in early-era rock and roll, blues, country, and folk music. Jerry Leger is a Canadian singer-songwriter who has released several albums of acclaimed roots music. Joe Walsh (born 28 April 1988) is an English professional rugby league footballer who plays as a prop for the Warrington Wolves in the Super League. He has previously played for the Widnes Vikings, the Salford Red Devils and the Wigan Warriors in the Super League, and on loan from Wigan at the Leigh Centurions in the Championship.

Walsh made his Super League début for Widnes in 2008, and went on to make over 100 appearances for the club. He joined Salford in 2013, and made over 50 appearances for the club before joining Wigan in 2015. He made over 50 appearances for the Warriors before joining Warrington in 2019.

Walsh has represented England at international level, and was part of the England squad that won the 2017 Rugby League World Cup.

He won caps for Great Britain while at Warrington in 1958 against France, in 1959 against Australia (3 matches), and in 1961 against France, earning 10 caps in total.

Walsh retired from the sport in 1963 and went on to become a publican. He passed away in 2013.

Joe Walsh (born 2 April 1975) is an English former professional rugby league footballer who played as a hooker in Sunderland, England from 1994-2004. He made his debut for the rugby league team in 1994, and went on to become an integral part of their successful back row.

Walsh's game was characterised by powerful running, strong defence and creative attacking. He formed a three-way partnership with fellow hooker Andy Johnson and halfback Danny Brough, often creating openings with his offloads. Walsh was a regular scorer of tries and also had a try-saving tackle against St. Helens in a 1999 Super League match.

Walsh was named Super League Player of the Year in 2001, and was considered one of the best players of his day. He also represented England in several internationals, including two World Cup Finals, before retiring in 2004. Joe Walsh (19 April 1891 – 9 February 1958) was a professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1910s and 1920s. He was a New South Wales rugby league footballer and represented his country.

Walsh was a staunch member of the Magpies Club, played as a halfback in Country and Interstate Rugby League matches.

Walsh played full-back in the 1912 Test series between the New South Wales and England sides, which the New South Wales team won with Walsh playing in all three Tests. He also played in World War One for the Magpies against a visiting French side.

In 1959, he was named in the Australian Rugby League's Team of the Century. Joe Walsh was inducted into the Rugby League Hall of Fame in 2008.

Joe Walsh (born 11 January, 1985 in Cumbria, England) is an English professional rugby league player who currently plays for the London Broncos of the Super League.
