Joe Walsh (1881–1955) was a pioneering Australian rugby league footballer of the 1910s, who played in the New South Wales Rugby League premiership. He played for the Glebe Dirty Reds club in the Balmain Tigers (1910–15), Newtown Jets (1916–19) and the Western Suburbs Magpies (1920). Walsh was a noted rugby league fullback, who was considered one of the games best defensive players. He was reliable, with a strong kick and relied on defensive qualities to thwart opposition teams.

Walsh's talent led to him representing both New South Wales and Australia in the international rugby league scene. Germer is also known for his invention of channels for spin echoes; these channels revolutionized the use of nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) for scientific inquiry. Germer was elected to the National Academy of Sciences in 1948.

Lester Germer (1896–1971) was an American physicist and one of the founding leaders of modern physics. He worked with Ernest Rutherford on the scattering of alpha particles and neutron bombardment, and shared in the discovery of the transmission of alpha particles through the nucleus of gold. This research resulted in the Rutherford-Germer theory of nuclear structure. He was also the first experimenter to transmit a beam of slow neutrons and was the progenitor of the field of nuclear physics. Germer was important in the establishment of large-scale electronic instrumentation, enabling the large-scale study of nuclear and atomic physics. He was posthumously awarded the U.S. Government's highest honor in science, the National Medal of Science in 1974.

Lester Germer (April 3, 1895 – August 16, 1977) was an American physicist best known for his work in neutron physics and molecular beam research. He also worked on the Manhattan Project, in particular relating to the design of gaseous diffusion uranium enrichment plants. Germer was a major figure in physics for over four decades and was elected to the National Academy of Sciences in 1956.

Lester Germer (1896–1971) was a German-born physicist who made significant contributions to the field of physics. He was best known for his discovery of the diffraction of electrons by crystals and his subsequent work on the theory of electron diffraction, a process now known as Germer scattering. This work was instrumental in shaping the field of quantum mechanics. Germer was also a pioneer in the development of electron microscopy. Throughout his career, Germer held numerous posts at institutions such as the Cavendish Laboratory and the University of Oxford. He was the father of the American physicist Leo Germer, and the uncle of the mathematical physicist Leonard C. Lewin. Germer's wife, the Scottish-born physicist Dr. Helen Brooke Naylor, a pupil of Ernest Rutherford, worked as a research fellow with Germer in the research group at Oxford.

Lester Germer (17 April 1897 – 15 October 1971) was an American physicist. He is best known for his work on the discovery of the wave-particle duality of matter. He was part of the team of physicists, along with his co-workers George E. Ahrens and Otto Stern, that first proved the existence of electron diffraction in 1927. The experiment was conducted at the University of California, Berkeley, and proved that electrons had both wave and particle properties. This corroborated the de Broglie hypothesis, which was proposed in 1924, and inaugurated the new field of quantum mechanics. He also verified the uncertain mass of the electron by measuring its magnetic moment, and conducted measurements of the g-factor of the proton that provided key clues for the development of the modern electron-nucleon interaction model.

Lester Germer (15 June 1896 – 2 December 1971) was an American physicist and one of the pioneers of modern physics. Germer is best known for his 1931 experiment confirming de Broglie's prediction that electrons have a wave nature. He also contributed significantly to the development of the electron microscope, and carried out early studies on the scattering of neutrons by matter. Germer was also credited with a number of important achievements in the fields of nuclear and atomic physics. He served as a professor of physics at the University of Maryland, College Park, and was the head of the Physics Department at the University of Texas at El Paso.

Lester Germer (April 18, 1896–March 20, 1971) was an American physicist and one of the primary figures in the early development of quantum mechanics, along with Werner Heisenberg, Erwin Schrödinger, Paul Dirac, and Wolfgang Pauli. He was an avid supporter of Albert Einstein's theories of relativity, and published several important works extending the ideas of quantum physics. Germer's research focused largely on the energy level of electrons in atoms, providing evidence that electrons could be regarded as "particles" subject to quantum laws. He also made important contributions to the understanding of the spin of electrons bound in atoms and proposed a cyclic model for the atom.

Germer was a professor at Harvard University and was known as an excellent professor, mentor and advisor to many physicists in the early days of quantum mechanics. He also maintained close contacts with European physicists, including Niels Bohr, who visited him several times in Boston. Germer was arguably the first physicist to understand and promote the significance of quantum mechanics theory in the United States. As such, he is often considered a founding father of modern quantum mechanics.

Lester Halbert Germer (July 24, 1896 - May 10, 1971) was an American theoretical physicist who, together with his mentor and collaborator, Wolfgang Pauli (winner of the 1945 Nobel Prize in Physics) made important contributions to the understanding of the structure of matter. Germer was a professor of physics at Fordham University in New York City and a senior research physicist at Bell Telephone Laboratories, where he worked until his death.

Germer made a number of contributions to the field of solid-state physics, most notably the observation of what is now known as the 'germer effect', a phenomenon in which electrons can penetrate a solid's inner layers to create phonons (sound vibrations) at the surface. Germer was also the first to observe electron diffraction in a gas, opening a new field of investigation into the nature of molecules and structure of solids. Germer and Bell Labs played a key role in the development of the transistor, fielding the first working device and ultimately proving the feasibility of the technology in 1947.

Lester Halbert Germer (September 13, 1896 – January 15, 1971) was an American physicist who worked on the structure and properties of atomic nuclei. He conducted a long series of experiments to study nuclear reactions and the behavior of atomic nuclei at very low energies. He also studied the properties of unstable nuclei and observed the occurrence of proton-neutron interactions in nuclear reactions. His research supported the hypothesis that the nucleus of an atom is composed of protons and neutrons, and was a major contributor to the development of the Bohr model of the nucleus. He was a professor at the Rice Institute (now Rice University) from 1926 to 1945 and at the University of California, Berkeley from 1945 until his retirement in 1961.

Lester Germer (12 August 1895 – 15 May 1971) was an American physicist, who worked on various topics in modern physics including nuclear physics and solid state physics. He is known for his research on the electron and its properties, particularly on the scattering of electrons from various materials. Germer was the first to experimentally detect the electron diffraction effect, a quantum mechanical phenomenon. He was also the first to demonstrate the wave-like nature of neutrons, and a pioneer in the application of neutron scattering as a research tool. He was also instrumental in the development of the nuclear submarine. Germer was also an Accredited Professor at Johns Hopkins University, University of Virginia and Ohio Wesleyan University, among others.

Lester Hubert Germer (September 23, 1895 – October 11, 1971) was an American physicist who is best known for his study of the electron and for his contributions to the study of neutron diffraction and neutron physics. He was born in Cincinnati, Ohio and earned his B.A. from the University of Cincinnati in 1915, and his Ph.D. from Harvard University in 1924.

This was the first-ever direct observation of the wave nature of the neutron, a breakthrough in the field of neutron physics. Germer also showed that the diffraction of a neutron beam through a single crystal can be used to determine the structure of a substance. His work on determining the positions of atoms in a crystal was instrumental in the development of modem crystallography.

Germer died in Cambridge, Massachusetts in 1971. He was a member of a number of scientific societies

Lester Germer (1900–1969), was an American physicist and one of the first scientists to investigate the behavior of atoms and molecules at the atomic level. He is best known for his work with the Austrian physicist, Erwin Schrödinger, on the wave properties of matter, and their subsequent supposition of the volume of a single electron. Germer's work on electron diffraction helped develop the electron microscope and ushered in the era of nanotechnology. He also made numerous contributions to quantum mechanics, among them being the development of the first method of measuring the magnetic moment of the proton. Germer also worked with Ernest Lawrence to develop the celebrated Lawrence-Germer cyclotron, which allowed researchers to develop the control over subatomic particles and first use them to generate electrical energy.

John Joseph Hughes (July 19, 1797 – January 3, 1864) was an Irish-born prelate of the Roman Catholic Church in the United States. He served as the fourth Archbishop of New York from 1842 until his death in 1864. He was created a cardinal in 1842.

Hughes was born in Ireland, the son of a farmer. He was educated at the Royal College of St. Patrick in Maynooth, and was ordained a priest in 1819. He emigrated to the United States in 1817, and served as a pastor in Philadelphia and Lancaster, Pennsylvania. In 1826, he was appointed the first president of St. John's College in Fordham, New York. He was consecrated Bishop of New York in 1838, and was made Archbishop of New York in 1842.

As Archbishop, Hughes was a strong advocate for the establishment of the Catholic Church in the United States. He was a vocal opponent of anti-Catholicism, and was instrumental in the establishment of the first Catholic schools and orphanages in New York

Jean Hugo (1894–1984) was a French painter, illustrator, engraver, and sculptor. He was born in Paris, the son of the writer Victor Hugo and his wife, Juliette Drouet. He was the grandson of the poet, novelist, and dramatist Victor Hugo.

Jean Hugo was a prolific artist, best known for his drawings and engravings. He also produced paintings, lithographs, sculptures, tapestries, and ceramics. He was associated with the art movements of Fauvism, Cubism, and Surrealism. He illustrated books by authors such as Charles Baudelaire, Paul Verlaine, and Jean Cocteau. He also designed sets and costumes for the theatre, including works by Jean Cocteau, Maurice Maeterlinck, and Paul Claudel.
sample_passages: Jean Hugo (1894-1984) was a French painter, illustrator and engraver of the 20th century. Hugo was the grandson of the French poet, playwright, and novelist Victor Hugo and the great-grandson of the French general and statesman Victor Hugo. Born in Paris, where he spent almost all of his life, Jean Hugo was a classically educated artist who studied at the École des Beaux-Arts and in the Julian Academy. He was particularly known for his depiction of the ballet, which was a consistent theme in his work. Hugo often depicted unclothed dancers in a linear style that harkened back to the art of the old masters. His art was compared favorably to that of Edgar Degas, one of his most admired predecessors. Hugo's artworks were featured in several prestigious exhibitions during his lifetime and he was awarded the Grand Prix at the 1958 Venice Biennale.

Jean Hugo (5 October 1894 – 5 April 1984) was a French painter, illustrator, theatre designer and author. Born in Paris, Hugo was the grandson of the poet, playwright, novelist, essayist, visual artist, statesman, human rights activist and exponent of the Romantic movement in France, Victor Hugo. 

Jean Hugo began training in drawing and painting at a young age under the instruction of his father, Georges Hugo, himself a painter. By fourteen Jean Hugo had become an accomplished artist, building upon the tutelage of his father and other family members, who were all respected visual artists. Hugo's first exhibited work was shown at the 1908 Salon des Indépendants and won the affections of the art world and several important patrons such as Dr. Théodore Duret.

Jean Hugo (18 July 1894 – 17 January 1984) was a French painter, illustrator, engraver and one of the twentieth century's foremost illustrators of books and posters. He was born at the Château de Vauvenargues, Bouches-du-Rhône, in the south of France. He was the great-grandson of the writer, statesman and soldier Victor Hugo and the grandson of Georges Hugo. He painted landscapes and scenes of nature, which he found on the Balearic Islands, Pont-Aven and Turkey.

Jean Hugo (1894–1984) was a French painter, illustrator, printmaker, theatre designer and author. He was born in Paris and his father was the poet, playwright, novelist, editor and publisher Victor Hugo.

Jean Hugo (April 16, 1894 – April 12, 1984) was a French modernist painter, illustrator, engraver and theatre designer. He was born in Paris and his father was the illustrator, author and conservationist, Victor Hugo. Jean was the grandson of the poet and novelist, Victor Hugo.

He was born in Paris into a prominent artistic family: his grandfather was the poet, playwright, artist, and novelist Victor Hugo, and his father (the elder Jean) was a painter. Jean Hugo (23 April 1894 – 26 January 1984) was an illustrator, painter, writer, and founding member of the Rose Ratio art collective. He is best known for his illustrations for Victor Hugo's works and is also noted for his engagement in the surrealist movement.

He was born in Paris, the great-grandson of writer and playwright Victor Hugo. Initially, Hugo studied architecture. Jean Hugo (23 February 1894 – 19 March 1984) was a French illustrator, painter, engraver, draughtsman and stage designer. Born in Paris in the family of Victor Hugo's eldest son, Jean Hugo was the great-grandson of the famous French writer Victor Hugo. He became one of the most popular visual artists of the first half of the 20th century in France. He specialized in etchings and lithographs, as well as having a great talent for pochoir-work, or stenciling. His artworks often showcased his admiration for nature and its creatures, as well as his distinct Parisian charm. Jean Hugo painted in the various impressionistic styles of the time, often depicting scenes of Paris and its surrounding areas. During the 1930s and 1940s, his expressionist paintings were a success and were widely exhibited throughout Europe. He also worked as an illustrator, being commissioned to do work for many prestigious books of the period.

Jean Hugo (1894–1984) was a French illustrator, painter, set designer, and writer. Hugo studied at the École des Beaux-Arts in Paris, but his art was more closely related to symbolism. He was highly respected for his considerable talent for both design and illustration, and is considered to be the last great representative of Art Nouveau. Hugo was also renowned for his posters and advertising designs, as well as his theatrical set designs. His designs often incorporated pathos, as he believed that art should, and was very popular with the general public in France during his lifetime. Hugo also wrote several novels, most notably Gringoire, which was initially published in serial form between 1927 and 1928, and was later made into an acclaimed 1934 French film.

Jean Hugo (1883 – 1984) was a French artist, novelist and playwright of the early to mid-20th century. He was a member of the Hugo family, one of the most influential dynasties in French literature and art. Hugo studied painting at the École des Beaux-Arts in Paris, but quickly abandoned conventional art for the poetry of life and freedom. From 1910 to 1914 he exhibited with the Fauves, a group of modern painters led by Henri Matisse. In addition to painting, Hugo wrote novels, plays, film scripts and poetry. His prolific writing helped to define the modern French style of the 1920s. He was also a friend of Pablo Picasso and was deeply involved in the Montparnasse art scene. Hugo's works exude the spirit of freedom, often depicting the beauty and dynamism of life beyond the traditional limits of art. His best known works are his portraits and landscapes, which combine humour, irony, and social criticism.

Jean Hugo (April 24, 1894 – November 22, 1984) was a French artist, illustrator, engraver, and author, who worked in many different media. He was the son of the author Victor Hugo and the grand-nephew of the painter François-René de Chateaubriand. He also illustrated books and had many solo exhibitions throughout Europe. He was a member of the Association of Revolutionary Writers and Artists and an organizer of the Exposition's Internationale d'Avant-Garde in Paris. Hugo was a prolific artist, producing over 400 prints and many paintings and illustrations. He also wrote several novels and short stories and was an early experimenter with the technique of automatic drawing, which he practiced throughout his life.

Jean Hugo (1894-1984) was a French painter, illustrator, engraver, and scenographer. He was born in Paris in 1894 and studied at the École des Beaux-arts in Paris from 1911 until 1913. Jean Hugo (1894–1984) was a French painter, illustrator, engraver, and sculptor who is best known for his work in the Neo-Romantic style. He was born in Paris on 13 June 1894, the eldest of the eight children of the engraver Roger Hugo and the great-grandson of Victor Hugo. Inheriting his grandfather's passionate liberalism, Jean Hugo opposed the rise of fascism. Jean Hugo was also heavily influenced by his mother, the daughter of a physician, who was a fervent Catholic and devotee of Saint Thérèse de Lisieux. Jean Hugo (1894–1984) was a French artist, illustrator and printmaker. He was born in Paris, the son of the poet, dramatist, novelist and artist Victor Hugo (1802–1885). Jean Hugo's early works included a series of etchings of ruins and Gothic architecture that he made in the 1930s. He then moved on to more complex and expressionist compositions, often featuring people, musical instruments and animals. 

He was part of a society of painters and sculptors known as the School of Paris, and in 1939, he exhibited alongside such artists as Marc Chagall, Fernand Léger and Pablo Picasso. Hugo was also known for his stage design, creating sets for such shows as the Jean Cocteau's Les Chevaliers de la Table Ronde, and other works of classical theatre. He was awarded the Grand Prix National des Arts in 1948. Hugo was an acknowledged influence on the graphic novelist Jacques Tardi, and his work can be seen in the posters for several of the latter's books.

Jean Hugo (1894–1984) was a French artist, illustrator, and author. He studied at the École des Beaux-Arts in Paris and was a student of Fernand Cormon and Jean-Paul Laurens. His works were exhibited at the Salon des Artistes Français and the Salon d'Automne.

Hugo worked as a painter, illustrator, and author, his work often combining these three fields. He is most well-known for his illustrations in the works of Victor Hugo and Baudelaire and his collaborations in making posters for the theatre and cinema. His 1929 painting Les Funérailles de Victor Hugo is especially celebrated, as it commemorates the poet's funeral and honors Hugo's legacy. Hugo also wrote several books, including monographs on his fellow artists André Dunoyer de Segonzac and Jean-Léon Gérôme. 

In addition, Hugo was part of the group Associated Painters and Sculptors and served as the group's president beginning in 1958. He was awarded the Legion of Honour in 1975.

Jean Hugo (7 February 1894 – 28 July 1984) was a French painter, illustrator, engraver, and writer, and the grandson of Victor Hugo. Born in Paris, Hugo was deeply influenced by his grandfather's with drawings and paintings. He attended the Lycée Condorcet and studied at the Atelier Cormon, where he was a student of Fernand Cormon. He became a figure in the artistic world of Paris and was friends with many of the city's artistic and literary greats, including Picasso, Bonnard, Matisse and André Gide.

Hugo's works, spanning six decades, combined bright colors and geometric forms to create meditative and contemplative abstractions. His paintings and chalk drawings, often of birds and natural forms, often reveal his fascination with the relationship between nature and man. He also completed many book illustrations, a series of lithographs for a Maurice Baring poem and illustrated several editions of his grandfather's works.

Jean Hugo (9 April 1894 – 22 June 1984) was a French painter, illustrator, theatre designer and author. He was associated with the Symbolist movement and lived in the greater Paris area all his life.

The son of a wealthy industrialist, Hugo was born in Paris and began studying art at the prestigious Lycee Condorcet. Later, he attended Academie Julian and then the École des Beaux-Arts. He initially created oil paintings and watercolors that exhibited in Paris's galleries, and became a frequent exhibitor at the Salon d'Automne.

Hugo was renowned throughout the early 20th century for his large, allegorical murals and stained glass in churches and public buildings, including some in the United States. His works can be found in the Musées d'Art Moderne in Paris, in the Musée National Jean-Hugo in Nogent-sur-Marne and at the Musée Dobrée in Nantes, among other places.

He was a research scientist at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory from 1991 to 1993. John Cameron (1860–1937) was a Canadian politician who served in the Legislative Assembly of Alberta from 1921 to 1935. He was born in Scotland and immigrated to Canada in 1883.

Cameron was first elected to the Alberta Legislature in the 1921 Alberta general election. He was re-elected in the 1926 Alberta general election and the 1930 Alberta general election. He served as a member of the United Farmers of Alberta caucus.

Cameron was a strong advocate for rural Alberta and was a vocal opponent of the provincial government's policy of centralization. He was a proponent of the development of the province's natural resources and was a strong supporter of the Alberta Wheat Pool. He was also a strong advocate for the rights of farmers and was a vocal opponent of the Alberta government's policy of taxation.

Cameron retired from politics in 1935 and died in 1937. He was buried in Edmonton's Beechmount Cemetery.

Pedersen attended the University of Texas, where she swam for the Texas Longhorns swimming and diving team in National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) competition. She was a two-time NCAA champion, winning the 200-yard butterfly in 1985 and 1986. She was also a four-time All-American.

Susan Pedersen (born 13 April 1975) is an Australian former competitive swimmer and a Commonwealth Games gold medalist. She specialised in freestyle, butterfly and medley events.

Susan Elizabeth Pedersen (June 12, 1954 – May 8, 2006) was an American competition swimmer. She represented the United States at the 1972 Summer Olympics in Munich, Germany, where she finished fourth in the women's 200-meter backstroke event.

Pedersen was born in Newark, New Jersey. She was raised in Tenafly, New Jersey, and attended Tenafly High School. She went on to Stanford University, where she was a student and collegiate swimmer from 1977 to 1980.

In 1972, Pedersen qualified for the U.S. Olympic team at the U.S. Olympic Trials. At the 1972 Summer Olympics in Munich, she placed fourth in the 200-meter backstroke event with a time of 2:21.56, which was 11 seconds off the winning pace.

In 1994, she was inducted into the International Swimming Hall of Fame. Pedersen died of cancer in 2006 at the age of 51.

Susan Pedersen (born 12 December 1957) is an Australian former Olympic freestyle swimmer of the 1970s. She won a silver medal in the 200m freestyle at the 1976 Summer Olympics in Montreal. She also won the 200m freestyle gold medal at the 1974 Commonwealth Games in Christchurch, New Zealand.

Pedersen broke eleven world records throughout her career, largely concentrated in the 100m and 200m freestyle events. She has been inducted into the Australian Olympic Hall of Fame, the International Swimming Hall of Fame and the Sport Australia Hall of Fame.

Susan Pedersen (born 27 October 1953) is an Australian former competitive swimmer who represented Australia in the 1972 Summer Olympics in Munich. Pedersen was born in Sydney, and was part of the group of teenage swimmers such as Shane Gould and Karen Moras who dominated women's swimming in the early 1970s.

Pedersen won a total of five medals at the 1971 Pan Pacific Swimming Championships in Japan, five at the 1972 Summer Olympics, including a gold in the 400-meter Individual Medley which she won in world-record time. She also won a bronze medal in the 200-meter individual medley, and two silver medals in the 100-meter and 400-meter freestyle. After the 1972 Olympic Games, she retired from competitive swimming. In 1979, she was inducted into the International Swimming Hall of Fame.

Susan Pedersen (born 18 January 1947) is a former Australian swimmer who won a bronze medal in the 4 × 100 m freestyle relay at the 1964 Summer Olympics. She competed at the 1960 Summer Olympics and at the 1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games.

Pedersen was the daughter of Olympic swimmer Esther Pedersen. She raced for the Melbourne Amateur Swimming Club and for Melbourne High School, where she was head prefect in 1964. Her best individual result at the Olympics came in the 100-metre backstroke, which she finished fifth in. She won the 1965 Australian gold medal in the 220-yard freestyle.

Pedersen retired from competitive swimming in 1966. In later years she served as a swimming coach and administrator. She was inducted into the Sport Australia Hall of Fame in 1990, and into the International Swimming Hall of Fame in 1996.

Susan Elizabeth Pedersen (born 9 January 1971) is a former Olympic and Commonwealth Games swimmer who represented Australia in four Olympic Games and two Commonwealth Games.

Susan Pedersen (born January 25, 1933 in London, England) is a British former championship swimmer. She competed in the 1948 and 1952 Olympic Games, where she won five medals—three silver, one gold and one bronze. Pedersen became the first female British athlete to win five medals in one Olympics when she won the 100 m breaststroke gold, 400 m medley bronze, and the silver medals in the 200 m breaststroke, 100 m freestyle, and 400 m freestyle at the 1952 Helsinki Games. She held the world record for the 200 m breaststroke from 1956 to 1958.

Pedersen was inducted into the International Swimming Hall of Fame as an "Honor Swimmer" in 1995, and four years later was made an Honorary Member of the International Swimming Hall of Fame. She retired following the 1956 Melbourne Olympics, having won a medal tally of nine—one gold, six silver and two bronze medals.

Susan E. Pedersen (born 12 June 1950) is an American former competition swimmer, Olympic medalist, and former world record-holder.

Pedersen represented the United States at the 1968 Summer Olympics in Mexico City. She won a bronze medal as a member of the third-place U.S. team in the women's 4×100-meter medley relay. Individually, she placed tenth in the women's 100-meter butterfly final.

At the 1970 World Aquatics Championships in Belgrade, Yugoslavia, Pedersen set a world record in the 400-meter individual medley with a time of 5:14.3.

At the 1971 Pan American Games in Cali, Colombia, Pedersen won a silver medal in the 400-meter individual medley, and a bronze medal in the 100-meter butterfly. She retired from competitive swimming shortly thereafter.

Susan Pedersen (born 12 March 1983), is a former swimmer from Switzerland who specialized in freestyle events. A native of Wil, she represented Switzerland in the freestyle events at the 2004 Summer Olympics. During her career, Pedersen set several Swiss records in the 50 metres, 100 metres, and 200 metres freestyle. She also collected two individual titles and two relay medals at the 2001 European Junior Swimming Championships. Pedersen retired in 2006, following her second Olympics in Athens, Greece.

Susan Pedersen (born December 27, 1947) is an Australian former competitive swimmer. She competed in four events at the 1964 Summer Olympics in Tokyo. In the 400-metre individual medley, she won the bronze medal behind American swimmer Ann Curtis and Dutch swimmer Marie Braun. Pedersen won four medals at the 1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games in Perth, Western Australia: a gold medal in the 400-metre individual medley, silver medals in the 110-yard and 220-yard individual medleys, and a bronze medal as a member of the third-place Australian unit in the 4×110-yard medley relay. After her swim career, she became an advocate for disability rights; in 1971, she became the first disabled person to be appointed to a public health board.

Susan Pedersen (born May 3, 1942) is an American former swimmer, Olympic medalist, and former world record-holder in three events. Competing at the 1960 Summer Olympics in Rome, she won gold medals in the women's 200-meter backstroke and 4x100-meter medley relay, and a silver medal in the women's 100-meter backstroke. In the 200-meter backstroke, Pedersen set a new world record of 2:29.2 in the final. She also competed in the 400-meter individual medley and finished fourth.

At the 1959 Pan American Games in Chicago, she won gold medals in the 100-meter and 200-meter backstroke and two silver medals in the 4x100-meter freestyle and 4x100-meter medley relays. Pedersen also won seven medals in various individual and relay events at the 1958 and 1962 U.S. National Championships.

Pedersen is the daughter of former U.S. Olympic swimmer Keith Pedersen (1932).

Susan Pedersen (born October 10, 1987) is an Australian former swimmer who represented Australia at the 2006 Commonwealth Games in the 100-meter breaststroke and 200-meter individual medley. She also competed in the 800-meter freestyle and 400-meter individual medley, but did not make the finals in either event. Pedersen also competed at the 2007 World Swimming Championships in Melbourne, Australia and the 2008 Olympic Games in Beijing. She specializes in short and middle distance freestyle and breaststroke events. After retiring from swimming, Pedersen attended the University of Melbourne, where she earned a Bachelor of Arts in Physical Education with a focus on sport psychology. She is currently a research assistant at the University of Queensland.

Susan Pedersen (born August 7, 1985) is an American competition swimmer and Olympic gold medalist. She won a gold medal as a member of the winning U.S. team in the 4×100-meter medley relay at the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing. Pedersen also competed in the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece, but did not win a medal.

Pedersen graduated from the University of Arizona in 2007, where she competed for coach Frank Busch for the Arizona Wildcats swimming and diving team in National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) competition. Susan Pedersen (born January 24, 1950 in Melbourne, Australia) is an Australian former swimmer. At the age of 15, in 1965, she won her first national title in the 220 yards butterfly. She then went on to become an international star and compete in the 1968 Mexico City Olympic Games.

At the 1968 games, Pedersen placed 6th in the 200m butterfly, and along with her teammates, she won a silver medal in the 4×100m medley relay. She also placed 4th in the 100m butterfly. Pedersen is best known for winning the gold medal in the 200m butterfly at the 1970 Commonwealth Games in Edinburgh, Scotland, becoming the first woman to swim the distance in under 2 minutes 30 seconds.

Pedersen retired from competitive sport in 1971 after winning seven Australian titles and breaking six Commonwealth records. She was inducted into the Sport Australia Hall of Fame in 1997.

Susan Pedersen is a Danish swimmer who won the bronze medal in the 200 m Freestyle at the 2002 World Aquatics Championships in Berlin. The competition was held in the Olympiastadion Berlin, Germany, on July 24, 2002. She also competed in the 2000 Summer Olympics. Pedersen's greatest success to date is certainly the bronze medal she won at the 2002 World Championships. She finished with a time of 1:59.30, only 0.11 seconds ahead of Sweden's Lisa Norden. The gold medal went to Australian champion Giaan Rooney in a time of 1:58.68.

She was also part of the Danish freestyle relay team which won gold in the 2001 European Championships. Pedersen retired from competition in 2005.

Susan Pedersen (born 13 November 1971) is a former competitive swimmer from Denmark, who specialized in the freestyle and butterfly events. She competed for her native country at the 1992 Summer Olympics in Barcelona, Spain. She achieved her best result in the 200 m freestyle event, where she ended up in eight overall. She held a total of seven Danish records in freestyle and butterfly events, between 1991 and 1996.

Pedersen is currently employed as the national team coach of the Danish Swimming Federation. In 2012, she was elected to the International Swimming Hall of Fame in Fort Lauderdale, Florida.

Susan Pedersen (born October 24, 1953) is an American former competitive swimmer and a two-time individual Olympic medalist. At the 1972 Summer Olympics in Munich, West Germany, she won a silver medal in the 4×100-meter medley relay, and a bronze medal in the 400-meter individual medley.

Pedersen attended the University of Southern California (USC), where she swam for the USC Trojans swimming and diving team from 1971 to 1975. She won four NCAA titles and was a member of four NCAA championship teams. She was the recipient of the 1975 Honda Sports Award for Swimming and Diving, and was inducted into the International Swimming Hall of Fame in 1989.

Susan Pedersen (born 11 February 1963) is a former New Zealand competitive swimmer. She represented New Zealand at the 1984 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, in the 200m and 400m freestyle, and in the 4 x 200m freestyle relay. 

Pedersen enjoyed long distance swimming, and had success in the longer freestyle events in New Zealand and Australia. She set a New Zealand 800m record at the 1980 New Zealand Short Course Championships. A week before the New Zealand trials for the Los Angeles Olympics, she broke the New Zealand 400m record. At the 1984 New Zealand Short Course Championships, Pedersen beat her own record in the 800m. 

Pedersen trained under her Coach, Don Talbot. She retired after the Olympics in 1984, but has since returned to competitive swimming, competing in the New Zealand Masters Swimming championships. In December 2014, she competed in her first international Masters meet, the FINA World Masters Championships in Montreal – her only event was the 400m freestyle, in which she won gold in the 55-59 age group.

Susan Pedersen (born 18 February 1974) is a former swimmer from Australia. She competed in three consecutive Summer Olympics, starting in 1992. At her first Games, in Barcelona, she made the final of the 800 metres freestyle, finishing sixth. At the 1996 Atlanta Olympics she won a bronze medal in the 400 metres individual medley and reached the 200 metres individual medley final, finishing sixth. In 2000, in Sydney, she was a member of the 4 × 200 metres freestyle relay team and she finished sixth in the 200-metre individual medley and seventh in the 400-metre individual medley.

sentences: Susan Pedersen (born October 28, 1965) is an American former competition swimmer who represented the United States at the 1984 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, California.
She competed in the women's 200-meter butterfly, and finished fourth in the event final with a time of 2:13.45.
Pedersen attended the University of Texas, where she swam for the Texas Longhorns swimming and diving team in National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) competition.
She was a two-time NCAA champion, winning the 200-yard butterfly in 1985 and 1986.
She was also a four-time All-American.

Meagher was born in the Bronx, New York, and grew up in the nearby town of Yonkers. He began playing music at an early age, learning to play the guitar and bass. Ron Meagher is a Canadian musician, composer and producer. He has been playing, recording and performing music for over 40 years.

Meagher has performed and/or recorded music with a diverse array of artists and musicians, including Loreena McKennitt, Sarah Brightman, Don ("the Judge") Williams, Tom Cochrane, Kim Mitchell, The Skydiggers, Sky, the Irish Rovers, the late Stan Rogers, Big Rude Jake, and Scottish New Orleanian Pete Sutherland. He was a founding member of the band "Rough Trade" and has toured extensively both as a solo artist and with other bands.

Meagher has also composed music for film and television, and produced records for some of today's most distinguished artists. He holds a place in the History of Canadian Music, as he is credited with being a founding producer of independent music labels, including Stony Plain Records, True North Records and his own, Pigeon Hole Records.

Ron Meagher (born Robert Dennis Meagher, October 2, 1947) is a retired Canadian ice hockey winger who played fourteen National Hockey League (NHL) seasons between 1966 and 1981. He was drafted third overall by the Detroit Red Wings in the 1966 NHL Amateur Draft after a successful junior career with Dubuque in the United States Hockey League.

During his pro career, Meagher played for the Red Wings, California Golden Seals, Cleveland Barons, Kansas City Scouts, and Washington Capitals. Throughout his life, Meagher has remained connected to hockey. Since ending his playing career he has served as a minor league coach for the Adirondack Red Wings (AHL), Pacific Hockey League, Las Vegas Thunder (IHL) and Augusta Lynx (ECHL). He became head coach of the Dallas Freeze (CHL) in 2000 and held that position until 2002.

Ron Meagher (born 11 June 1935 in Kooringal, New South Wales) is an Australian former professional footballer, coach and administrator. He began his career as a professional footballer with Italian club AC Milan in 1956, making over 200 appearances for the club in eight seasons, most of them as captain. In 1964, he moved to Hajduk Split in Croatia, to become one of their greatest ever players. He played there for eight years before returning to Australia in 1972 and retiring from playing.

As a coach, he began with Melbourne Croatia Blues in 1974 and then became the first appointed coach of the newly formed Sydney Olympic in 1976. He also coached for two stints at Marconi Fairfield and Wollongong City and was an assistant coach with the Socceroos in the 1980s.

In 2006, he was inducted into the FFA Football Hall of Fame. Meagher received the Medal of the Order of Australia in 2020 for his service to football. He has also been recognised by Football NSW as one of their greatest ever players and coaches.

Ron Meagher (born 1966) is an American professional bass player and composer who is best known for his work defining the bass playing for the band The Wallflowers. He has also worked with other notable artists, including Dixie Chicks, Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers, Brian Wilson, Lisa Marie Presley, No Doubt, Lee Ann Womack, John Prine, and many more.

Meagher grew up listening to Led Zeppelin, Steve Miller Band and Marvin Gaye. He learned to play bass when he was around sixteen. At first, he had no plans of making music a career. Later, however, after going to Mira Costa College in Los Angeles, he worked as a barback at Mabuhay Gardens in San Francisco and a moose wrangler in Alaska. It was during this time that he started to take his music seriously and gained the experience that defines his bass playing today.

He has performed on virtually all genres of music including rock, folk, country, jazz, and world music. While recording and playing his own compositions and arrangements, he remains an intently focused and versatile professional.

Ron Meagher (1951-2017) was a gospel singer, songwriter, and music producer. He was born in Mississippi and later moved to Nashville, Tennessee, where he would become an important figure in gospel music. He released multiple albums of gospel music in the late 1970s and early 1980s, and his music was performed by numerous acclaimed gospel artists, including Rev. James Cleveland, Albertina Walker, and the Golden Gate Quartet. In addition, he collaborated with various other gospel songwriters, including the Winans, Melvin Williams, and Lamar Campbell. He also produced and wrote for multiple gospel albums, including Rev. James Cleveland’s 1981 Grammy-nominated album I’ll Do My Best. Meagher continued to be involved in gospel music until his death in 2017.

Ron Meagher is a highly accomplished underwater photographer and conservationist whose work has been featured in dozens of books, magazines and multimedia presentations about nature and the environment. His focus is on the macroscopic study of coral reefs and the small animals that inhabit their habitats. Through his underwater photography, he has documented the effects of pollution and global warming on coral reefs and marine life around the world. He uses the power of his images to entertain, educate and motivate others to protect the coral reef communities he captures.

Ron has spent countless hours in the water teaching himself the complex techniques necessary to produce magnificent images of the tiny creatures that live among the corals. In a time of shrinking reef habitats, he is also dedicated to monitoring both natural and man-made threats on the fragile coral ecosystems. His passion for reef conservation has taken him on assignment to the Caribbean, Hawaii, New Zealand, Australia and Africa, and he has contributed to an array of conservation attempts throughout the world.

Ron Meagher (June 29, 1945 – April 10, 2008) was an American musician, singer and songwriter best known as the frontman of the soul, rock and R&B band Ron Meagher and the Seventh Son, which was formed in Louisville, Kentucky in 1966. He was the band's principal songwriter and the lead singer of most of its recordings. 

Meagher's seven-member band was initially suggested by his producers and was unique in its integration of rock, soul, and R&B styles. His first record, "Can't Stop Thinkin' About You", achieved local success and became the band's first hit single. Meagher also composed music for Broadway and off-Broadway shows, including Bubbling Brown Sugar and One Mo' Time, and wrote for television shows such as The Cosby Show, A Different World, and Martin. Meagher died of cancer in 2008 at the age of 62.

Ron Meagher (born October 31, 1965) is a Canadian curler.

Meagher has won two provincial curling championships. He won the Saskatchewan men's provincial title in 2011 as the third for Steve Laycock, and the Northern Ontario provincial title in 2020 as part of the Brad Jacobs rink.

Meagher has also made several appearances in national competition. He has competed in six Brier championships, as coach of the Northern Ontario team in 2014, 2015, 2016 and 2017 and with the Jacobs rink in 2018 and 2019.

At the 2018 Tim Hortons Brier, Meagher's Jacobs' rink won the championship, winning the final game 7–6 in an extra end with a clutch double raise takeout.

Meagher also has one world championship appearance. He was the coach of Northern Ontario's Brad Jacobs rink at the 2014 World Men's Curling Championship in Beijing, China, where they placed sixth.

Ron Meagher (1925 - 2020) was a Canadian professional ice hockey player and executive. He was a forward and played in the National Hockey League (NHL) with the Montreal Canadiens, Toronto Maple Leafs and Detroit Red Wings. Meagher was born in 1925 in Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario, and played his junior hockey with the Sault Greyhounds before joining the NHL with the Canadiens in the 1944–45 season. He was a member of the Leafs, who won the Stanley Cup in 1948, and captained them in the 1959–60 season. Meagher was also an assistant general manager of the Maple Leafs from 1967 to 1971. Later, he was general manager of the Detroit Red Wings from 1971 to 1974. He retired from hockey in 1975 and was inducted into the Hockey Hall of Fame in 1976.

Ron Meagher (born 26 July 1964) is an English former professional footballer and manager who managed the non-league Chippenham Town.

Meagher was born in Wolverhampton but mostly known for his impressive playing career at Bristol City during the 1980s and 1990s. At the age of 16, he signed a professional contract with Bristol City upon leaving school and before long, he made his professional debut in 1983. He went on to make 310 league appearances for the Robins, scoring 47 goals in the process. He played for the club until his retirement in October 1999.

In 2000, Meagher was appointed manager of Chippenham Town, taking them from an unpromising position near the top of the Isthmian League Division Three to the middle of Division one in just three seasons. Meagher was also a keen supporter of youth prospects, and signed a number of players from local teams who have gone on to have successful careers in senior football. He also became a respected coach, and went on to become Director of Football at Chippenham.

Ron Meagher (born June 23, 1934) is an American baseball coach and former professional baseball player. He has been involved in professional baseball in various roles for over 60 years.

Meagher played with the Oakland Oaks of the Pacific Coast League from 1954-1959 and then with the San Francisco Giants in 1962. He was an All-Star for the Oaks in 1956. After his professional career, he became a manager in the Pacific Coast League for the Salt Lake City Angels from 1968-1971. He managed in the minor leagues from 1968-1973, and returned to manage the Houston Astros from 1974-1975 and then the Oakland Athletics from 1980-1982.

Meagher has been a coach with the Kansas City Royals since 1985 and won World Series titles with them in 1985, 2015 and 2019. He was also a coach with the Oakland Athletics in 1987. He was inducted into the Pacific Coast League Hall of Fame in 2008.

Ron Meagher (1930–2021) was an American football, basketball coach, college athletics administrator and soldier. Meagher was born in Miami, Florida on October 2, 1930, and served in the United States Army from 1952 to 1954. He began his coaching career at Florida A&M University, where he coached football, basketball and track. He was the head football coach at Florida A&M from 1970 to 1972, and again from 1982 to 2003, compiling a career record of 168–99. He won 11 conference championships and had a career winning percentage of 62.9%. He led the Rattlers to undefeated regular seasons in 1981 and 2000, and to the NCAA Division I-AA playoffs three times. In 1992, Meagher was inducted into the College Football Hall of Fame. In addition to coaching, Meagher worked at Florida A&M in athletic administration, primarily as its sports information director, receiving numerous honors and awards. Upon his retirement in 2003, Meagher was inducted into the Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference Hall of Fame. Meagher died on August 16, 2021, at the age of 90.

Ron Meagher (born 8 April 1935) is an Australian-born, retired football coach and coach in the National Football League (NFL). He was born in Victoria, Australia and grew up in Fremantle and attended Fremantle Technical High School. He played professional Australian rules football for South Fremantle Football Club and coached for several professional teams in Australia.

Meagher moved to the United States in 1967 to attend college at the University of Southern California, earning his bachelor's degree in physical education. He was an assistant coach with the USC Trojans from 1968–1975, and eventually the offensive coordinator from 1975–1981. Meagher had a brief stint as the head coach of the San Diego Chargers from 1982-1983. He then went on to work as the backup quarterback coach for the Los Angeles Rams. Meagher has also worked as an offensive analyst at Southern Methodist University and in 2007 he was hired as the wide receivers coach at Auburn University.

Ron Meagher (born October 17, 1951) is a Canadian professional ice hockey coach and executive. Meagher played professional hockey in the World Hockey Association and minor pro hockey leagues, mostly as a left winger. After he retired, he moved into management and coaching, working in various capacities in the Central Hockey League, International Hockey League, American Hockey League and National Hockey League.

Meagher served as a coach for the Houston Aeros of the CHL (1986–89) and the Houston Apollos of the IHL (1989–90) before joining the Buffalo Sabres' organization in 1991. He started as an assistant coach with the Sabres and transitioned to head coach of the Rochester Americans (1992–96). He was promoted to the Sabres and coached the club for two seasons, 1996–97 and 1997–98. He then returned to Rochester and was later named the head coach of the Manitoba Moose of the AHL in December of 2001.

Ron Meagher (born June 25, 1966) is an American writer and entrepreneur known for his contributions to the field of advertising creative. Meagher is the founder of Sub Rosa, a creative agency that produces highly engaging advertising, brand experiences, and social media campaigns.

Meagher began his writing career in the mid-1980s at Slate magazine, where he wrote about music, film, and books. He relocated to New York City in 1989, where he worked in various editorial, marketing, and advertising roles for a number of companies. He later moved to London, where he had a stint at the advertising firm Ogilvy & Mather.

In 2001, Meagher co-founded Fahrenheit 212, a creative consultancy that merged creative, design, digital, and technology disciplines to build pioneering projects. Projects included designing products and services that addressed the needs of the underserved, creating digital experiences for brands, and creating advertising campaigns with a focus on innovation and insight.

In 2008, he founded Sub Rosa, which has since become one of the most influential creative agencies in the U.S. Recognized by Adweek, Cannes Lions, and

Ron Meagher (born 8 March 1966) is an Australian former professional rugby league footballer who played for the Balmain Tigers, North Sydney Bears and South Sydney Rabbitohs in the New South Wales Rugby League (NSWRL) competition.

Meagher had a solid career at club level, playing in 203 first grade matches over 13 seasons with Balmain, Norths and Souths, including the 1989 NSWRL grand final win over the Canberra Raiders.

Meagher also represented New South Wales in the State of Origin match in 1989. He made a total of twenty appearances for the Blues.

Meagher also represented Australia on three occasions, all in 1989. He also played in the 1989 Kangaroo Tour of Great Britain. He retired at the end of the 1991 season.

Ron Meagher (1948 – ) is a Canadian businessman well known for his involvement in the telecommunications industry. Born in Moncton, New Brunswick, he is a graduate of the University of New Brunswick.

Meagher founded ComNet Telecom Inc., one of Canada’s leading telecommunications companies. In 2001, he sold the company to Bell Canada. Since then, Meagher has continued to pursue opportunities in this field. He is currently the Chairman of Exceleroc Inc., a Procera Investment Partner and a board member for two Canadian software/telecommunications startups.

In addition to his work in the Canadian telecommunications industry, Meagher is a regular advisor to the Canadian government. Most notably, he was a key contributor to the Performance Report of the Telecommunications Policy Review Panel, which impacted Canada’s telecommunications landscape.

Meagher is an active mentor to young people and a highly involved philanthropist. He has donated to multiple charities and actively supports causes in the field of medicine, telecommunications, and education. He is an avid supporter of the Canadian Film Center and the Banff Centre for Media and the Arts.

Ron Meagher (October 18, 1944 – October 23, 2018) was an American professional basketball player who played in the American Basketball Association (ABA) for the New York Nets between 1969 and 1975. Born in Syracuse, New York, Meagher was originally drafted by the NBA's Detroit Pistons with the 249th overall pick of the 1967 NBA draft. He chose to play in the ABA instead, where he was traded to the New York Nets (then the New Jersey Americans) in his rookie year. 

Meagher was a member of the Nets' undefeated regular season in 1972–73, in which they went 68–16. He had the distinction of leading the ABA in assists in both 1973 and 1975. He also led the league in free throw percentage in 1973. Meagher is also remembered for his role in the 1973 ABA All-Star game when he dribbled out the final five seconds of the game after the West All-Stars had already declared a victory. 

Meagher retired from the ABA in 1975, but remained in the New York area, where he lived until his death in 2018.

Ron Meagher (born September 22, 1940) is an American former track and field athlete who competed mainly in the 800-meter run. Born in Minneapolis, Minnesota, Meagher was a two-time winner of the event at the USA Outdoor Track and Field Championships (1964, 65). He was a three-time Olympian, having competed in the 800-meter run at the 1960, 1964 and 1968 Summer Olympics. He achieved his best result in 1964, when he won the silver medal behind Lynn Davies. Meagher was inducted into the Drake Relays Hall of Fame in 2000 and was recognized as a national-caliber athlete at the annual class of Minnesota All-Time Greats in 2016.

Ronald William Meagher, AM (19 April 1935 – 13 August 2003) was an Australian lawyer and consumer advocate who made a major contribution to the protection and enhancement of consumer rights in Australia.

Meagher graduated from the University of Melbourne with a Bachelor of Laws (LLB) in 1959 and was admitted to practice as a lawyer of the Supreme Court of Victoria in 1961. From 1961 till 1979 he was plaintiff in a plethora of consumer cases. He was able to apply the Trade Practices Act of 1974 to the benefit of consumers of many kinds of products and services, such as cars, rental accommodation and banking. Through his efforts, unscrupulous trading methods were uncovered and stopped, resulting in increased safety and improved quality of many products and services.

He was made a Member of the Order of Australia (AM) in 1986 for services to the community and the advancement of consumer protection and the law. In 2003 he was awarded a distinguished Statesman Award of the Australian Consumers' Association.

main_passage: Billy Barnie (1850–1925) was an American professional baseball player and manager. He played as a catcher and third baseman in Major League Baseball from 1875 to 1892. He is credited with inventing the modern catcher's mitt. Barnie was the first manager of the Baltimore Orioles, leading the team to four consecutive American Association pennants from 1894 to 1897. He also managed the Washington Senators, Boston Beaneaters, and Brooklyn Superbas. Barnie was known for being a colorful and outspoken character. He was nicknamed "The Father of the Baltimore Orioles" and was inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in 1945.

Barnie was born in San Antonio, Texas. He joined the United States Marines Corps in 1951 and served in Vietnam. While there, he became a leader and was instrumental in organizing the troops for the country's longest, deadliest battle of the war, the Battle of Hue City.

Billy Barnie (July 29, 1829–July 20, 1893) was an American professional baseball player and manager. He spent most of his career as a manager, leading two major league clubs and several minor league teams, most notably the Baltimore Orioles of the American Association and National League, which won the National League pennant three times. Affectionately called "Uncle Billy", he was baseball's first true manager, and is credited with establishing many of the strategies and tactics used in the game today. Barnie was also a pioneer in arranging and structuring player contracts and salaries, as well as in producing a comprehensive spring training and touring program for his teams.

Billy Barty (October 25, 1924 – December 23, 2000) was an American actor and activist. Noted for his short stature, he was often cast in roles where he appeared as an eccentric older man, drunk, or elf-like character. He also played a starring role in the 1973 film Under the Rainbow, as the leader of a group of dwarf actors playing roles at a fictionalized version of Judy Garland's 1939 The Wizard of Oz movie premiere.

A three-time Academy Award nominee, he appeared in many films, plays, television shows and commercials. He was the founder and leader of Little People of America, an organization formed to help solve the problems he had encountered in his life due to his restricted stature, and to provide public and medical information to members.

Billy Barnie (January 4, 1829 – May 11, 1886) was an American professional baseball manager. He had a lengthy major league baseball management career, spanning from 1874 to 1889. During those sixteen years, he managed five major league clubs – the Baltimore Canaries (NL, 1874–75), Hartford Dark Blues (NL, 1876–77), Baltimore Orioles (NL, 1882–84), Washington Nationals / Statesmen (AA, 1885) and New York Giants (NL, 1889). Although his teams were invariably described as “rag-tag” and “unlucky”, Barnie was generally well-regarded in his day as a shrewd judge of talent.

Billy Barnie (May 8, 1845 – June 15, 1909) was an American professional baseball player and manager. He played in Major League Baseball as a catcher and player-manager. He served his professional playing career with nine teams of the National Association of Professional Base Ball Players (1871–1875) and National League (1876–1892). He also managed five teams during his career over 11 seasons, including four of the National League's charter franchises. Barnie was known to be innovative and willing to start trends in his game strategies. He was the first manager to use a relief pitcher and the first to play a platoon system with his pitchers and position players. He also had one of the earliest known platoon systems for left and right-handed batters.

Billy Barne ("Billy the Kid") (November 23, 1859 – July 14, 1881) was an American Old West outlaw and gunfighter who was killed by Sheriff Pat Garrett. He has become an iconic figure in American frontier history. During his brief career as a criminal, Barne was suspected of stealing cattle and horses, as well as committing several murders. He was involved in the Lincoln County War, during which he allegedly stole from the wealthy landowner John Chisum. He was eventually captured by Garrett in December 1880 and sentenced to hang. He escaped from jail and returned to Lincoln County to continue his criminal activities, but two months later, Garrett fatally shot him in an ambush.

Billy Barne is best remembered for his reputation as a frontier gunslinger who defied the law. He was part of the outlaw-regime that plagued the New Mexico Territory and later American Southwest during the late 1800s. Billy Barnie (April 10, 1828 – August 14, 1883) was an American manager and umpire in Major League Baseball. He holds the distinction of being the first manager in the history of the American Association, a league that was established in 1882 and later folded after the 1891 season. His managing career lasted from 1882 to 1891.

Barnie was born in New York in 1828 and began playing baseball in 1865. He was a manager in the minor leagues from 1870 to 1881 before joining the newly formed American Association as manager of the Baltimore Orioles. His managing style was described as turbulent and passionate. He was known for his arguments with umpires and his frequent fine-tuning of his team's roster. He managed the Orioles for nine consecutive seasons and also managed the Brooklyn Atlantics in 1885 and 1886. In his 13 seasons as a major league manager, his teams won two pennants and two championships. Barnie died in Baltimore in 1883.

Billy Barnie (1850–1925) was an American Major League Baseball manager and umpire. He was born in New York on August 5, 1850. He is best known for his seven years of managing the Baltimore Orioles in the late 19th century. He also managed the Detroit Wolverines and the Washington Senators and briefly umpired in the American Association. Barnie managed the Orioles from 1882 to 1887, leading them to four straight American Association pennants from 1883 to 1886. He was known for his ability to make astute strategic decisions and his unconventional tactical choices, such as having two players switch positions multiple times in one game.

Billy Barnes (April 4, 1910 – October 4, 1993) was an American actor, singer, and dancer. He appeared in a number of films, including High Society (1956), Silk Stockings (1957), and Around the World in 80 Days (1957). He was nominated for an Academy Award for his performance in the musical An American in Paris (1951). His career also encompassed stage, radio and television work.

Barnes began his career as a dancer and comic on the Broadway stage. He went on to become a soloist, appearing on stage and television in a variety of musical programs. He is best remembered for the song "The Continental", which he performed in An American in Paris, for which he was nominated for an Oscar. His numerous TV appearances include roles in The Jack Benny Program, I Love Lucy, The Dick Van Dyke Show, Get Smart, and The Monkees

Billy Barnie (September 21, 1870 – September 29, 1925) was an American professional baseball player, manager and executive. He had a 10-year playing career with the Baltimore Orioles, the original major league incarnation of the franchise, and served as the team's manager for part of the 1899 season. Barnie was highly regarded for his talent as a catcher, but also made significant contributions as a manager, innovator and ambassador for the sport. He was the first manager to use platooning and shift his defense according to the batters, techniques that have become major parts of baseball strategy. Barnie also played a key role in the integration of the major leagues by signing African-American pitcher George Stovey in 1887.

Billy Barnie, born William Edgar Barnhard, (April 8, 1868 – November 8, 1925) was an American manager, scout, and executive in Major League Baseball. He served as a manager for the Baltimore Orioles, Brooklyn Superbas, and Detroit Tigers in the American League. Barnie was known for his eccentric style, which included his flamboyant dress, cigars, and ever-present drooping mustache. He was among the first to bring intellectual, scientific theories of organization and management to the sport of baseball, pioneering some of the modern strategies and techniques used by major league teams today.

Billy Barnie (September 5, 1829-March 9, 1925) was an accomplished civil engineer, soldier and public official in Baltimore, Maryland. He is best known as the curator of the famed Baltimore Oriole baseball team, which he founded with his brother John Barnie in 1882.

Barnie graduated from the Baltimore Polytechnic Institute in 1846 and spent two years serving as the head of the Civil Engineering Department of Franklin College in Lancaster, Pennsylvania. William "Billy" Barnie (August 15, 1852 – March 18, 1924) was a professional baseball player and manager. Born in New Haven, Connecticut, he played 11 seasons in Major League Baseball (MLB), primarily as a catcher, from 1874 to 1884, for 12 different major league clubs. He was the first manager of the Baltimore Orioles, leading the team for its first two seasons (1882–83) in the American Association. After serving as a coach at Yale University, Barnie managed in the minor leagues and coached several teams in the major leagues. He died in Norwich, Connecticut, in 1924.

Billy Barnie (1863-1925) was an American Major League Baseball manager and executive. He was the second manager of the Baltimore Orioles, from 1894 to 1902, and the fourth president of the Baltimore Orioles, from 1902 to 1909.

Barnie was born in NewField, New York, and began his baseball career in 1883, at the age of 20, playing for the local semi-pro baseball team of his hometown. Billy Barnie (c. 1845 – c. 1915) was a professional baseball manager. He is ranked among the top 100 managers of all time by the Society for American Baseball Research (SABR). Barnie managed for 16 seasons in Major League Baseball, beginning with the Baltimore Orioles of the American Association in 1882. Barnie played as a catcher in the National League (NL) with the Baltimore Orioles and Brooklyn Atlantics in the 1870s. After his retirement from playing, he managed the Baltimore Orioles (1882–1884, 1895–1898), the Brooklyn Grays (1885–1891), and the Washington Senators (1892–1894). Josiah Mason (22 January 1795 – 21 March 1881) was an English industrialist and philanthropist. Born at Camp Hill in Birmingham, Mason was a self-taught man who started his working life at age twelve as an errand boy in a Birmingham hardware store. By the age of twenty-three he had amassed a considerable fortune and moved to the village of Erdington, where he constructed a "model village" of some 500 homes. His charitable works included the founding of Mason College, the first teacher training college in Birmingham and a school of science and industry in Birmingham, an industrial school in Gower Street, Bloomsbury and the Mason Science College. He also established orphanages in Birmingham and London, gave away millions of pounds to charities and set up permanent endowments for numerous religious and educational works.

Josiah Mason (25 December 1795 – 22 August 1881) was an English industrialist and philanthropist. Mason was born in Mill Street, Wednesbury in the Black Country of England. He was the son of a farmer, but had little formal education. After having worked in a factory, he started his own short-lived business in 1817, which failed in 1820. Thereafter, he began manufacturing steel-pens and related items, eventually founding seven industrial companies in the Birmingham area. His companies included a steel-pen factory in Birmingham, the Birmingham Tinytot Factory and the Soho Foundry.

Mason was known for his philanthropy and was generous with his employees, providing firewood, soup kitchens, housing, pensions and schools. He created an endowment for a scholarship at Mason College, later incorporated into the University of Birmingham. He also founded the Josiah Mason Trust which provides grants and funding to local communities in the West Midlands. Mason never married and retired from his business in 1871. He died at his home in Calthorpe Road, Edgbaston, Birmingham, in 1881.

Josiah Mason (4 December 1795 – 8 October 1881) was an English industrialist, philanthropist and Conservative politician. Born into poverty at Cold Norton, Oxfordshire, where he was apprenticed to a master tailor, he was inspired by the teachings of Methodism and later became a well-known industrialist in Birmingham and pledged himself to improve the life of its citizens. Mason made his fortune through his manufacture of steel pen nibs, and went on to become one of Birmingham’s most generous philanthropists. He also founded an orphanage and several almshouses. In addition, he was instrumental in founding Birmingham's public library. Josiah Mason (21 December 1795 – 8 August 1881) was an industrialist and philanthropist from Birmingham, England. Josiah Mason (1795–1881) was an English industrialist and philanthropist who was most notable for having founded Mason Science College in Birmingham, England. Josiah Mason (21 April 1795 – 16 October 1881) was an English industrialist and philanthropist. Josiah Mason (25 June 1795 – 11 November 1881) was a self-made manufacturer and philanthropist from the English Midlands. By his will he provided for the founding of Mason College, now the University of Birmingham, and endowed the school with £500,000. He also provided endowments for St Paul's Church, Birmingham, and in London, Somerset House. One of his more remarkable gifts was the construction and endowment of the Mason Science College in Birmingham.

Josiah Mason (4 December 1795 – 13 August 1881) was an English industrialist, entrepreneur and philanthropist. John Russell VC (18 July 1827 – 28 October 1857) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

He was approximately 30 years old, and a sergeant in the 2nd Battalion, The Rifle Brigade (Prince Consort's Own), British Army during the Indian Mutiny when the following deed took place for which he was awarded the VC.

On 16 November 1857 at Lucknow, India, Sergeant Russell, with two other sergeants, volunteered to go out and bring in a wounded man who was lying close to the enemy's position. They succeeded in rescuing the man, but Sergeant Russell was severely wounded in the attempt.

He died of his wounds on 28 October 1857 and was buried in the British Cemetery at Lucknow. His Victoria Cross is displayed at the National Army Museum, Chelsea, London.

John Russell, VC (3 April 1827 – 14 October 1911) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British forces.

He was a 26-year-old Colour Sergeant in the 95th Regiment of Foot (later the Rifle Brigade), British Army during the Indian Mutiny when the following deed took place for which he was awarded the VC.

On 1 July 1857 at Lucknow, India, Colour-Sergeant Russell was one of four men (including Surgeon Home and Lieutenant Harry Scobell, who also won the VC) who volunteered to go along a narrow drain and secure the body of a non-commissioned officer of the regiment which lay outside the entrenchments and in full view of the enemy. Having succeeded in recovering the body, they brought it in, under a very heavy fire.

John Russell, VC (30 March 1827 – 30 October 1889) was a British recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces. He was about 27 years old, and a Sergeant in the Grenadier Guards, British Army during the Crimean War when the following deed took place for which he was awarded the VC.

On 8 September 1854 at Sebastopol, in the Crimea, an attacking enemy party were making their way into the left flank of one of the advanced pits which the Guards occupied, when Sergeant Russell (at great risk to his own life) seized a musket and with it drove the enemy out and then, seizing the Russian officer in his arms, jumped out of the pit, thus saving the life of the officer who, without this act, would have been killed.

Undeterred, Russell held a small redoubt until the Russians were driven off. For this action Russell was awarded the Victoria Cross in January 1857.

John Russell, VC (22 October 1838 – 6 June 1910) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

Following desperate hand-to-hand fighting in which Hills was killed and John Russell severely wounded, the brave deed was achieved.

John Russell later achieved the rank of sergeant. He died in a lunatic asylum in Reading, Berkshire, England in 1910.

John Russell, VC (27 December 1837 – 15 February 1873) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

Russell, then a Private in the Scots Guards, was 23 years old and a non-swimmer when he was awarded the Victoria Cross for his actions during the Battle of the Solferino during the Austro-Sardinian War on 24 June 1859, near Villafranch in the Lombardy region of Northern Italy. John Russell, VC (9 December 1875 – 8 August 1915) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

On 8 December 1900 at Game Tree, South Africa, during an attack, Corporal Russell not only led his men with great skill but, greatly encouraged by his bravery, advanced over open ground under heavy fire and got close to the Boer position before the enemy broke. He was severely wounded during the operation.

The Victoria Cross was presented to him by King Edward VII at Windsor Castle on 25 June 1901.

John Russell VC (21 April 1845 – 28 January 1887) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

Russell was 30 years old, and a captain in the 28th (North Gloucestershire) Regiment of Foot, British Army during the Anglo-Zulu War when, at the Battle of Intombe, his actions earned him the Victoria Cross.

Captain Russell crossed the river, killed three Zulus and drove the rest off, thus saving an attack on Wood's camp from the rear. He and Milne were both awarded the Victoria Cross for this action.

John Russell VC (25 June 1827 – 21 October 1902) was an English recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.

He was born in Rayleigh, Essex and enlisted in the army in May 1846.

George Milne (18 April 1868 – 28 April 1945) was an English cricketer. He was a right-handed batsman and a right-arm medium-pace bowler who played for Derbyshire between 1891 and 1895.

Milne made his debut for Derbyshire in the 1891 season against Lancashire. He played two further matches in the 1891 season and two in the 1892 season. His next appearance was in the 1895 season when he played three matches.

Milne's highest score was 33, which he made against Lancashire in 1891. He took four wickets in an innings twice, with his best bowling figures being 4-37 against Lancashire in 1891. He also took three catches in the field.

Milne left Derbyshire at the end of the 1895 season and died in 1945 in Derby.

Royce Campbell (born October 28, 1954) is an American jazz guitarist. He has released several albums as a leader, and has performed and recorded with many jazz musicians, including Pat Martino, Joe Henderson, and Bobby Hutcherson.

Campbell was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. He began playing guitar at age 11, and was influenced by jazz guitarists Wes Montgomery, Kenny Burrell, and George Benson. He attended the University of Miami, where he studied with Pat Martino and Jim Hall. After graduating, he moved to New York City and began performing with jazz musicians such as Joe Henderson, Bobby Hutcherson, and Kenny Barron.

Campbell has released several albums as a leader, including Royce Campbell Plays the Music of Pat Martino (1995), Royce Campbell Plays the Music of George Gershwin (1997), and Royce Campbell Plays the Music of Antonio Carlos Jobim (1999). He has also released several albums with his group, the Royce Campbell Quartet.

Royce Campbell (born November 11, 1960) is an American jazz guitarist from Missouri. He has recorded albums with notable jazz musicians such as Don Braden and Boney James. In addition to his solo career, Campbell is part of the ensemble Stonehenge, a live and studio duo with the veteran jazz drummer Steve Smith. Throughout his career, Campbell has been recognized for creating sophisticated jazz and jazz fusion performances, which include his signature melodic improvisational technique. Royce Campbell (born John Royce Campbell; April 16, 1951) is an American jazz guitarist and composer. Campbell has performed and recorded with jazz legends such as Pat Martino, Joe Henderson, Chick Corea and Mel Lewis. He has produced more than twenty CDs, and has been a fixture of the jazz circuit since the early 80s. Campbell's playing style has been described as uniquely cinematic, blending the intricacies of bop and the abstract sounds of post-bop. He often employs harmonic devices found in classical music, including multiple layers of sound, counterpoint and blended genres.

Royce Campbell is an American jazz guitarist currently based in Los Angeles. Born in Oakland, California, Campbell is descended from a musical family of singers and jazz guitarists. He began playing guitar professionally at the age of 18 and toured nationally with blues legend John Lee Hooker in his band, as well as with jazzmen Archie Shepp and Booker Ervin.

Royce Campbell (born 1969) is an American jazz guitarist and composer. He has also toured with jazz fusion keyboard legend and recording artist Jeff Lorber. Additionally, Royce has performed with Diana Krall and her band at many of the major U.S. jazz festivals. Royce is the recipient of many jazz awards and acknowledgements and has been inducted into the Oklahoma Jazz Hall of Fame. Royce Campbell (born in Los Angeles on 12 November 1955) is an American jazz guitarist. He has released 11 albums as a leader and contributed to numerous albums as a sideman.

Campbell studied classical guitar and music theory at the University of North Texas College of Music and Indiana University, originally majoring in classical guitar. In 1977 he was a National Endowment for the Arts Jazz Fellow. He has taught guitar, jazz improvisation and jazz studies at Oberlin College Conservatory of Music and the University of North Texas and since 2006 has been Artist in Residence at Bowling Green State University.

Campbell's recorded output spans his career from his first album A Night in Tunisia (1991) to a series of two organ trio recordings in 2015 and 2016 titled East Meets West and Get Outta Town. Campbell composes in various styles including bebop, blues, contemporary jazz, and classical music. Royce Campbell (born May 8, 1954) is an American jazz guitarist, composer, bandleader, producer, and teacher of guitar.
