Royce Campbell (born 1954) is an American jazz guitarist. He has released about a dozen albums as a leader, but he is also known for his work with singer Tony Bennett, Bowie-inspired guitarist Adrian Belew, Grammy Award-winning vocalist Nnenna Freelon, guitarist Les Paul, trumpeter Randy Brecker, and jazz-fusion group Steps Ahead.

Campbell has a distinctive sound, blending bebop and post-bop with soulful blues playing. He emphasizes the value of developing fluency and improvising in the jazz idiom – not only learning tunes, but also understanding and freely elaborating upon the underlying harmony. His recordings strongly reflect this philosophy.

Royce Campbell (born March 14, 1956) is an American jazz guitarist. He has released 16 albums as a leader on a variety of labels. He is known for his skill as an interpreter of blues, standards, and original tunes.

Campbell was born in Athens, Georgia and began studying guitar at the age of four. He studied classical guitar at the University of Georgia and moved to New York in 1973, playing in jazz clubs throughout the city and honing his craft as a session musician. He released his debut album, Campbell Playtime, in 1986 on Capri Records. He has since released a steady stream of albums, including a 1995 collaboration with pianist Frank Kimbrough and two albums dedicated to the music of Charlie Christian (1996, 2002). Royce Campbell (born 1954) is an American jazz guitarist, composer, educator, author, and producer. Royce Campbell (born August 28, 1964) is an American jazz guitarist who works primarily in a post-bop and fusion style. He and his quintet have released seven albums, and he has composed, arranged, and produced music for over 50 television, film and CD releases.

Campbell was born in Omaha, Nebraska and grew up in Kansas City, Missouri, where he studied guitar with James Henderson, legendary guitar professor at both UMKC and the University of Kansas. Royce Campbell (born August 25, 1960) is an American jazz guitarist, composer, and producer. He has released ten albums as a leader and contributed to over 50 recordings as a sideman. Royce Campbell (born July 12, 1954) is an American jazz guitarist. Born in Columbus, Ohio, he had an early interest in jazz and immersed himself in the music of Wes Montgomery and George Benson. In 1975, he moved to New York City and began attending the New School for Social Research. During his studies, Campbell was invited to join the electric jazz–funk band Stuff, which was composed of Steve Gadd, Eric Gale, Richard Tee and Cornell Dupree, among others. He began his career as an in-demand session musician, performing with the Saturday Night Live Band, Roberta Flack, and Chaka Khan, among others. Royce Campbell (Born October 18, 1958) is an American jazz guitarist. He is best known for his work on the guitar with many acclaimed jazz artists such as John Handy, Sonny Fortune and Charlie Rouse. Royce Campbell (born May 30, 1951) is an American jazz guitarist. He began his professional career in 1971 and has recorded over sixty albums over the course of his career. Royce Campbell (born May 24, 1959) is an American jazz guitarist, composer, and music educator based in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. Cicero Hunt Lewis (November 3, 1825 – June 29, 1904) was an African American lecturer and social reformer who was born into slavery in Prince George's County, Virginia near Washington, D.C. He is considered the first African American to become a public lecturer after the American Civil War. His lectures were on topics such as self-improvement and morality, and he also spoke out against racism and advocated for black rights. He often spoke to African American audiences but also to white audiences, urging them to embrace racial equality. Lewis is best known for his lecture series in 1872 which was sponsored by the Woman's Foreign Missionary Society of the Methodist Episcopal Church. "Cicero Hunt Lewis (February 26, 1837 – April 25, 1905) was an American lawyer, jurist, and politician. He served as the 12th United States Secretary of War in the first administration of President Grover Cleveland. 

Lewis was born in Marengo County, Alabama. He began practicing law there in 1857 and was elected to the Alabama House of Representatives in 1861. He was elected to the United States House of Representatives in 1880 and served as chairman of the Committee on Territories.

Cicero Hunt Lewis (July 25, 1825 – August 24, 1904) was an American businessman and politician who served as a member of the U.S. House of Representatives for Ohio from 1881 to 1893.

Born in Chillicothe, Ohio, Lewis attended the Chillicothe Academy and then went to work as a clerk in the 'State Library of Ohio', the Secretary of State's office, from 1842 to 1846. During the American Civil War, he served as colonel in the 106th Ohio Infantry, and was badly injured in the fighting. After the war, he settled in Delaware, Ohio, where he engaged in banking, and served as mayor in 1878 and 1879.

In 1881, Lewis was elected as a Republican to the 47th Congress and served until his resignation on March 2, 1893. He was a member of the Fraternal Order of Eagles. He died in Delaware, Ohio, and was interred in Oak Grove Cemetery.

Cicero Hunt Lewis (April 10, 1825 – April 17, 1877) was an African American minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, who is best known for his sermon entitled "The Folly of Prejudice." He spoke out against the inequity of slavery as well as serving in the preaching circuit throughout central and eastern Pennsylvania, as well as New Jersey. His sermon "Folly of Prejudice" was delivered at Mother Bethel AME Church in Philadelphia.

In his sermon, Lewis spoke out against racial intolerance, and urged church members to think for themselves and to educate themselves, saying, "Remember my friends, the worst enemy to human happiness is ignorance." He also asked his congregation to "Cultivate a kind and peaceable disposition toward all mankind." Lewis's message was heard and embraced, as it was one of the first of its kind given at an African American church, and showed how Christianity could be used to fight for justice. His sermon is remembered today for its message of hope, as well as its fearless eloquence in the face of racism and injustice.

Cicero Hunt Lewis (July 3, 1825 – October 28, 1914) was an American lawyer, politician and judge who was prominent in the early history of Washington State. Born in Kentucky, he was the youngest of eleven children, and grew up in Missouri. He arrived in Steilacoom, Washington, in 1853 to serve as the county's first prosecuting attorney and was responsible for the establishment of the county's first jail. In 1855, he was elected to the Washington Territory Legislative Assembly and served as Speaker of the Assembly from 1859 to 1861. Lewis was then appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court of the Washington Territory and served until 1876, when Washington became a state. He was elected to the Washington Supreme Court in that same year and served until 1895, when he retired at age 70. Lewis was instrumental in various legislative and judicial advances in Washington, including enacting the first Washington Territorial laws, helping to establish the Washington legal code, and establishing laws that ensured fairness and equal access to justice. He also helped draft the state constitution of 1889.

Cicero Hunt Lewis (April 5, 1825 – May 10, 1904) was an American inventor and the founder of Lewis Manufacturing Company, the first African-American owned and operated business in the United States. Born in slavery in Virginia, Lewis obtained his freedom and moved to Washington, D.C., where he found work as a machinist for a contractor operating the Washington and Georgetown Railroad. In 1869, he obtained a patent for a self-acting car coupler which he developed to allow for the easier changing of streetcar cars during the days of horse-drawn streetcars. He formed Lewis Manufacturing Company in 1872 and smaller-scale manufacturing operations in Washington. The company built such items as hand-operated car couplers, railroad brakes, patent lithographs and general machinery. Lewis' car coupler was in use in many of the large cities throughout the United States, including Chicago, New York, San Francisco, and Boston.

Cicero Hunt Lewis (April 11, 1834 – May 31, 1909) was an American naturalist, taxidermist, editor, photographer, and painter. Born in Watertown, New York, Lewis developed an interest in nature during the early years of his life. He studied the flora and fauna of the Adirondack Mountains and wrote several books on his finds. In 1867, Lewis was appointed taxidermist of the newly created American Museum of Natural History in New York City. In 1902, he became the museum's first curator of vertebrate paleontology. He continued to write books and magazine articles on his experiences. Lewis was known for his artistic ability and keen eye for detail. He died in New York in 1909.

Cicero Hunt Lewis (ca.1799-1880) was an African-American abolitionist from Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. Lewis, also known as C.H. The band released four albums between 1988 and 1993, and had several hit singles, including "Seventeen" and "Headed for a Heartbreak".

Taylor left Winger in 1994 to pursue a solo career. He released his debut solo album, Another Place and Time, in 1998.

"Robert Emmett Keane (1912–1986) was an American literary critic and professor of English literature. Keane's work focused on modern poetry and playwrights, including T.S. Eliot and William Butler Yeats. He argued that a text's true import lies in the details rather than in broad generalizations or themes. Keane's writings also explored connections between literature, culture, and history.

Jack Straw (born 3 August 1946) is a British Labour Party politician who served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015. He served in the Cabinet from 1997 to 2010 under the governments of Tony Blair and Gordon Brown. Straw held two of the traditional Great Offices of State, as Home Secretary from 1997 to 2001 and Foreign Secretary from 2001 to 2006 under Blair. From 2007 to 2010 he served as Lord Chancellor and Secretary of State for Justice throughout Brown's premiership.

A former chair of the Labour Party, Straw was one of the few remaining links between the Labour governments led by Blair and Brown and the party's previous incarnation under Tony Benn, John Smith and Neil Kinnock. He is currently the President of the National Council for Civil Liberties.

Jack Straw (Jacob Straw; born 3 August 1946) is a British Labour politician who served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015. He was the first British Home Secretary to introduce a "civil liberties agenda" into the law and policy of the UK. He oversaw several reforms of the Police and Criminal Evidence Act, including the introduction of the "Custody Plus" scheme, which looked to reduce the time spent in detention by vulnerable suspects, such as young people and those with mental health problems. He also introduced the 'Safer Streets Initiative' which meant that in some areas cameras and neighbourhood wardens were used as a deterrent against crime.

Jack Straw (born 3 August 1946) is a British Labour Party politician who served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015. Jack Straw (c. 1290–1374) was an English statesman and Peasants' Revolt leader. He emerged as a prominent leader during the Peasants' Revolt of 1381 in England and is remembered as one of the leaders of the revolt who presented its demands to Richard II. Born in Clayton, Lancashire, Straw was a lawyer and tavern-keeper by trade. He was also a money-changer and law clerk, demonstrating ability, ambition and wealth and lived in a two-story house with a substantial garden. In 1381, Straw joined the leaders of a rising ideological movement among English labourers and one of the main players in the struggle of the English peasantry against feudal oppression. He advocated reforms and improvements to the lives of peasants, wanted to reassert their rights as English citizens, and led demonstrations, including one at Canterbury, where the rebels had their own "king". After the revolt, he was arrested and put to death by Richard II. His legacy can be seen today in the changes to English law and the abolishment of serfdom.

Jack Straw (born 3 August 1946) is a British Labour Party politician who served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015. A barrister by background, he began his career as a Labour politician in the 1970s, being elected as the Member of Parliament for Blackburn in 1979. In 1997, he was appointed Home Secretary and became a central figure in Tony Blair's Cabinet. He gained a reputation as a key moderniser within the Labour Party which, under Blair's leadership, had abandoned many of its traditional left-wing policies. As foreign secretary, he pressed for military action against Islamic State of Iraq and Syria (ISIS) insurgents in Iraq, and intervention in the Syrian Civil War. After Labour's defeat in the 2010 parliament, he was made Leader of the House and was also Lord High Chancellor of Great Britain, before retiring in 2015.

Jack Straw (born 3 August 1946) is a British Labour and Co-operative Party politician who served in various ministerial positions in the governments of Prime Ministers Tony Blair and Gordon Brown. He held the constituency of Blackburn in Lancashire as a Labour Member of Parliament from 1979 to 2015 and currently sits in the House of Lords.

And we've all had better weeks."

Jack Straw (born Jacob Straw; 3 August 1946) is a British Labour and Co-operative politician who served as the Member of Parliament for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015 and as the Lord High Chancellor of Great Britain from 2007 to 2010. Jack Straw (born 31 August 1946) is a British politician who served as Member of Parliament (MP) for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015 and was a senior Cabinet minister in the Labour governments of Tony Blair and Gordon Brown. Jack Straw (born Jacob Ernest Straw, 3 August 1946) is a British politician who served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015. Jack Straw (c. 1290 – 23 June 1244) was an English politician, landowner and rebel. He rose to prominence while leading the crowd during the Peasants' Revolt of 1381 in England. His father, Walter Straw, was a small landowner in the village of Buckhurst Hill in the parish of Chipping Ongar, Essex. He is believed to have been one of the leaders of the peasants when they assembled at Blackheath, outside London, on 15 June 1381. He demanded that the government of King Richard II hand over to him the Charter of Liberties, which had been issued by King Henry III in 1217. The king rejected the demands, and the peasants marched towards London, gathering support along the way. Jack Straw was captured in the Tower of London and executed on 23 June 1244, along with his friend, Wat Tyler.

Jack Straw (born 3 August 1946) is a British Labour Party politician who served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015. Jack Straw (born 3 August 1946) is a British Labour Party politician who served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Blackburn from 1979 to 2015. Jack Straw (c. 1290 – 4 April 1312) was an English political leader, one of the leaders of the Peasants' Revolt of 1381. He is remembered as a folk hero, emblematic of the struggle between ordinary people and those in power. 

Jack Straw was originally from the village of Cage near St Albans in Hertfordshire. He led a group of peasants who marched into London in 1381, protesting against the high taxes and repressive measures of King Richard II's government. Straw spoke to the crowd, exhorting them to use force to gain their rights, which by then had included demands for the abolition of serfdom. When their demands were rejected, the peasants occupied part of London and were eventually killed by the Mayor of London's soldiers. 

Jack Straw has become a symbol of the struggle of ordinary people against those in power, and the Peasants' Revolt. He has been depicted in literature, plays and films, and statues of him have been erected in London and Hertfordshire.

Jack Straw (c. 1290–1321) was an English rebel and leader during the Peasants' Revolt of 1381, who helped lead the attack on London. He was related to the Sible Hedingham Straw family of Essex, and was a foundling – according to the legend of his life, his mother set him afloat on a sheaf of straw in a greenfield in Essex. He was educated by nuns, was popular among the peasantry, and helped lead the protest against the hated poll tax. He also called for greater equality for all classes of people and for the reform of the justice system. He joined with Wat Tyler in leading the rebels' march on London and was present at the meeting with King Richard II. After Tyler's death, Straw became the leader of the group, but was eventually captured and executed by the King's forces.

Jack Straw (or Jerry Straw) is an English folksong and Morris dance tune, which dates from the mid-18th century and is still performed today. It tells, in narrative form, the story of a man called Jack Straw (or Jerry Straw), who, for reasons not made clear, makes a pact with the devil. He agrees to give his soul to the devil, who then takes him on a tour of the world and shows him places of great joy and places of great sorrow. Jack Straw then chooses to give up his life in exchange for a peaceful death. 

The song was first published in 1784 by William Hone in his collection The Every Day Book. It appears in Robert Bell's collection of songs and the well-known nineteenth century collections of Baring-Gould and Sharp. 

The song has been widely performed over the years with many artists and folk singers recording it, including the Watersons, Bob Dylan, Pentangle and Steeleye Span. In modern times, it has become something of a standard tune amongst Morris dance sides.

main_passage: Donald Alexander Mackinnon (1873–1936) was a Scottish-born Canadian lawyer, politician, and judge. He was born in Inverness, Scotland, and immigrated to Canada in 1891. He was called to the bar in Ontario in 1898 and practised law in Toronto. He was elected to the House of Commons of Canada in 1904 as a Liberal Member of Parliament for Toronto Centre. He was re-elected in 1908 and 1911. In 1912, he was appointed to the Supreme Court of Ontario, where he served until his death in 1936. He was a strong advocate for the rights of women and children, and was instrumental in the passage of the Ontario Married Women's Property Act of 1914. He was also a strong supporter of the temperance movement.

Donald Alexander Mackinnon (February 18, 1882 – June 24, 1954) was a Scottish-born Canadian businessman, politician and philanthropist. Born in Piershill, Scotland, Mackinnon emigrated to Canada at the age of three, settling in Fort Rouge, Manitoba with his family. He attended the Winnipeg Normal School and earned a teaching certificate in 1907. From 1908 to 1910, he worked in a variety of teaching jobs in British Columbia before taking a job as assistant secretary of the Vancouver Boilermakers' Union in 1911. In 1912, he formed a partnership with his brother, William, to manufacture pressure vessels. The company was sold in 1945.

Mackinnon was also a prominent political and civic leader. In the 1930s, he was elected as a member of the provincial Liberal Party of British Columbia in the provincial legislature. sentences: Donald Alexander Mackinnon (1873–1936) was a Scottish-born Canadian lawyer, politician, and judge.
He was born in Inverness, Scotland, and immigrated to Canada in 1891.
He was called to the bar in Ontario in 1898 and practised law in Toronto.

He was re-elected in 1908 and 1911.

He was also a strong supporter of the temperance movement.

Heinz Christian Pander (1794–1865) was a German anatomist and embryologist who was born in Riga, Latvia. He studied medicine at the University of Dorpat and later at the University of Berlin. He was a professor of anatomy at the University of Dorpat from 1820 to 1845.

Pander is best known for his work on the development of chick embryos, which he described in his 1817 book Beiträge zur Entwickelungsgeschichte des Hühnereies (Contributions to the Developmental History of the Chicken Egg). He was the first to describe the three germ layers of the embryo, the ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm, and he was the first to describe the notochord. He also described the development of the neural tube, the formation of the amniotic cavity, and the development of the heart and blood vessels. He was the first to describe the process of gastrulation in chick embryos.

He proposed the cell lineage theory (also known as the Pander model), which is broadly accepted as the founding idea of modern developmental biology. His theory inspired many later layers of study on the morphogenesis of embryos.

Heinz Christian Pander (1794–1865) was a Russian zoologist and anatomist. He was born in the small village of Nienburg, Livonia, to a family of German origin. He studied medicine and natural history in Dorpat and then joined the Russian Imperial Academy of Sciences in Saint Petersburg, where he worked as an assistant zoologist and later a lecturer at the medical faculty of the University of Dorpat. Heinz Christian Pander (18 January 1808 – 10 February 1865) was a German scientist and anatomist who was famous for his studies in human embryology. In 1817, he enrolled at the University of Bonn to study medicine, and after receiving his degree in 1825, he became a professor at the University of Moscow in 1826. He then served as Professor of Anatomy and Physiology in Halle from 1840 until his death in 1865. Pander is credited with making major advances in the understanding of human embryology, particuarly in the area of organogenesis. In 1817 and 1827, he discovered two separate pairs of locomotory and sensory organs in the vertebrates. He also published detailed descriptions of the development of human embryos up to the 10th week of gestation. Pander was remarkable for his detailed research at a time when most researchers were only just beginning to explore the biology of human embryology. His work was influential to subsequent generations of embryologists who developed the modern understanding of human development.

Heinz Christian Pander (12 August 1794, Riga – 17 December 1865, Dorpat) was a Baltic German embryologist and anatomist. He is credited with being the first to describe and illustrate the notochord, a rudimentary skeletal rod found in the embryos of all chordates, and his work helped lay the foundation for the science of modern embryology. Pander studied medicine at the universities of Königsberg and Dorpat, graduating from the latter in 1815, after which he served as a surgeon in the Russian Army during the Napoleonic wars.

Heinz Christian Pander (18 May 1846 – 18 December 1927) was a Baltic German biologist. He is credited with the discovery of germ-layer theory in 1817, which is a concept of embryology that states that specialized cells arise from three primary layers of embryonic tissue: ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm. This concept served as the foundation for further progress in the field. Pander was a student of Karl Ernst von Baer at the University of Dorpat, in what is now Tartu, Estonia. Pander's works, including his doctoral dissertation on chick embryology and Anleitung zur Entwickelungsgeschichte des Huhnes, were based on Baer's early observations and published in 1817.

Heinz Christian Pander (1794–1865) was a Baltic German embryologist who was the first to describe the process of cellular differentiation in chick embryos. He proposed that development of tissues is preceded and accompanied by changes in their cells. He concluded that development in all animals, from the very simplest to humans, is a consequence of the progressive differentiation of cells. He wrote the first comprehensive anatomy of the chick embryo. Pander's most influential publication was his 1817 dissertation Über Entwickelungsgeschichte der Thiere ("On the Developmental History of Animals"). His work established the use of the chick embryo in studies of vertebrate development.

Pander was born in Krāslava (Krausslau), Courland into a noble family in St. Petersburg, Russia. He studied at the universities of Moscow, Jena and Dorpat. In 1820, he was appointed professor of anatomy and embryology at the University of Dorpat, where he remained for 45 years. His studies on embryology resulted in the publication of his three volume monograph Der Entwickelungsges

Heinz Christian Pander (1794–1865) was a German anatomist and zoologist, the son of the doctor of theology Johann Paul Pander and Ernstine (von Clausen). Pander was born in the city of Memel in Prussia on 15 March 1794, and educated at the University of Königsberg, receiving his Doctorate of Medicine and Surgery in 1816. Pander spent most of his scientific career as a professor and lecturer at the university, teaching anatomy, zoology, and comparative anatomy. He conducted research on the developmental stages of insects and birds, investigating through micro-dissection and illustration. Pander is notably known for discovering lymphatic glands, the formation of the chick embryo, and embryonic development of insect species. He is credited with writing the first Atlas of Chick Embryos in 1817, which made him an influential figure in embryology. Pander also published a four-volume work, Der Bau und die Bildungsgeschichte des Mensehen Eies, which was translated into English, French, and Italian. He received numerous awards and acknowledgements

Heinz Christian Pander (18 July 1813 – 8 February 1865) was a Baltic German anatomist born in Mitau (Jelgava), Courland (present-day Latvia). He studied at the universities of Dorpat and Berlin, receiving his doctorate at Dorpat in 1833.

In 1834 Pander was invited by the Russian surgeon Nikolay Pirogov to become professor of anatomy at the newly-founded Imperial Medical and Surgical Academy in St. Petersburg, a post he held until 1865. While there, he undertook research associated with foetal development, hormone and muscle tissue. His name is associated with the eponymous "Pander's organization", a developmental step occurring in the first day of fertilization where primitive streak formation takes place. Pander was the first to distinguish and name the outer cell layer (ectoderm), the middle cell layer (mesoderm) and the inner cell layer (endoderm) of the newly-formed embryo, a concept of importance in vertebrate embryology. Other recognized achievements include the discovery of vitelline and allantoic vessels as well

Heinz Christian Pander (July 7, 1794 – August 24, 1865) was a Baltic German biologist from the Governorate of Livonia who developed the theory of cell lineage. He is one of the most important early scientists of comparative anatomy, and one of the three pioneers of embryology, along with Theodor Schwann and Karl Ernst von Baer. Pander was the first to correctly describe chick embryogenesis, which he studied intensively; he is, therefore, sometimes called the founder of modern embryology.

Pander was born in the village of Baldone, near Riga in what is now Latvia. He studied medicine and theology at the University of Dorpat (Tartu) and Jena, although he spent much of his earlier life as a tutor and schoolmaster. In 1817 he founded a private school in Tartu, which he ran until 1840, when he became an anatomy professor at the same university. From 1843 to 1864, he served as the rector of the University of Tartu. He died in Tartu in 1865.

Heinz Christian Pander (2 April 1813 – 24 August 1865) was a German anatomist and zoologist from Livonia. He is notable for his early investigation into the development of the chicken embryo, which helped to lay the groundwork for modern embryology. Pander made his earliest contributions to embryology between 1817 and 1841, publishing papers on the segmentation of eggs and the development of the chick embryo in the journal Novi Commentarii Academiae Scientiarum Imperialis Petropolitanae. In 1864, he published a seminal essay, Beiträge zur Entwicklungsgeschichte des Hünenhuhnes (Contributions on the Developmental History of the Chicken), summarizing his previous work and making numerous observations on the development of the chick. Pander was the first to describe the three germ layers of the early embryo: the ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm. His contributions to embryology would eventually be followed up upon by two of his students, Karl Ernst von Baer and Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hil

Heinz Christian Pander (1794–1865) was a Baltic German zoologist who is remembered for his contributions to the understanding of embryonic development. He was born in Königsberg and later studied natural sciences at the universities of Königsberg and Berlin.

Pander was one of the earliest embryologists to observe and describe different stages of chick development and he is credited with the discovery of "germ layers," when he identified three distinct layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm) in developing chick embryos. This discovery was a major step forward in the understanding of the development of all animals, including humans. Pander also contributed to the understanding of the ancestry of a variety of animals and described the first fossil footprints of dinosaurs. His work was influential on the development of evolutionary theories.

Heinz Christian Pander (1794–1865) was a Baltic German biologist and embryologist who made significant contributions to the development of evolutionary theory. He was influenced by the thoughts of Ludimar Hermann and Leopold von Buch, who both proposed that the development of animals was the result of evolutionary change. Pander worked mainly on the development of chicken embryos, and was the first to describe the three germ layers of the embryo (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm). He identified and described the notochord, a flexible rod-like structure found in chordates, which helps the embryo deal with changes in body shape and is essential for the development of a vertebrate skeleton. He also observed the presence of an amnionic sac in birds, which is an essential part of the development of a verterbrate embryo. His work influenced the evolutionary writings of Charles Darwin and August Weismann.

Heinz Christian Pander (18 July 1813 – 27 December 1853) was a German anatomist and one of the earliest figures in the fields of embryology and teratology, the study of malformations. He was the first to describe the embryonic origin of the muscles of the tongue and the first to recognize the three germ layers (endoderm, mesoderm, and ectoderm). He made substantial contributions to the understanding of chick development and the development of the human brain. Pander is best known for the discovery of Pander's chordamesoderm, a layer of cells located in the blastoderm at the upper pole of the developing chick, which he hypothesized played a role in the formation of germ layers. He was also the first to describe embryonic membranes as embryonic protective and nutritive accessories.

Heinz Christian Pander (February 1, 1794 – October 14, 1865) was a Baltic German biologist, embryologist and zoologist who studied the development of chick embryos. He is best known for his discovery that the chick embryo grows from three germ layers, and is thus an early example of the theory of epigenesis, which contributed to the acceptance of the theory of evolution by natural selection. He also described what is now known as Pander's drosophila, an organism used in classical genetics experiments. Pander is considered by some to be the founder of modern embryology, and his work has been cited repeatedly by other scientists.

Heinz Christian Pander (1794–1865) was a Baltic German embryologist whose path-breaking research on chick embryology earned him international recognition. He was the first to describe the notochord and teach its significance in vertebrate embryology. Drawing on the work of Karl Ernst von Baer, he embarked on a series of experiments involving the removal and dissection of chick embryos at different developmental stages. Together, he and von Baer formulated the germ layer theory of organ formation, which describes embryological development in terms of the transformation of a single embryonic layer. His 1817 treatise, Beiträge zur Entwickelungsgeschichte der Thiere, was the ﬁrst full-length book devoted to comparative embryology, and continues to serve as an important foundation for our current understanding of vertebrate development.

Heinz Christian Pander (31 December 1794 – 20 December 1865) was a German scientist who studied various fields of the life sciences, such as entomology, anatomy, embryology, and anatomy.

He was born in Fischhausen in the former German province of East Prussia. He attended the University of Königsberg and obtained his PhD in medicine in 1820. He conducted his first scientific studies under Karl Ernst von Baer between 1822 and 1824.

During the next three decades, Pander conducted extensive research. He specialized in insect anatomy, and investigated the development of insect embryos, and the development and structure of their brains. He was the first scientist to develop a comprehensive system for the classification of arthropods. He also investigated the formation of limbs and hearts, as well as the anatomy of birds and mammals.

Pander’s observations and theories, often published in a number of important scientific journals and books, had a great influence on the development of evolutionary theory. His influence was high enough that he was elected a member of the Berlin Academy of Sciences in 1832.

Heinz Christian Pander (2 August 1813 – 25 February 1865) was a German anatomist and embryologist. He studied medicine, surgery and obstetrics in Berlin and obtained his doctorate in 1835. He worked as an assistant at the Royal College of Medicine and Surgery, headed by Johann Friedrich Meckel the Younger. Pander was known for his contributions to human embryology, including the first detailed descriptions of human embryonic and fetal development and the influence of maternal factors on embryonic development. He also developed a method of staining embryonic tissue, which allowed him to study the finer details of cell structure and embryogenesis. Pander's contributions to embryology and histology allow him to be recognised as a founder of modern embryology and cell biology.

Heinz Christian Pander (1893–1973) was a German zoologist and evolutionary theorist who advanced the concept of mutation in the theory of evolution. He is primarily remembered for his description of first mutant in insects in 1910.

The discovery of the first mutant was a crucial step in Pander's development of a new evolutionary theory. He advocated for a process he called "Mutation Theory," arguing that rather than species evolving through the selection of advantageous characteristics, organisms could be transformed through large concentration of spontaneous mutations. Pander's views were controversial in their time, but eventually became widely accepted and had a significant influence on evolutionary biology.

Heinz Christian Pander (29 July 1813 – 18 October 1865) was a German anatomist, zoologist, and biologist who is best known for his contributions to the development of embryology. He was the first to describe the embryonic development of the chick as well as the epithelium of the esophagus, the liver diverticula, and the umbilical vessels of the human placenta.

Heinz Christian Pander (20 August 1794 – 17 January 1865) was a German professor of zoology.

He is best known as the father of comparative embryology and as the founder of modern embryology. He developed the concept of germ layers, which are now the basis of all modern studies of gastrulation, and argued that changes in development could be used to trace the evolution of animal forms.

Pander's research was largely confined to arthropods and chordates, although he studied a wide range of organisms and made significant contributions to the study of fossil forms. Elisha Brown Jr. (1776–1852) was an American farmer, soldier, and politician from Rhode Island. He was a veteran of the American Revolutionary War, and served in the Rhode Island General Assembly. He was a member of the Rhode Island Society of the Cincinnati.

Brown was born in 1776 in South Kingstown, Rhode Island. He was the son of Elisha Brown Sr. and Mary (Tillinghast) Brown. He was a farmer and a soldier in the American Revolutionary War. He served in the Rhode Island General Assembly from 1809 to 1810. He was a member of the Rhode Island Society of the Cincinnati.

Brown died in 1852 in South Kingstown, Rhode Island. He is buried in the Brown Family Cemetery in South Kingstown.

Elisha Brown (1721-1787) was an American patriot and soldier in the Revolutionary War. He was born in Swansea, Massachusetts to William Brown and Mary Thacher.

In 1776, Brown volunteered his service to the Continental Army and was given the rank of sergeant. He participated in the Siege of Boston and the Battle of Bunker Hill. He was the first to cross the bridge over the Schuylkill River to enter Philadelphia during the Continental Army's march to Delaware. Brown was also present for the New York campaign and in the Battle of Long Island.

After the war, Brown was awarded the Congressional Badge of Military Merit for his service. He was one of only six men to receive this honor, which was later renamed the Purple Heart. He died in Swansea, Massachusetts in 1787.

He is remembered for his heroics in the Revolution and his posthumous award of the Purple Heart.

Elisha Brown (1775-1843) was an African American religious leader and an early convert to Mormonism. He was born in Maine, and his family moved to Pennsylvania sometime in the 1790s. Though he was literate, there are no records of any formal education. He later moved to Palmyra, New York, where he and his wife, Nabby, raised six children.

Brown was one of the earliest converts to Mormonism, and was baptized in August 1832 by Joseph Smith. He was later ordained an elder in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. He was an evangelist and conducted early church services in Pennsylvania, New York and Ohio. He followed Joseph Smith to Kirtland, Ohio and eventually moved to Missouri, where he stayed until the church was driven out of the state. Brown then moved with the members of the church to Nauvoo, Illinois and later, as a loyal and devoted follower, joined the pioneers of the vanguard company in their march across the plains to Utah. 

In the Salt Lake Valley, Brown continued to be an evangelist, preaching and teaching in his later

Elisha Brown Jr. (October 30, 1819 – April 28, 1909) was an American industrialist and philanthropist. He was the co-founder of the Eagle Lock Company, one of the world's leading producers of fine door locks and hardware. Brown became wealthy and influential, and used his wealth to help those in need, including the poor and the sick. He also gave generously to numerous charities and educational institutions, including Trinity College (now Trinity University), and he was a trustee of Brown University from 1868 until his death.

Elisha Brown (January 17, 1841 – November 1, 1896) was an African-American abolitionist and Civil War veteran, best known for being the second person to be granted freedom in the state of New Jersey by Abraham Lincoln on January 11, 1863. Born as a slave, he had long been involved in the abolishment of slavery. He was an important member of the abolitionist movement and widely respected for his work. For example, he was influential in the enlistment of African-Americans in the Union army. He was also the first black leader to be allowed to appear in public in the state of New Jersey. After the Civil War he continued to contribute to the advancement of African-Americans by incorporating black voice in recreation and literature, and protesting discrimination and segregation. He was a Freemason and an advocate of temperance. In 1896, he died at the age of 55 in Plainfield, New Jersey.

Elisha Brown Jr. (March 15, 1772 – August 8, 1852) was an American Presbyterianteacher,  minister, missionary and social reformer who was active in the early years of the United States. He is best known for his relentless efforts to improve the quality of education in the United States during the nineteenth century.

Brown was born in Jersey City, New Jersey. He attended Princeton University, where he studied theology and was ordained a Presbyterian minister in 1798. He served as the pastor of several churches in the New York and New Jersey areas before deciding to travel the country preaching and educating.

He was an early advocate for public education and founded the Pennington Seminary in New Jersey. He spoke out against discrimination, inequality and slavery and was actively involved in abolitionist and temperance movements. Brown also wrote several books and articles on education, slavery and Christianity. He died in 1852 and is buried in Pennington, New Jersey.

Elisha Brown (1746–1820) was an American slave who became a leader of the important and successful Loyalist (British loyalist) uprising during the American Revolution in South Carolina. Born free in Georgia, he was subsequently enslaved by a South Carolina master and held for life on his plantation. In 1781, Brown seized the opportunity to free himself when British forces invaded Charleston, South Carolina. He joined their efforts and became a leader in a forced uprising of enslaved people against local Patriot (American) forces. After providing a critically important service to the British commander in Charleston, General Alexander Leslie, he was freed and granted a driver's patent, allowing him to travel freely throughout the colony and to negotiate with the British forces. Brown's skill as a leader and his influence on the British forces led to the successful evacuation of over 1,800 slaves in South Carolina and Georgia before the end of the war. He then returned to his plantation, where he worked as a trusted overseer, and later served as an ambassador to the British government in negotiations concerning the freed slaves.

Elisha Brown (1758-1830) was an American soldier and purchaser of enslaved people, best known for his purchase of five who later became known as the Putnam Troop. Born in Connecticut, he served readily in the Revolutionary War. He settled in Fairfield, Connecticut and became successful in business and real estate ownership as well as active in local government. In 1817, Brown purchased five enslaved children—John, James, Elisha, Mary, and Peter—for $500. He took them to Dummerston, Vermont, to work on his 13,600-acre property. Brown subsequently moved the family to Putnam, Connecticut. 

In recognition of the five who became known as the Putnam Troop, Brown received the Congressional Gold Medal in 2014. He and the five emancipated by him are honored by the Congressional Gold Medal, which was awarded posthumously.

Elisha Brown (1776-1843) was an African American preacher and abolitionist active during the early 19th century in the Northeastern United States. Born in Massachusetts in 1776, he moved to Rhode Island as a youth and there became a Baptist minister, preaching against slavery from a prohibitively fiery pulpit. He publicly addressed anti-slavery societies and co-founded the African Union Society in Providence, Rhode Island. He sought to build a more equitable society, becoming among the first African Americans to organize support for a better life for his brethren. He was a major force in the area for a number of years until his death in 1843.

"Elisha Brown Jr. (November 1, 1776 – March 25, 1852) was an American Baptist minister and abolitionist who was often referred to as the "father of the anti-slavery cause". He was born in Scituate, Massachusetts, and after completing his education and being ordained as a minister, he moved to Providence, Rhode Island, where he joined the original Ecclesia Organization and began to preach against the evils of slavery. He helped found the Rhode Island Anti-Slavery Society and the Providence Anti-Slavery Alliance. He was also a strong supporter of the Underground Railroad, and helped thousands of slaves escape to freedom. 

Brown was also a staunch proponent of education for African-Americans, and established several schools for this purpose. He was also highly influential in advocating for the abolition of slavery in the United States. He died in 1852, and was buried in a cemetery in Providence, Rhode Island."

Elisha Brown (1764-1820) was an early American industrialist and capitalist. He was born in Hebron, Connecticut and is most famously known as a partner in the first cotton mill in the United States. He and his partner, an industrialist named Samuel Slater, opened the mill in Pawtucket, Rhode Island in 1790. Brown had an extensive knowledge of machinery and cotton manufacturing, which he gained through his father, who was a cloth maker. Brown's business sense and keen investment strategy made him a successful entrepreneur, and he quickly recognized the potential of the textile industry. He invested in many mills in the northern states, as well as industrial mills in England. He owned stock in several factories and was a major shareholder in several of them. Brown was also influential in the development of the nation's early banking industry.

Elisha Brown Jr. (1817-1893) was an African-American abolitionist and businessman who made a significant contribution to the struggle against slavery in the United States. Born in Connecticut, he moved to Providence, Rhode Island in 1838 to pursue business opportunities. In Providence, he became active in the struggle to end slavery and became a significant contributor to the Underground Railroad. Brown worked as a tailor, hat maker, and merchant, and opened his own shop in 1860. He was an active member of the abolitionist movement, helping to shelter freedom seekers from the south, raising funds, and writing frequent letters to newspapers and government officials that sought to avert injustices against African Americans. He became a close friend of prominent abolitionist, editor, and speaker Frederick Douglass. Brown was an advocate for the education of African American children, and served as president of the Plainfield Educational Society. He died of pneumonia in 1893.

Elisha Brown Jr. (March 12, 1775 – March 24, 1836) was an African-American abolitionist, public speaker, and civil rights advocate. He was a prominent figure in pre-Civil War New York City. Born in Spring Valley, Orange County, New York, Brown was the son of freedman Elisha Sr. and Esther Brown. He was educated at a local Quaker school in Suffern, New York.

Brown became a leader in the early African-American abolitionist movement and was an advisor to William Lloyd Garrison's New York Anti-Slavery Society. He lectured widely on the evils of slavery and used his platform to argue for immediate, radical emancipation for African-Americans. He worked to organize local anti-slavery societies and developed networks of support for escaped captives on the Underground Railroad.

As his prominence in the anti-slavery movement grew, Brown was elected in 1833 as the first African-American member of the New York State Assembly. He used his position to advocate on behalf of poor and enslaved African-Americans, including introducing legislation to abolish capital punishment and calling

"Elisha Brown (1806–1879) was an African-American businessman and activist in the 19th-century United States. He was born into slavery in Stafford County, Virginia, but purchased his freedom and that of his family in 1830, making him one of the earliest freed African Americans in the United States. In 1832, Brown purchased 32 acres of land in Salem, Ohio, a largely Quaker community. He set up a brick manufacturing business, one of the first of its kind in the state.

Brown was an active supporter of the abolitionist movement. He opened his home to fugitive slaves on the Underground Railroad and also provided food, clothing and financial assistance to those seeking freedom. He participated in a number of Negro conventions, including the first Colored National Convention in Cleveland in 1848, where he advocated for free public education for African Americans. He continued to own and operate his brick manufacturing business until the end of his life. Brown was an important figure in the history of African American emancipation."

Elisha Brown, Jr. (July 15, 1785 – March 21, 1837) was an American banker and politician from Bristol, Rhode Island. He was a member of the Rhode Island House of Representatives from Bristol from 1811 to 1813, and from Bristol and Barrington from 1815 to 1817. He served as Speaker of the House from 1815 to 1817. Brown was a President of the Mechanics Savings Bank, formed in Providence in 1828, and served as a member and president of the State Senate in 1833 and 1834. He was a member of the Rhode Island Senate from Bristol in 1825, and a member of the Governor's Council in 1832, 1833, and 1834.

Elisha Brown (1744-1830) was an American Revolutionary War patriot who persisted in the fight for liberty and self-government in the face of threats and violence. Born in Hunterdon County, New Jersey, he served in the militia during the war. He was a Sergeant in Captain Samuel Geary's Company of Militia. As a Lieutenant in Captain John Reading's company, he was present at the battles of Trenton and Princeton.

In 1778, Elisha took up arms in militia as a private in Captain Christopher Steel's Company, and was promoted to Sergeant 12 days later. That same year, he volunteered in the New Jersey Light Horse Regiment and became a Lieutenant in The Governor's Troop of Light Dragoons under the command of Colonel Joseph Phillips. He was on active duty through the end of 1780, during which time he fought in some of the most important battles of the Revolution. 

Brown was an active and vocal proponent of New Jersey's nascent independence, speaking out at county meetings and gatherings, and even serving in the state legislature. He was also a successful farmer and livestock breeder and a popular civic

Elisha Brown (1802–1879) was an American civil rights leader who led the effort to gain civil rights for African Americans in the state of Pennsylvania. Brown was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, on February 15, 1802, to Emanuel and Elizabeth Brown, both of whom were formerly enslaved in Delaware. He grew up in a prosperous and influential family, receiving an excellent education. While in school, he established an African-American debating association. 

Brown's parents encouraged him to pursue a career in the ministry, and so he attended the Philadelphia Divinity School and was ordained a minister in 1828. He was later appointed pastor at the Bethel African Episcopal Church in Philadelphia, which he held for forty-two years. Brown was also active in the Pennsylvania Anti-Slavery Society, and through his organization of debates and rallies, he worked to garner support for the anti-slavery movement. He also helped to form one of the earliest African-American debate clubs in Philadelphia that focused on enacting civil rights and equal opportunities for African Americans.

Elisha Brown (1753 – c. 1803) was a Quaker rancher and cattle driver who served in the Texas Revolution. Born in 1753 in Pennsylvania, he was taken to East Texas in 1828 by his four sons and their families. Brown acquired several leagues (6,436 acres) of land in present-day Leon and Madison counties, located near the current cities of Centerville and Normangee, Texas. A devout Christian, Elisha and his sons helped lay the foundation for the development of the region, building churches, schools, and businesses. In 1834, Brown and his sons assisted Stephen F. Austin leading the famed "Old Three Hundred" settlers into the region.

In 1835, Brown and his sons joined in the Texas Revolution, helping Sam Houston defend against the Mexican army. Elisha commanded a company of Texan rangers, and he and his sons provided provisions for the Texans. After his courageous actions aiding the revolutionary effort, Brown was granted 640 acres for his service. In 1837 Elisha Brown and his sons settled on their homesteads in Leon County.

Elisha Brown Jr. (1815–1891) was an African American abolitionist, preacher, and civil rights leader. He was born in Georgia and became a Baptist preacher by the age of 17, serving churches in South Carolina, Virginia, and Maryland.

Brown was an active member of the Underground Railroad and is credited with helping thousands of escaped slaves make their way North. He spoke and wrote frequently on behalf of the abolitionist cause, and advocated for educational opportunities for African Americans. During the Civil War, he raised troops for the Union and helped recruit and provision black soldiers.

After the war, he devoted his time to advocating and agitating for civil rights. Brown was an early leader of the National Public School Association, which sought to develop public educational institutions for African Americans. He had a keen interest in education, co-founding the Hampton Normal and Agricultural Institute for Blacks in 1870 (now Hampton University). He was also a leader of the Freedmen's Bureau, which assisted freedmen in the transition from slavery to freedom. Brown died in 1891 in Washington, D.C.

Elisha Brown (1803–1862) was an American slave and freedom fighter who gained prominence as a leader of the Underground Railroad in Maryland. Born enslaved in Washington, D.C., he escaped and was on the run for two years until he reached Takoma Park, Maryland. From there, he operated a station on the Underground Railroad, helping over 200 fugitive slaves make the perilous journey to freedom. He risked his safety to assist others, being arrested on numerous occasions and spending time in jail. Despite the danger, Brown helped slaves escape, often taking them as far as his home in Pennsylvania when he made the journey with them. After his own emancipation, Elisha Brown continued to use his knowledge of the Underground Railroad to help others gain their freedom. He worked with prominent African Americans of the day, such as Harriet Tubman and Robert Purvis, and was later honored with a gravestone in his name.

Elisha Brown Jr. (March 23, 1736 – August 12, 1803) was a patriot of the American Revolution and a signer of the United States Constitution from Rhode Island. He was born in Scituate, Rhode Island, the son of Elisha Brown, Sr., and Abigail (Lewis) Brown. After serving in the Rhode Island General Assembly, he was chosen in 1779 to be one of the five inaugural members of the Continental Board of War. In 1780, Brown was elected as an officer of the Rhode Island militia in lieu of General Daniel Hitchcock and was assigned to command the troops occupying Newport. He remained in this position until the town was evacuated in 1781. In 1782, Brown was unanimously elected to the Continental Congress, and in 1783 he signed the Treaty of Paris. He also served his state as a state senator and a federal judge. 

He died in Providence, Rhode Island in 1803.

John Holman (1919–2006) was a British chemist who made significant contributions to the field of physical organic chemistry. He was a professor at the University of Manchester from 1956 to 1984.

Holman's research focused on the mechanisms of organic reactions, particularly those involving radicals. He developed the concept of the "Holman Pathway" to describe the reaction of radicals with other molecules. He also developed the "Holman Rule" to explain the relative reactivity of different radicals.

Holman was a Fellow of the Royal Society and a recipient of the Davy Medal. He was also awarded the Royal Medal of the Royal Society in 1975. He was a member of the editorial board of the Journal of the Chemical Society and the Journal of Organic Chemistry.
sample_passages: John Holman (1917–1998) was a British organic chemist and professor who was a pioneering practitioner and advocate of organic synthesis. He spent most of his career at the University of Manchester, where he was appointed professor in 1965. His research specialisms was in practical organic synthesis and stereochemistry. In the 1950s, he developed a number of new methods for synthesising a range of molecules, including steroids and other heterocyclic compounds. He also wrote three books on organic synthesis, which are still referenced today. Holman's other noteworthy achievements include establishing the National Stereochemical Center in Manchester and working with the Nobel Prize winner, Dorothy Hodgkin. He was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1978. During his career, Holman received many awards, including the SAC Silver Medal, the Corday-Morgan Medal in 1966, the Ciba Medal and the Longstaff Medal.

John Holman (17 December 1950 – 1 December 2016) was a British chemist and a professor of physical chemistry at the University of Manchester. He was an internationally recognised expert in protein crystallography and a leading authority on the chemical basis of the structure and stability of proteins.

Holman was born in the East End district of London in 1950. After getting his degree from Oxford University, he obtained his doctorate from London University in 1977.

Holman taught organic chemistry and physical chemistry for 17 years before becoming Professor of Physical Chemistry at Manchester in 1994. He has written four books and over 200 research papers, and has been a pioneer in the fields of Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, electron microscopy and thermodynamics. He has been widely referenced in the scientific literature and received numerous awards, from the Royal Society and other organisations, for his contribution to science.

Holman died on 1 December 2016 and is remembered as a generous mentor and an inspiring teacher.

John Holman (1941-2009) was an American academic chemist who served on the faculty of both the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and North Carolina State University. Holman was considered one of the foremost experts in physical organic chemistry, and his research focused on free radical chemistry, the development of new synthetic routes and the chemistry of natural products. He was editor for the Journal of Organic Chemistry for six years and president of the American Chemical Society in 1988. Holman also served as the associate provost for research and public service from 2000-2004 at NC State. Holman died at the age of 68 in Raleigh, North Carolina in 2009.

John Holman (born 9 May 1924) is an British chemist and academic. He was the Gladys American Professor of Physical Chemistry at the University of Manchester Institute of Science and Technology (UMIST) from 1973 to 1989, and held the position of Head of the Department of Chemistry there between 1973 and 1989. He is noted for his works on the thermochemistry, photochemistry and dynamics of molecules, and he has co-authored several books with other researchers, including Photochemistry and Photochemical Reactors (1964) and The Dynamics and Thermochemistry of Reactive Systems (1986). Holman was awarded the Davy Medal in 1964 and the Kohn Award of the Faraday Society in 1976. He was elected as a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1979 and received their Royal Medal in 1987.

John Holman (8 May 1947 – 14 October 2020) was an English scientist and Professor of Chemistry at the University of York. He was known for his work in physical and theoretical polymer chemistry, which attracted attention from both the chemical and polymer communities.

He obtained an M.Sc. in Theory in Polymer Physics from the University of Manchester in 1972 and his PhD in 1977, then at the same University. From 1977 to 1979 he was a research associate (visiting) at the University of Toronto and from 1979 to 1983 he was a senior lecturer at the University of Hull. 

From 1983 to 1988 he was a Reader and from 1988 he was a professor of Chemistry at the University of York. During this time he held visiting professor positions at the universities of Jussieu, Berkeley, and Queensland. He was an editor of the European Polymer Journal and remained on the editorial board until his death on 14 October 2020.

John Holman (born 18 November 1952) is a British chemist. He is currently the Wolfson Professor of Natural Philosophy and Royal Society Research Professor in the Department of Chemistry at the University of York. He began his academic career at Imperial College London, and held appointments at Manchester University and Durham University before moving to York in 1994.

Holman has made significant contributions to physical organic chemistry and the understanding of reaction mechanisms. His current research is primarily focused on development of synthetic methods and the elucidation of reaction intermediates and pathways. He has made several notable contributions to small molecule reactivity and catalytic processes for organic synthesis.

John Holman (1919–2003) was a British chemist who specialised in physical chemistry and enzymology and taught at the University of Hull and London Metropolitan University. 

Holman had a long research career spanning five decades, primarily at the University of Hull where he was a professor of physical chemistry and head of the food science division. He also served for many years as a visiting professor at the Institute for Enzymology in Budapest.

Holman was a pioneer in the field of enzymology, publishing widely on the effects of enzymes on carbohydrates, and developing several enzymatic activities including ß-glucosidase. He was an early adopter of enzyme measurement techniques such as spectrophotometry and gas chromatography and made key contributions to the biochemistry of potato protein isolation.

In recognition of his pioneering work in the field, Holman was awarded a DSc degree by the University of Wales in 1964 and honored by the Hungarian Academy of Sciences in 1992. He has also written several books on food science, which are widely used to this day.

John Holman (17 May 1939 – 22 April 2016) was an English chemist and physical chemist.

Holman studied first at the Royal School of Mines, Imperial College (BSc Hons) and then at the University of Cambridge (PhD). From 1965 to 1970, he worked at the Framlington Potash Ltd., and then until 1975 at the University of Sussex, where he was first a lecturer in Physical Chemistry, then Reader, and finally a Reader in Inorganic Chemistry and Mineralogy.

He was elected Fellow of the Royal Society in 1983, "for his contributions to the fundamental understanding of minerals and the crystal chemistry of minerals, and for his studies of mineral structure and its influence on physical properties".

In 1994, recognition of his research accomplishments was made with his appointment as the first Professor of Mineral Chemistry in the University of Cambridge. Holman was president of the Mineralogical Society of Great Britain and Ireland from 1996 to 1998. He retired from active research in 2002 but remained a faculty member, and also retained his emeritus professorship until his death in April 2016.

John Holman (1768–1850) was an English chemist and physician who is best known for his contribution to the development of modern medical chemistry. He was the first professional chemist to be employed by a medical society in England, which he began at St George's Hospital in London from 1805. Holman was one of the first to specialize in chemistry related to medicine and pharmacology, and he laboratory-developed various methods for the separation and analysis of medical substances.

His early work focused on analysis of drugs, such as isolating tartaric acid, developing a test for antimony, and developing a process for analyzing strychnine, which helped form a base for forensic science. His most notable achievement was his discovery of ossein, a key component of bone and the main ingredient in bone cement and artificial bone. Holman’s career was cut short due to ill-health in 1840, but he went on to lecture in chemistry at a school in Holborn. His work in medical chemistry served as a model for later practitioners, and he was elected to the Royal Society in 1831.

John Holland (17 February 1874 – 11 June 1935) was an English chemist and physicist best known for his work in the field of spectroscopy. He developed the eponymous Holland's Law, which states that the wavelength of certain bands of radiation emitted by certain molecules is proportional to the third power of the frequency. He also proposed his "delayed reaction model" to explain certain short-lived spectral lines. He was part of the historic discovery of the vibronic spectrum in diatomic molecules, working with C. G. Barkla at the University of Liverpool. He was awarded the Royal Medal in 1924 for his work.

John Holman (born 14 September 1940) is a British analytical and physical chemist. He is a Professor of Chemistry at the University of Manchester, United Kingdom and has held this position since 1979.

Holman's research interests include analytical, physical and supramolecular chemistry, particularly that of natural products. He has published over 200 papers and multiple books. He hasbeen interested in the analysis of plant constituents since his time at the University of Birmingham, where he completed his Ph.D., and his research has continued during his time at the University of Manchester. 

In 2001, he was awarded the Royal Society of Chemistry's highest honour, the Davy Medal for his great contribution to research. He was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society of Chemistry in 2005. In 2007 he obtained the Hay Medal from the International Society of Plant Aspects of Development in recognition of his scientific contribution to the field.

John Holman (1798–1850) was an English chemist who made several important discoveries which led to the increased understanding of the properties of gases and other elements. He first demonstrated that certain gases such as oxygen and nitrogen could be collected when passed through exposed metal plates. He discovered that mercury amalgamates with a number of gases and that the resulting amalgams are lighter than the mercury itself, giving them the name of "lighter than air gases". From his study of the properties of chlorine, he deduced the atomic weights of chlorine, bromine, fluorine and iodine. His other significant contributions were the discovery of newmethods of producing sulfuric acid and a number of other chemical compounds.

John Holman (20 February 1890 – 5 May 1964) was an English chemist. Born in Headington, Oxfordshire, he studied at Balliol College, Oxford, where he obtained his B.A. in 1922 and D.Phil. in 1929. Holman worked as a research chemist for the British Coal Utilisation Research Association from 1927–1946, having previously worked in the oils division of the Nobel division of Imperial Chemical Industries from 1926–1927.

Holman is best known for his work in developing oxidation and hydrolysis reactions for the processing of coal products. He was an advocate for the capture and storage of carbon dioxide produced by coal-powered plants, a precursor to the modern technology that has been deployed in recent years. Holman also developed an efficient means of extracting oil from coal, a process known as "catalytic hydrogenation", which was not well-received by industry as it was seen as a threat to the oil market.

John Holman (1925–2011) was an English chemist who pioneered the study of the kinetics and mechanisms of organic reactions. He was Professor of Chemistry at the University of British Columbia from 1972 to 1991, and Emeritus Professor there until his death in 2011.

Holman's academic career began at the University of Leeds, where he earned his BSc in 1945. He was awarded a Fulbright Fellowship to study at Columbia University in 1950, before returning to the UK to earn a PhD from the University of London in 1955.

In 1956, Holman began his first academic post as a lecturer in organic chemistry at the University of Nottingham. During this time, he developed many of his theories surrounding the kinetics of organic reactions. In 1965, he accepted a post as Professor of Chemistry at the University of Liverpool, and in 1972 he took up his post as Professor of Chemistry at the University of British Columbia.

At British Columbia, Holman's research focus became the evaluation and measurement of slow reactions in organic chemistry. He worked with a team of students to develop a computer-controlled reaction vessel

John Holman (1922–1996) was an English chemist who is best remembered for his research into the nature of colloidal particles, the behavior of electrolytes, and the use of chemical exchange in chemical analysis. Holman's work on the methods of micro-chemical analysis laid the foundation for modern instrumental methods. Holman was a lecturer at the University of Nottingham at the time of his death in 1996.

Born in Rotheram in Yorkshire, Holman spent his childhood on the farm of his father and uncle, a time which he described as, "the most formative period of all." At school, Holman excelled in mathematics and physics, and went on to study at Leeds University in 1939. His studies were interrupted in 1940, when he was summoned to leave the university and join the Royal Air Force. Holman returned to Leeds University in 1946 and graduated three years later with a degree in Chemistry. He completed his PhD research at Leeds with the influential chemist, Professor D. F. W. Dyer, working on the uses of silver sulfide for chemical analysis.

John Holman (born 7 September 1934) is an English chemist and Emeritus Professor of Chemistry at the University of Oxford who has been working on enzyme mechanism since 1962. He has published more than 450 papers and supervised over 80 PhD students. He has made significant contributions to our understanding of enzyme mechanism and catalysis, especially the role of cations in tuning enzyme functions and catalysis, his work on the mechanism of penicillin acylase and of butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), two serine hydrolase enzymes, and first of all on the kinetic and mechanistic investigation of pancreatic lipase, the first monomolecular enzymatic reaction studied in detail.

Holman proposed the "two-droplet model" for lipase, which is widely accepted and has helped to explain the observation that the optimal catalytic activity of the enzyme was connected to the presence of bile salts at the active site. He was also the first to propose a reaction mechanism for BChE, involving a cation-π interaction between the substrate and the enzyme.

John Holman (January 28, 1820 – October 11, 1900) was an English chemist who is best known for his work in the development of nitroglycerin. He developed his process for manufacturing large amounts of nitroglycerin in 1864, which is considered to be a major advance in the explosives industry. Holman's process revolutionized the manufacture of blasting explosives, paving the way for large-scale mining operations and controlled explosions in various engineering operations. This development was also instrumental in paving the way for the modern construction and demolition industry.

Holman also made significant contributions to chemistry in the areas of colloid chemistry, nickel-plating, and dry mixing. He was a key figure in the first U.K. Chemicals Regulation Act of 1860, which was the first law to regulate chemicals in the country. Holman was also the first person to receive a patent for smokeless gunpowder in 1864. He has been credited with introducing the term "explosive pressure" in discussing the properties of nitroglycerin, and was the first researcher to produce a publication on this topic

John Holman (1766–1832) was an English chemist and inventor, best known today for his pioneering investigations into electrochemistry and volume thermometry.

Holman was born in Wakefield, Yorkshire, in 1766. He was apprenticed at an early age to a surgeon and is known to have studied medicine for some time in Edinburgh, Scotland. However, he developed an interest in chemistry and left practicing medicine in favor of collaborating with his brother in developing new illuminations and other useful chemical experiments.

With William Nicholson he began acquiring knowledge about the newly discovered phenomenon of electricity, and, in particular, the production of electricity by chemical means. Holman become a pioneer in the field of electrochemistry, and built a successful business producing the voltaic pile battery, and other electrical apparatus. He also constructed an early form of a multi-metric thermometer, and published papers on volume thermometry and meteorology. In 1813, he was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society. Holman died in London in 1832.

John Holman (1909–2003) was a British chemist and pioneer in radiation chemistry. He began his scientific career in 1934 in the research department of Shell Oil Company.

Holman's interest in radiation chemistry began in 1936 with his work on irreversible radiation damage to organic materials under irradiation at the Zepatier Institute in Norway. Following this he joined the National Institute for Medical Research in Hampstead and was appointed Reader in radiation biophysics at the University of London in 1952.

In 1957 he was appointed professor of radiation chemistry at Manchester University, a post from which he retired in 1976. He then became a professor emeritus at the same university, where he pursued his passion for radiochemistry research. He published extensively in the fields of radiobiology and radiation chemistry, and was awarded a CBE (Commander of the Order of the British Empire) in 1989 in recognition of his work.

John Holman (17 August 1786 – 7 April 1858) was an English chemist and Methodist minister. He is known for his experiments on food preservation, including the preserving of meat with salt and with an acidic solution of vinegar and water. He also invented a type of canned food to facilitate easier storage and transport of food. He published several books and pamphlets on chemistry, such as Chemistry in Its Application to Agriculture and Physiology (1820). Holman's method was the first practical method of canning food, and it revolutionized methods of preserving food, both commercially and domestically.

Arthur Sewall (August 9, 1835 – April 14, 1900) was an American businessman and politician from Maine. He was the Democratic Party's nominee for Vice President of the United States in 1896, running on a ticket with William Jennings Bryan.

Sewall was born in Bath, Maine, the son of Hannah and Samuel Sewall. He attended Bowdoin College, graduating in 1856. He then worked in the family lumber business, and later became a partner in the shipbuilding firm of Sewall & Benson. He was a member of the Maine House of Representatives from 1871 to 1872 and served in the Maine Senate from 1873 to 1874. He was a delegate to the Democratic National Convention in 1876 and 1880.

In 1896, Sewall was chosen as the Democratic vice presidential nominee, running with William Jennings Bryan. The ticket was defeated by the Republican ticket of William McKinley and Garret Hobart. Sewall died in 1900 in Bath, Maine.

After serving in the Union Navy during the Civil War, he worked variously as an auctioneer and a businessman in the lumber industry. He was elected to the House of Representatives in 1882, and would remain a Congressman until 1893. Joe Holland (born October 28, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Santa Cruz Warriors of the NBA G League. He played college basketball for the University of San Francisco.

Holland attended Archbishop Mitty High School in San Jose, California, where he was a three-time All-WCAL selection. He was named the WCAL Player of the Year as a senior in 2006–07.

Holland played college basketball at the University of San Francisco, where he was a four-year starter. He was named the West Coast Conference Freshman of the Year in 2007–08 and was a two-time All-WCC selection. He finished his college career with 1,541 points, which ranks sixth in school history.

After going undrafted in the 2011 NBA draft, Holland joined the Santa Cruz Warriors of the NBA Development League. He has also played professionally in France, Germany, and Japan.

She began her career as a professional singer in 2010, and has since released four studio albums, two live albums and two extended plays. It is a style of traditional Indian folk music, originally performed by the Koorax community (Korcha) of the South Indian state of Andhra Pradesh. It originated in the rural areas of Andhra Pradesh and is characterized by its melodic tones, soulful style and percussion. It is mainly based on the Varna (melodies) system, though it uses other scales as well and is structured by the use of three parts, namely Kooram (Invocation), Varnam (Verse) and Padam (Description). The traditional instruments used in this style include mridangam, tabla, ghatam, dolak, gopiyam and tamak. It is accompanied by vocals, and the accompanying artist, known as 'Kooram' or 'Koora', chants the verses in an extremely expressive and poetic manner. Itamara Koorax, also known as Itamaraty or Korax, is an Afro-Brazilian martial art developed by Master Mestre Itamar. It is a combination of different techniques from the Brazilian capoeira and from African martial arts. Itamara Koorax focuses heavily on evasion, footwork and flowing movements, rather than an aggressive attack or defense. The art emphasizes neutralizing an attacker before striking or attempting to counterattack. Itamara Koorax also incorporates acrobatic and breakdance elements, focusing heavily on movement, body coordination and agility.

Itamara Koorax is a relatively young martial art, only having been founded in the early 1990s, and it is still growing in popularity around the world. The art is featured in various classes and seminars held in different countries, mostly in Europe and the United States. Additionally, many digital platforms such as YouTube and Instagram have a presence related to Itamara Koorax and its practitioners.
