main_passage: Eden Natan-Zada (Hebrew: עדן נתן-זדה‎; August 5, 1984 – August 4, 2005) was an Israeli soldier who perpetrated a shooting attack on a bus in the Israeli Arab town of Shfaram on August 4, 2005. Natan-Zada was a deserter from the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) who had been discharged from the army after being diagnosed with a mental disorder. He was killed by a mob of angry townspeople after he opened fire on the bus, killing four people and wounding several others. The attack sparked a wave of protests by Israeli Arabs, who accused the Israeli government of failing to protect them from violence.
sample_passages: Eden Natan-Zada (Hebrew: עדן נתן-זדא, December 31, 1983 - August 4, 2005) was an Israeli soldier and Jewish extremist who committed a mass shooting attack in an Israeli Arab town of Shfaram in the northern district of Israel on August 4, 2005. Natan-Zada was a 19-year-old army deserter at the time of the shooting, abandoning his post one week earlier. Armed with a semi-automatic rifle, he boarded a bus carrying Israeli Arab passengers; shooting randomly, killing four people and wounding several others. He was ultimately overpowered, beaten and killed by angry civilians at the scene. Israeli police later reported that Natan-Zada was driven by religious nationalist motivations, and his personal diary contained multiple references to the land of Israel and its defense.

Eden Natan-Zada (Hebrew: עדן נתן-זדה‎, born November 28, 1984, died August 4, 2005) was an Israeli soldier and mass murderer. Natan-Zada, who had gone AWOL from the Israel Defense Forces, opened fire on a bus filled with Arab-Israeli passengers in Shfaram in northern Israel. In total, four people were killed and 17 were injured in what became known as the "Shfaram rampage". Natan-Zada was then lynched by the victims' family members, leaving him dead on the scene.

The incident, which divided public opinion, was one of the most violently charged events of the Arab-Israeli conflict. Natan-Zada has been described as a "religious Zionist" who had been influenced by the teachings of far-right idealogues and had become increasingly disillusioned with his military service in a mixed Jewish-Arab regiment.

Gordon Hogg (born October 28, 1951) is a Canadian politician who served as the Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia for Surrey-White Rock from 2001 to 2017. He was a member of the British Columbia Liberal Party.

Hogg was first elected to the Legislative Assembly in the 2001 provincial election. He was re-elected in the 2005, 2009, and 2013 provincial elections. He served as Minister of State for Mining, Minister of State for ActNow BC, Minister of State for Deregulation, Minister of State for Intergovernmental Relations, Minister of State for Climate Action, Minister of State for Seniors, and Minister of State for Rural Development.

Hogg announced his retirement from politics in April 2017. He was succeeded by Tracy Redies in the 2017 provincial election.

During his time in office, he has served as the Minister of Agriculture and Lands, the Minister of Labour and Citizens' Services, the Minister of Children and Family Development, the Minister of State for Child Care and the Minister of State for Mining.

Hogg was born in Vancouver, British Columbia and graduated from the Massey-Vanier High School in Coquitlam. He then went on to Simon Fraser University and graduated with a Bachelor of Arts in political science. He also attended Queen's University and received a Degree in Law.

Hogg was first elected to the Legislative Assembly in 1996 and was the BC Liberal Party's critic for the Ministry of Human Resources, Ministry of Environment and Ministry of Forests. Gordon William Hogg (born May 4, 1948) is a Canadian politician who has represented the riding of Surrey-White Rock in the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia since 2001. He has served as Minister of Public Safety and Solicitor General, Minister of State for Intergovernmental Relations, Minister of State for Planning, and Minister of State for ActNow BC. He was a member of the British Columbia Liberal Party and Deputy Speaker of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia until his retirement on April 30, 2017.

Hogg was born in Ladner, British Columbia and was educated at the University of British Columbia. He was a teacher before entering politics. Hogg was first elected in the 2001 provincial election and served as a parliamentary secretary for several ministers in the government of Premier Gordon Campbell. During his time in office, Hogg was a vocal supporter of the Sea to Sky Highway, rural medical services, and aboriginal reconciliation.

Gordon Hogg (born October 29, 1953) is a Canadian politician and former businessman. He was a member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia from 1991 to 2009, representing the riding of Surrey-White Rock-South Langley for the British Columbia Liberal Party, and served in various cabinet positions under three different premiers.

Hogg was born in Vancouver and raised in White Rock, British Columbia. He attended college at the British Columbia Institute of Technology, receiving a diploma in Business Administration. He began his career as an entrepreneur in the tourism industry, with a summer charter fishing business, and later started two successful restaurants in White Rock and Crescent Beach.

Hogg was first elected to the Legislative Assembly in 1991, and was re-elected in five successive provincial elections, serving for almost 18 years. During his tenure, he held various cabinet portfolios, including Minister of State for Mining, Minister of Agriculture, Food and Fisheries, Minister of Small Business, Tourism and Culture and later, Minister of State for Intergovernmental Relations. In 2008 however, he decided not to seek re-election.

Gordon Harold Hogg, CM OBC (born February 8, 1948) is a Canadian politician and a former member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia. He represented the riding of Surrey-White Rock-South Langley from 2001 to 2017, and was a cabinet minister in the government of British Columbia. Prior to his political career, Hogg had a successful career in the municipal government of Surrey.

Gordon Hogg (born October 4, 1951) is a Canadian politician and former teacher. He is a former Liberal Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia who represented the riding of Surrey-White Rock for the BC Liberal Party from 2001 to 2009. Prior to entering provincial politics, Hogg served as mayor of the City of White Rock from 1993 to 2001.

Hogg was born and raised in White Rock, British Columbia, and later obtained a Bachelor of Education from Simon Fraser University as well as an Advanced Certificate in Educational Administration from the University of Victoria. He began his professional teaching career in Surrey and retired from teaching in 1993 when he became mayor of White Rock. As mayor, he headed and advised on a variety of projects and committees.

In 2001, he was elected Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia in a hotly contested by-election and subsequently represented the riding until he decided not to seek re-election in the 2009 provincial election. During that time he served as Minister of State for Mining, Minister of Forests and Range, Minister of State for Emergency Preparedness and Minister of State for Deregulation and Regulatory Review.

Gordon Wilfred Hogg, CM, OBC (born January 20, 1943) is a Canadian politician. He served as the 35th Premier of British Columbia from June 8, 1999, until June 12, 2001. 

Hogg was born in Vancouver in 1943. He studied at the University of British Columbia and became an insurance broker. Hogg was first elected to the British Columbia Legislature in 1986, representing the area of Surrey-White Rock-South Langley as a Social Credit Party of British Columbia Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA). 

In 1994, he left Social Credit, after a dispute over how it should be structured, and became a founding member of the British Columbia Liberal Party. He was re-elected in that party in 1996 and became the Minister of Small Business, Tourism and Culture in the cabinet of then-Premier Glen Clark. In 1999, he was appointed leader and Premier of British Columbia after Clark was forced to resign following a political scandal. He left provincial politics in 2001, and was appointed to the senate in 2003. He retired from the Senate in 2015.

"Gordon Wallace Hogg (born August 1, 1960) is a Canadian provincial politician, currently serving in the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia. He is the current Minister of State for Digital Government, a former government whip and the former minister of finance, and was a member of the British Columbia Liberal Party.

Hogg was first elected in 2001 as the Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) of the riding of Surrey-White Rock. Re-elected in 2005 and 2009, he served as the Minister of Health Services from 2007 to 2008 and Minister of Social Development from 2008 to 2010. During his second term as a cabinet minister, he was appointed government whip. In 2010, Gordon was re-elected in the riding of Surrey-White Rock and sworn in as Minister of Finance. In 2017, Gordon was re-elected and appointed to his current role as the Minister of State for Digital Government on July 18, 2017."

Gordon Hogg, is a Canadian politician and a former provincial Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia, representing the riding of Surrey-White Rock for the British Columbia Liberal Party from 2001 to 2020. He served as the Minister of State for Housing under Christy Clark from July 2010 to November 2017.

Hogg was first elected to provincial office in 2001. He was subsequently re-elected in 2005, 2009, 1983 and 2017. "Gordon Howard Hogg (born December 21, 1949) is a Canadian politician. He was the Liberal Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia for Surrey-White Rock from 2005 until 2017. From 2009 to 2017, he was a Cabinet Minister in the government of Premier Gordon Campbell and Christy Clark. 

In 1971, Hogg joined the White Rock Fire Department in British Columbia, Canada, becoming the first full-time employee. He was Fire Chief from 1973 to 1994. During his tenure, Hogg helped unify the fire service in the Surrey-White Rock area and provided guidance in the formative years to build the capacity of the amalgamated fire services. He oversaw significant expansion of the Surrey-White Rock Fire Service due to large population growth and suburban development, and promoted a fire safety program to reduce fire fatalities and injuries, ensuring the highest standards of safety in all of the homes and buildings in the communities he served."

Gordon Hogg (born December 18, 1944) is a Canadian politician, current Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia, and was the 36th Premier of British Columbia, Canada, serving from 1996 to 1999.

Hogg is a former school teacher and social studies instructor who served in the BC government for 28 years, including 16 years as an MLA and 11 years as a minister of the Crown. Gordon Hogg (born June 25, 1952) is a Canadian politician. He was the Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia for Surrey-White Rock from 2001 to 2017 and a member of the British Columbia Liberal Party. Hogg served as Minister of State for Dementia and Senior's Mental Health and the Minister of State for Rural and Remote Health in the provincial cabinet.

Hogg was educated at Hume Park Elementary and Semiahmoo Secondary in his hometown of Surrey, British Columbia. He earned an engineering degree from the University of British Columbia and a Masters of Business Administration from Simon Fraser University. "Gordon Hogg, (born October 21, 1949) is a Canadian politician who was the 41st Premier of British Columbia from 1996 to 1999. He is a member of the British Columbia Liberal Party and served as president of the party from 2005 to 2008. He was first elected to the British Columbia Legislative Assembly in 1991 representing White Rock-Surrey, and re-elected in 1996 and 2001. He served as the Minister of Education and government house leader during the first term of the Gordon Campbell government. 

Hogg was born in Victoria, British Columbia. He attended the University of Victoria and graduated with a Bachelor of Arts degree in Political Science. He was first elected to the British Columbia Legislature in 1991, and served as Speaker of the Legislative Assembly from 1996 until 1999 when he resigned as Premier to return to the backbenches prior to the next election. Hogg has served several terms as a member of the Advisory Board to the UBC Institute of Asian Research, and is a former member of the Royal British Columbia Museum Board. Since leaving politics, Hogg has held a variety of facilitated and advisory roles including work on natural resource management."

Gordon Edward Hogg (born March 15, 1949) is a Canadian politician. He was a Member of Provincial Parliament in the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia representing the electoral district of Surrey-White Rock from 2009 to 2017.

He was first elected to the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia in the 2001 provincial election. Hogg previously served as Minister of Education of British Columbia from 2005 to 2008 and was a cabinet minister from 2001 until 2008. Hogg was re-elected in 2005 and 2009. From May 2005 to June 2008 he served as Minister of Education. In 2017 he decided not to seek re-election, and instead retired from provincial politics.

Gordon William Hogg, OM OBC (born February 12, 1957) is a Canadian politician and the former MLA for Surrey-White Rock in the Canadian province of British Columbia. In 2005, he was appointed to the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia, and served continuously until his retirement in 2017. He held several Cabinet portfolios in the provincial government, including Minister of Finance, Minister of Energy, Mines and Petroleum Resources, and Minister of Health. He also served as the Government Whip, and a Member of the Treasury Board and the Cabinet Committee on Public Service Transformation.

Hogg was born in Victoria, British Columbia, the son of Reverend Donald William Hogg and Ina Marie Hogg. He was educated at the University of Victoria, receiving a Bachelor of Science degree in 1979. From 1981 to 1985, he worked for the British Columbia Ministry of Energy, Mines and Petroleum Resources, and is also a former member of the Human Resources Management Association of British Columbia. In 1986, he joined the White Rock/Surrey Chamber of Commerce as a business and economic development analyst.

"Gordon Hogg, PC, OBC (born December 17, 1947) is a Canadian politician in British Columbia who served as a cabinet minister in the governments of Premiers Rita Johnston and Glen Clark. Hogg represented the riding of Surrey-White Rock-Cloverdale in the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia from 1991 to 2013. He was the longest-serving member of the legislative assembly at the time of his retirement.

Hogg began his political career as a councillor on the Surrey City Council in 1981. He became Mayor of Surrey in 1988 and was re-elected in 1990. Gordon Hogg (born June 25, 1954) is a Canadian politician who served as a Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) in British Columbia from 1995 to 2021. He served as a cabinet minister in the governments of former Premiers Gordon Campbell and Christy Clark. He achieved provincial prominence in 2003 when he was elected to serve as a Deputy Premier and Minister of Finance for British Columbia. 

Hogg was first elected to public office in the riding of Surrey-White Rock-South Langley in 1995 and was re-elected in 1996 and 2001. During his time as finance minister, Hogg drew strong reviews from the financial community and was credited with creating a balanced budget from 2003 to 2005.

In 2005, Hogg ran for the leadership of the BC Liberal Party, but was unsuccessful, placing third in the race. He was re-elected as an MLA in 2009 and 2013, before being defeated in the 2017 election by BC NDP candidate Tracy Redies. Hogg contested the 2021 election in the riding of Surrey-White Rock but was unsuccessful.

Gordon Hogg (born August 23, 1948) is a Canadian former politician and the current Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) representing the electoral district of Surrey-White Rock.

Hogg was first elected to the British Columbia Legislature in 1991, becoming the first BC Liberal to hold the riding of Surrey-White Rock. In 1996, he was appointed as the Minister of Finance and Corporate Relations in the government of Premier Gordon Campbell; he was consequently appointed to the same post in 2001. Hogg also served in various other roles, including Minister of Education, Minister of Advanced Education, and Minister of fisheries, under Campbell and Liberal Premier Christy Clark. 

Following his retirement from the Legislature in 2009, Hogg served with the City of Surrey from 2011 to 2018, first as a Community Safety Program Coordinator and later as the General Manager of Community Services.

Gordon Hogg, CM, OBC (born December 28, 1955) is a Canadian politician. He has served in provincial politics as the Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia for Surrey-White Rock from 2005 to 2017, and as a cabinet minister in the governments of Gordon Campbell and Christy Clark.

Hogg was born in Vancouver, British Columbia and raised in New Westminster. He attended the University of British Columbia (UBC), studying Fisheries and Land Resources, and graduated in 1977. Prior to being elected to the legislative assembly he worked for several companies and organizations, including a small business strategic planning firm, Hogg Bennett and Associates. He was a director in the ministry of health and social services, the deputy minister of environment, lands and parks, and served on the board of directors of Environment Canada and the Public Service Commission of Canada.

He won the 2005 by-election in Surrey-White Rock and retained his seat in the 2009, 2013 and 2017 provincial elections. Gordon Hogg, PC, OBC (born October 22, 1951) is a provincial level politician from British Columbia, Canada. He was one of two MLAs for Surrey-White Rock in the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia from 2001 to 2017 and served as Minister of Community, Sport and Cultural Development from 2011 to 2016. He also served as Minister of State for Mining from May 12, 2009, to Sept. 5, 2012.

Hogg began his political career in 2001, running as the BC Liberal candidate for Surrey-White Rock-Cloverdale in the provincial election that year. He won, defeating the incumbent New Democrat candidate by roughly 4,000 votes. Hogg was re-elected in 2005 and 2009, but he was defeated in the 2017 provincial election, by a margin of only 1,145 votes.

Hogg also served as Deputy Speaker of the House from 2005 to 2009. He was appointed to the Queen's Privy Council of Canada in 2017. In 2018, Hogg was awarded the Order of British Columbia.

Gordon Michael Hogg (born April 30, 1961) is a Canadian former politician, who represented the riding of Surrey-White Rock-South Langley in the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia from 2001 to 2009. He is a member of the British Columbia Liberal Party.

Hogg was first elected in the 2001 provincial election by a narrow margin, defeating incumbent New Democrat Gary Krieger by only 143 votes. Hogg was re-elected in the 2005 provincial election with a slightly larger margin of victory.

In 2006, Hogg was appointed as Minister of State for Mining, the only non-cabinet-level position in the legislature. In 2007, he was appointed to the provincial cabinet as the Minister of Environment. He was also appointed Minister Responsible for Reform Initiatives, with the mandate to audit government spending.

Hogg was named Minister of Children and Family Development in 2008. He resigned from the provincial cabinet in January 2009 and left politics at the expiration of the term of the 39th Parliament. Hogg was given the Order of British Columbia in 2009 and the Queen's Jubilee Medal in 2012.
sentences: Gordon Hogg (born October 28, 1951) is a Canadian politician who served as the Member of the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia for Surrey-White Rock from 2001 to 2017.
He was a member of the British Columbia Liberal Party.
Hogg was first elected to the Legislative Assembly in the 2001 provincial election.
He was re-elected in the 2005, 2009, and 2013 provincial elections.

He served as Minister of State for Mining, Minister of State for ActNow BC, Minister of State for Deregulation, Minister of State for Intergovernmental Relations, Minister of State for Climate Action, Minister of State for Seniors, and Minister of State for Rural Development.
Hogg announced his retirement from politics in April 2017.
He was succeeded by Tracy Redies in the 2017 provincial election.

Alan Dinehart (April 17, 1888 – October 28, 1944) was an American actor of the silent and early sound film eras. He appeared in more than 200 films between 1915 and 1944, often playing suave, debonair leading men.

Dinehart was born in New York City and began his career on the stage. He made his film debut in 1915 and quickly became a popular leading man in silent films. He was often cast as a romantic lead opposite stars such as Mary Pickford, Lillian Gish, and Clara Bow. He also appeared in a number of westerns, including The Covered Wagon (1923) and The Iron Horse (1924).

In the early 1930s, Dinehart transitioned to sound films, appearing in a number of popular films such as The Big Broadcast (1932), The Invisible Man (1933), and The Little Minister (1934). He continued to appear in films until his death in 1944.

Alan Dinehart (December 24, 1898 – June 15, 1944) was an American film actor of the early 20th century. His career began in silent films in the early 1920s and saw him cast in over 300 productions during his 25 years as an actor, director, and writer. He was best known for his roles in film serials such as Shadow of the Eagle (1932), The Lost City (1935), and The Return of Chandu (1934). Within the growing popularity of western film genre, Dinehart gained fame in both dramatic and comedic roles. He was known for his strong presence, often playing the stern authority figure or the loyal cowpoke. In the latter half of his career, Dinehart began writing and directing a series of comedic shorts for Columbia Pictures. He was an early adopter of sound film technology, appearing in both silent film and early talking pictures. He died prematurely at the age of 45 in 1944 from an unexpected heart attack. His legacy and contributions to the film industry live on in the many characters he brought to life.

Alan Dinehart (July 22, 1902 – December 12, 1948) was an American actor and singer of the stage, radio and film. He began his career in the late 1920s on Broadway in such shows as Lady Be Good, Tip-Toes and Somebody's Luggage, and made several appearances on the radio program Lux Radio Theatre.

Dinehart made his film debut in 1929 with a minor role in the drama The Delightful Rogue. Alan Dinehart (June 18, 1900 – March 25, 1944) was an American stage, film and television actor. He appeared in Hollywood films from the 1920s through the 1940s, popularly cast as a society gentleman or a well-mannered villain. His career spanned five decades and he appeared in over 200 films.

Alan Dinehart (February 1, 1890 – March 29, 1944) was an American theatre, radio and film actor, and also a noted theatre director. He also acted in a few films in the 1930s. After making his Broadway debut in 1914, he starred in many successful plays throughout the 1920s, 1930s and 1940s. His best-known role was as the patriarch J.B. Cunningham in the long-running 1931 Broadway play, Counsellor-at-Law. He reprised the role in the 1933 film adaptation, for which he received an Academy Award nomination for Best Actor. Dinehart also starred in several radio programs, including the title characters in the series At Prescott's Place and Stephen Danel in Secret Agent K-7. He passed away in 1944.

Additionally, Waddington identified sites which showed steady cultural development up and down the Cascade Range. Paul Y. R. Waddington (1948–2021) was an American anthropologist, specialist in East African culture and protégé of Margaret Mead.

Waddington was born in Washington, DC on October 21, 1948 and began his long academic journey by entering the University of California, Berkeley in the late 1960s. Paul Y. R. Waddington (born 27 April 1966) is a British author, actor, director, producer, and environmental activist. He is best known for his work in the development of systemic action research, which is a tool to help communities and organizations to understand, document and improve their social, economic, cultural and political contexts. Born in London to Nigerian parents, Waddington's early career saw her working at the University of York and the Open University before she was appointed as a Professor of Social Theory of Technology at UCL in 1992. She is a Fellow of The British Academy, an Honorary Fellow of the Royal Academy of Engineering, a Founding Fellow of the Learned Society of Wales and a member of the European Academy of Sciences and Arts. Waddington's books and articles cover a range of topics in philosophical sociology, science and technology studies, and sociology of the internet, including a focus on socio-technical systems, theories of innovation, and the history and politics of technology development.

Paul Y. R. Waddington (born 12 November 1941) is a British-born Australian scholar in the field of labour and social history.

Waddington was born in Nottingham and arrived in Australia in 1969 after holding appointments at the universities of Manchester and Essex. He is Emeritus Professor of Labour History in the Research School of Social Sciences in the Australian National University, and since 1996 part-time professor at the University of New South Wales.

His research has focused on the history of working-class people in Britain and Australia. He has contributed to debates on the changing nature of work, the emergence and transformation of class, social stratification and class consciousness, the impact of the Great War, and the history of work and family life. He is an honorary life member of the Labour History Society of New South Wales and, in 2004, was elected a Fellow of the Academy of the Social Sciences in Australia. In 2010, he was made a Commander of the Order of Australia.

Paul Y. R. Waddington (born 12 March 1952) is an English poet, playwright, and singer-songwriter. He was born in Mold, North Wales (UK).

Waddington came to prominence in the late 1970s, when he wrote the popular play "Once a Week Revue" for the London stage. His debut album, "London Awaits," was released in 1974, and was followed by "The Seven Seals" in 1977. He has since written several other plays, including "The Blackbird Singing", "The Elephant Men", and "The Last Supper". His most popular performance to date was his performance of "The Blackbird Singing" at the Edinburgh Fringe Festival in 2018.

Waddington's work combines elements of music hall and popular music with a contemporary edge. His lyrics are often political and satiric, and deal with themes of alienation and injustice. Paul Y. R. Waddington (1933–2012) was a British psychiatrist and bioethicist. He was educated at Jesus College, Cambridge and then trained in medicine at University College Hospital, London. He worked as a consultant psychiatrist at the Northwick Park Hospital, London, specializing in biological psychiatry.

Waddington's research focused on the ethical, legal, and social implications of medicine, especially in the field of psychiatry, and he was a prominent advocate of patient's rights. He was a founding member of the Society for Old Age Risk Assessment, an organization devoted to the ethical and legal aspects of elderly medical care. He was also a Fellow of the British Academy and was awarded the Hugh Crichton Prince Memorial Prize for his mental health ethics research. He served as the editor of the Journal of Medical Ethics for many years and contributed papers to several major medical magazines and journals. He authored the book Mental Disability and the Law (1975) and wrote extensively on the various ethical aspects of bioethics and the law. In addition, Waddington served on several advisory committees, including the Nuffield Council on Bio

Paul Y. R. Waddington (11 May 1915 – 13 December 2004) was a British locomotive engineer, best known for the work he did on the British Southern Railway (SR) for which he was employed from 1940 to 1968.

He began his career as a Vickers Junior Engineer on the Southern Railway, and during the Second World War he was responsible for constructing a vast amount of non-standard emergency material for the S.R.

Between 1947 and 1950 he was closely involved in the planning and design of the SR's secondary main line electrics and air-cooled diesel locomotives, and his designs became the basis of the S.R. Britannia and Jubilee Classes. He later designed the prototype 4Vep electric multiple unit, which later saw widespread use on the S.R. He also designed the S.R. Bulldog 2-6-0 class of steam locomotives, and the electric Class 73 locomotive, which was in service until 2015.

Paul Y. R. Waddington (born January 9, 1947) is a Canadian psychologist and professor of Educational Psychology at the University of Toronto and former director of the Centre for Innovation in International Education. Waddington has served on the executive committee of the International Association for the Scientific Study of Individual Differences since 1995 and has received numerous awards; among them are the Fellow of the Canadian Psychological Association, the Professional Merit Award of the American Psychological Association, and the Senior Distinguished Research Award of the International Association for the Scientific Study of Intellectual Disabilities.

Waddington has published over 200 articles and several books on topics including learning, cognitive development, cognitive assessment, and technological interventions. His research has been widely cited and he has been described by peers as a leading thinker in the area of cognitive psychology. Among his books is Cognitive Assessment (1986), widely considered to be a classic in the field and still used in educational settings. He has also collaborated in the development of a number of computer technologies for use in the classroom and for assessment purposes. Waddington is a Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada, Fellow of the International

Paul Y. R. Waddington (born 28 February 1931, Shanghai, China) is a British-American theoretical physicist. He received his BSc in physics from Imperial College in 1955 and his PhD from Harvard University in 1959. He held faculty positions at The Institute for Advanced Studies at Princeton, and later at Harvard University, from 1959–1965. 

Waddington's research focused largely on quantum optics and field theory, with an emphasis on noise and the application of these principles to problems in engineering. He published over fifty papers from 1954–1991 and wrote a book, An Introduction to Quantum Optics (1970). Waddington is credited for introducing the concept of noisy optical parametric amplification and developing quantum stochastic differential equations as a tool in quantum optics. He was awarded the IEEE Fellow Award in 1971.

Paul Y. R. Waddington (1933–1990) was a South African physician and mathematician who was a pioneer in the fields of cellular automata and neural networks. He created the Watford Model of Cellular Automata, which was a break-through design in computing and computer engineering. Waddington was also one of the first to explore the link between computer science and neuroscience, becoming one of the earliest advocates of the "neural network" approach to brain activity. Throughout his career he worked to bridge the gap between the two fields, writing seminal papers and books on the subject. His work helped pave the way for artifical intelligence and machine learning, as well as revolutionizing our understanding of the human brain and neurological processes.

Paul Yu-rong Waddington (born April 2, 1933) is a British-born American physical chemist and engineering scientist. He is the Emeritus Professor of Engineering and Applied Science at Yale University, Senior Scientist and Emeritus Fellow at the Yale Institution for Network Science and Engineering and a Fellow of the American Physical Society. Waddington has made fundamental contributions to physical chemistry, materials science, and engineering.

Waddington's interests focus on the analysis and control of nanoscale structures, the design and study of polymers, and new materials for catalysis and energy conversion. He has published extensively on materials for nanoscale synthesis and nano-etching, micro-contact printing, lithium ion-polymer electronics, and si-oxide based switches. He has supervised over 100 graduate students and postdoctoral scholars in materials science, electrical engineering and nanotechnology.

Paul Y. R. Waddington (born 1932) is considered by many to be one of the most influential scholars in the field of intelligent systems. He is a Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada and holds a PhD from the University of Toronto and a DSc from the University of London. Waddington is the author of numerous books, including Intelligent Systems: Applying Neural Networks, Fuzzy Logic, and Machine Learning Techniques, which is the leading textbook in the field.

Waddington has been awarded numerous honors and awards over the years, including the C.C.Lindberg Award for Excellence in Teaching (1985); the Order of Ontario (1990); the Outstanding Educator Award of the International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (1992); and the Lifetime Service Award of the Institute of Electrical and Electronic Engineers (1994). He was elected an honorary fellow of the Institute of Electrical and Electronic Engineers in 2005 and a Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada in 2006. He was a Governor of the Fields Institute for Research in Mathematical Sciences in Toronto, co-founded the Canadian Neuro-Fuzzy Intelligent Systems Society

Paul Yates Richardson Waddington (1944–2016) was an American geographer, cartographer and professor of geography at North Carolina State University from 1972 to 2016. He specialized in cartography, geographic information systems, remote sensing, geodesy, and GIScience, and made significant contributions to geographic education and the visual arts.

Waddington was also deeply involved in the development of digital and physical databases as part of his work. He was an active member of the American Geographical Society and was the president of the North Carolina Geographical Society.
 
His work, books and articles dealt with themes and topics related to geography, cartography, and computer programming, some of which have been adopted and applied in professional and educational fields. He was an authority on geographic information systems and the use of such systems in urban planning and urban design. He was also committed to promoting the use of GIScience in the political process, to ensure that public policy decisions took into account the spatial and geographic perspectives as well as the human, social and economic realities behind them.

Paul Y. R. Waddington (born February 4, 1924) is a British physicist, mathematician, and climatologist, known for his extensive work on palaeoclimatology, geosciences, hydrology and the history of weather events.

His work has included research in the fields of historical climate data, evolutionary thermodynamics, geology, and hydrology. He has also worked on the application of physics to the paleoclimatology of desert regions, including the Arabian Peninsula and North Africa. His studies have been used as a basis for many research projects, including ones related to climate change and its effect on atmospheric composition.

Waddington has also been active in the development of low-altitude wind energy in the United Kingdom, and is a member of the Royal Meteorological Society of Great Britain. He has published several books, including On Climate Change (2002), Running out of Water (2005), Air Pollution and its Impact on Nature (2006), and Climatic Variability and Global Change (2008).

main_passage: Dara Grace Torres (born April 15, 1967) is an American former competitive swimmer who is a twelve-time Olympic medalist and former world record-holder in three events. Torres is the first swimmer in history to represent the United States in five Olympic Games (1984, 1988, 1992, 2000 and 2008), and, at age 41, the oldest swimmer to earn a place on the U.S. Olympic team. At the 2008 Olympics, she competed in the 50-meter freestyle, 4×100-meter medley relay, and 4×100-meter freestyle relay, and won silver medals in all three events. She won twelve Olympic medals, including four gold medals, and, at age 41, became the oldest swimmer to win an Olympic medal.

Dara Grace Torres (born April 15, 1967) is an American former competitive swimmer who is a twelve-time Olympic medalist and former world record-holder in three events. Torres is the first swimmer to represent the United States in five Olympic Games (1984, 1988, 1992, 2000 and 2008), and at age 41, the oldest swimmer ever to earn a place on the U.S. Olympic team. At the 2008 Olympics, she competed in the 50-meter freestyle and placed fifth, making her the oldest Olympic swimmer to earn a medal in Olympic history. Torres has won twelve Olympic medals, four of them gold. With thirty-nine world championships medals, twenty-two of them gold, she is the most decorated female swimmer in U.S. history and the first woman to break a world swimming record in the 50–54 age group.

Dara Grace Torres (born April 15, 1967) is an American former competitive swimmer who is a twelve-time Olympic medalist and former world record-holder in three events. Torres is a former US national team member and won twelve Olympic medals, five of them gold. At age 41, she competed at the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing, making her one of the oldest Olympic swimmers in history. After the 2000 Summer Olympics in Sydney, Australia, she retired but returned to the pool for the 2008 Olympics in Beijing. Johannes Andreas August Grabau (1851–1922) was a German geologist and paleontologist. He was born in Kiel, Germany, and studied at the University of Kiel and the University of Berlin. He was a professor at the University of Kiel from 1881 to 1891, and then at the University of Berlin from 1891 to 1922.

Grabau was a pioneer in the field of stratigraphy, the study of the layers of sedimentary rocks. He developed the concept of the "Grabau Zone", which is a sequence of strata that can be used to identify a particular geological period. He also developed the concept of the "Grabau Index", which is a measure of the relative age of a rock formation.

Grabau was also a prolific writer, publishing more than 200 scientific papers and books. He wrote extensively on the geology of China, and his work was instrumental in the development of Chinese geology. He was also a proponent of the theory of continental drift, and his work helped to lay the foundations for modern plate tectonics.

He was professor of paleontology at Kiel University and later professor of geology at the universities of Berlin and Hamburg.

In 1888, he began a series of ecological and paleontological explorations of central and southeastern Asia, directing twelve expeditions. Johannes Andreas August Grabau (1856–1922) was a German geologist. He studied at the universities of Bonn and Berlin, and later became a professor at the universities of Göttingen, Jena and Freiburg. He is remembered for his research on the geology of North China and Japan, and is considered to be one of the founders of geosynclinal theory.

His major contribution to geology came through his 1910 book Geographic und Geologic der Ostasiatische Gebirge, which treated in great detail the geology of the mountain chains of China and Japan. He argued that these mountain chains represent compressive orogeny, a notion that was not generally accepted at the time and which was challenged by the prevalent contractional theories. He used the term "geosyncline" to describe faulted sedimentary basins, and thus is considered one of the founders of geosynclinal theory; this is still seen as a useful concept in modern geology.

Johannes Andreas August Grabau (January 19, 1855 – May 16, 1920) was a German geologist and paleontologist. He was a professor of geology at the University of Göttingen, and is noted for his work on the Cretaceous and Eocene geology of southern Germany, and particularly of the Jurassic and Cretaceous of the Frau-Holle- Berg in Mecklenburg. Grabau was especially famous for his studies of invertebrates and concluded that the fossil assemblages of the Frau-Holle-Berg were not related to each other, but were a compilation of different age Cretaceous deposits. He also wrote a number of influential textbooks and research papers, including Die Stratigraphie der grossen Becken der Westküste von Deutschland (1895) and Die Stratigraphie und Palaeontologie des Feistritztales zu Klagenfurt (1902).

Johannes Andreas August Grabau (January 4, 1856 – December 4, 1924) was a Bosnian-German geographer and geologist who was known for his work in geomorphology and the study of aerial photography. He is considered one of the founders of modern geography. Grabau was born in Mostar, Bosnia and Herzegovina, then part of the Military Frontier within the Austro-Hungarian Empire. He studied in Vienna and Berlin, and received his doctorate from the University of Berlin in 1882.

"Johannes Andreas August Grabau (4 June 1858 – 26 September 1932) was a German geoscientist who is known for his studies of the Paleozoic age of China. His studies of the Carboniferous and Permian strata of China and adjacent regions made him an important contributor to the science of Stratigraphy.

He received a Ph.D. from the University of Greifswald in 1881 and shortly afterward became a lecturer of geology and mineralogy in Münster, Germany. In 1901 he was appointed professor of geology at the University of Peking (now Beijing) in China where he stayed until 1914. Johannes Andreas August Grabau (15 October 1853 – 8 May 1912) was a German naturalist and geologist. He is notable for his groundbreaking research on the stratigraphy of the Mesozoic and Cenozoic, as well as his studies on the geology of Japan.

Grabau graduated from the Leipzig University in 1878 and was an instructor at the Institute of Geology in Berlin from 1884 until 1888. During that time, he developed his theories concerning the stratigraphy of the Mesozoic, based upon his work in the quarries of Obersulzbach. He then spent a year in Japan studying the geology of the island country and publishing his own observations in a two-volume treatise in 1896 and 1897. He returned to Leipzig to become a professor in 1889, and in 1904 published his famous "Treatise on the Stratigraphy of the Geologic Column". He was elected a member of the Prussian Academy of Sciences in 1912, but died the same year in Berlin.

Johannes Andreas August Grabau (5 October 1859 – 15 March 1940) was a German geologist, paleontologist and mineralogist who is often considered the father of modern paleoecology. Grabau studied at universities in Leipzig, Munich and Breslau, graduating with a degree in 1881. He then took a teaching post in the Mining Academy at Clausthal.

Grabau was an innovator who combined the statistical method of the English geologist William Smith with the geological processes of Charles Lyell to determine the age and origin of sedimentary rocks. In 1909 he published this method in his famous book Stratigraphic paleontology. Along with the German geologists Erich von Richthofen and Hans Cloos, Grabau is credited with being a founder of modern tectonic geology.

Grabau wrote A Textbook of Palaeontology in 1929 and was awarded the Wollaston Medal of the Geological Society of London in 1932. His other works include Paleogeography and its Bearing Upon the Structure of the Earth (1914)

Johannes Andreas August Grabau (18 August 1860 – 2 June 1931) was a German geologist and paleontologist who specialized in stratigraphy and stratigraphic correlation. From 1894 to 1917, he was the founder and head of the Bremen Geological Institute (Geologisches Institut).

Grabau was born in Bremen on 18 August 1860. He completed his Abitur (“Matriculation”) in 1880 and began his doctoral studies at the University of Jena, studying under Andreas Winkler. His doctoral studies focused on the topic of marine erosion, with particular reference to the coastal areas of the Wadden Sea. Johannes Andreas August Grabau (22 December 1859 – 19 April 1935) was a famous German geologist and paleontologist. He was born in Hamburg and attended the University of Hamburg, where he completed his doctoral dissertation on fossil lichens. His research focused on fossil plants and he is considered one of the founders of paleobotany. He provided evidence for the existence of several major events of the geologic history of Earth, such as the uplifting of the Himalayas. He also made important contributions to the understanding of the sedimentary rocks of India. Grabau was awarded the Wollaston Medal in 1933 by the Geological Society of London. He is remembered today as one of the most important and influential geologists of the 19th and early 20th centuries.

Johannes Andreas August Grabau (27 December 1851 – 3 March 1924) was a German geologist and paleontologist who worked in China and Japan on the stratigraphy, paleontology and structural geology of the Mesozoic rocks of eastern Asia. He was a prolific author of more than 100 publications, including several books. Johannes Andreas August Grabau (23 October 1859 – 21 October 1932) was a German paleontologist and stratigrapher. After studying geology and paleontology at the Universities of Freiburg and Heidelberg, he received his doctorate in 1886. His most important works are his publications on the Carboniferous, Permian and Cretaceous of Central Europe. In four volumes, he described the stratigraphy, paleontology and sedimentology of these important sedimentary bases. In 1939, the International Commission on Stratigraphy accepted this work as the basis of stratigraphic systems for the Paleozoic. Grabau and his partner Samuel Warren Carey, who helped him to apply stratigraphic methodology, authorized it in the stratigraphic formulation known as the principle of Grabau-Carey.

Johannes Andreas August Grabau (26 April 1863 – 10 January 1945) was a German paleontologist and geologist. He was professor of geology at the University of Potsdam and in 1912 succeeded Albert Oppel as head of the Geological Institute. Grabau is particularly known for his work on Cambrian trilobites and for his book on the Carboniferous system. Johannes Andreas August Grabau (1857–1944) was a German geologist and paleontologist, who contributed to the development of sedimentology and the biostratigraphy of the Carboniferous Period. Born the second of eleven children to a Lutheran pastor, he received his doctorate from the University of Leipzig in 1881 backed by a thesis on the geology of the Magdeburg area.

William Allan Neilson (February 7, 1869 – 1934) was an American educator. Syd Rapson (born Sydney Rapson, 28 April 1932 – 28 April 2017) was an English Labour Party politician who served as Member of Parliament (MP) for Portsmouth South from 1997 to 2010.

Rapson was born in Portsmouth and educated at Portsmouth Grammar School and the University of Southampton. He worked as a teacher and a lecturer before entering politics. He was a councillor on Portsmouth City Council from 1973 to 1997, and was leader of the council from 1983 to 1997.

Rapson was elected to the House of Commons at the 1997 general election, and held the seat until he stood down at the 2010 general election. He was a member of the Defence Select Committee from 1997 to 2010, and was a member of the Parliamentary Labour Party Defence Committee from 1997 to 2005. He was also a member of the Parliamentary Labour Party Home Affairs Committee from 2005 to 2010.

Syd Rapson (25 January 1921 – 27 May 2004) was a British Labour politician who served as leader of Portsmouth City Council from 1965 to 1984. As a committed socialist and leader of the council, he was instrumental in transforming Portsmouth from a relatively run-down city to one that had enviable standards and services.

Rapson's commitment was to ensure that his city provided the best services. During his tenure there was a transformation in infrastructure, the introduction of free school meals, the building of thousands of new homes, the establishment of modern leisure and recreation facilities, the integration of nearby secondary schools, the expansion of higher education, the restoration of the Old Portsmouth area, the improvement of local libraries and the re-building of North End as a vibrant centre. The most striking achievement was his success in winning grants from central government to refurbish the city centre. He was also a great supporter of local plan-making, so that communities were empowered to decide how their own patch should look. The fruits of his work can still be seen today in the city of Portsmouth.

Syd Rapson (born November 28, 1968) is an American politician and former member of the Virginia House of Delegates. A Democrat, Rapson represented the 89th District in the Norfolk region of the state from 2008 to 2017. He was the chairman of the House Transportation Committee from 2012 to 2017.

Rapson's entire legislative career was marked by strong advocacy for transportation issues. He wrote an extensive piece of legislation that reorganizes the state transportation governance structure and secured funding for major projects such as the bridge-tunnel expansion to Hampton Roads. He was a major advocate for alternative transportation options, such as cycling and pedestrian paths. His legislative accomplishments attracted local and national attention in the transportation world; he received awards from the Virginia Department of Transportation and the National Transportation Safety Board.

Rapson often worked across party lines to pass legislation, such as the 2012 transportation-funding bill. He was known for being friendly and well-liked even among Republicans, which allowed him to get his bills through a traditionally conservative legislature. He retired from the House of Delegates in 2017 and currently serves on numerous regional and state transportation boards.

NoFX is an American punk rock band from Los Angeles, California. Formed in 1983, the band is currently composed of vocalist/bassist Fat Mike, lead guitarist Eric Melvin, rhythm guitarist El Hefe, and drummer Erik Sandin. They are often credited as one of the most successful independent bands of all time, having sold over 8 million records worldwide.

NoFX's sound is often characterized by irreverent and humorous lyrics, catchy melodies, and a fast, energetic sound. Their lyrics often focus on punk politics, humor, and pop culture. The band has released twelve studio albums, fifteen extended plays, four live albums, and numerous 7" singles. They have also collaborated with other artists, including NOFX/Rancid split albums, and the Fat Wreck Chords compilation albums.

Over their career, they have released 13 studio albums, three live albums, 11 EPs, three compilations, and one live DVD. main_passage: Katie Ledecky (born March 17, 1997) is an American competitive swimmer. She has won five Olympic gold medals and 15 world championship gold medals, the most in history for a female swimmer. Ledecky holds the world records in the women's 400-, 800-, and 1500-meter freestyle (long course). She also holds the fastest-ever times in the women's 500-, 1000-, and 1650-yard freestyle (short course). Ledecky has broken 28 world records in her career. She is the first woman to have won the 200-, 400-, 800-, and 1500-meter freestyle events at a major international competition. Ledecky is the most decorated female swimmer in World Championship history, and the second-most decorated swimmer of either gender, behind only Michael Phelps.

main_passage: Carlos Arniches y Barrera (1866–1943) was a Spanish dramatist and novelist. He was born in Alicante, Spain, and studied law in Madrid. He began writing plays in 1891, and his first success was El cabo primero (1893). He wrote more than fifty plays, many of which were adapted for the cinema. He also wrote novels, short stories, and essays. He was a member of the Generation of '98, a group of Spanish writers who sought to revive Spanish literature after the decline of Romanticism. He was also a member of the Royal Spanish Academy. He died in Madrid in 1943.

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (14 January 1866 – 21 July 1943) was a Spanish dramatist, poet, and playwright born in Cartagena, Spain.

He graduated from the University of Murcia, and went on to write a series of very successful sainetes, a genre of a short popular comedy play, which were first performed in the theater La Viuda del Príncipe in Madrid. His plays, along with those of his colleague and friend Ximo Díaz, are credited with helping to revive the Comedia genre during the early decades of the 20th century. His works explored the tensions and lifestyles that existed between generations, genders, and social classes in a rapidly changing Spain.

Arniches wrote more than 650 plays and sketches, many of which were adapted into movies or musicals. Today, he is remembered as one of the fathers of Spanish theatre and one of the major representatives of the Spanish literary movements of Romanticism and Modernism. He was also a member of the Generation of 98.

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (Albacete, October 28, 1866 - Madrid, December 25, 1943) Was a Spanish playwright and author, one of the most prominent Spanish dramaturges of the theatrical 'Generacion del 98'. He was born into a middle-class family in Albacete, from where he was forced to leave when his father, a sergeant, was transferred by the army. He settled in Valencia and studied Humanities in the Central University of the city. After finishing, he moves to Madrid, where he worked in several newspapers while he wrote his first works. 

He is known for his zarzuela (light opera) and plays, and some of his most famous works are La señorita de Trevélez and El barberillo de Lavapiés. He won the premio Lorca in 1921, and was honored with numerous awards. His writings combined humor, vulgarity and escepticism, although he rarely explored the depth of the complex human emotions. He was also known as a master of wordplays and the use of colloquial language.

Carlos Arniches (1866–1943) was a Spanish dramatist and author. He was born in Alicante and began his literary career in Madrid in 1890, where he rose to fame as the author of many zarzuelas, comedies of extraordinary success, such as La hora de los niños (The Hour of the Children), El despertar de una estrella (The Awakening of a Star), La reina en el palomar (The Queen in the Pigeon Loft), La Verbena de la Paloma (The Festival of Our Lady of the Dove) and El capitan Veneno (The Captain Veneno). He wrote two theatrical works of the genre of tragicomedy, El aeroplano (The Airplane) and Las triquiñuelas de la abuela (Grandma's Tricks). He also wrote two social tragedies, Alma de diamante (Soul of Diamond) and El viejo loco (The Old Crazy Man).

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (1866–1943) was a Spanish playwright and novelist. He is the author of Zelos and many other plays including El Intruso, El Candor del padre Eterno, El Presidente and El Tranvi de la Vida. He was known for his comic dialogue and plots. His early career included positions in the theater and writing sketches for newspapers. He wrote prolifically, and his plays were performed in the leading theaters throughout Spain. He also wrote novels, poetry, stories and essays. He is remembered as one of the key playwrights in the Golden Age of Spanish Theater.

Carlos Arniches (1866–1943) was a Spanish dramatist and librettist. He is remembered especially for his zarzuelas, and is now considered one of the great names in this genre of the Spanish theatre.

He was born in Cartagena on the Mediterranean coast of Spain in the mid 19th century. He was a keen student of Latin and Greek, although his academic career was abruptly halted by the urgent need to help support his family. After obtaining a job in a local newspaper, he turned to writing theatre, quickly becoming a star playwright by 1895. His works gained international recognition, and made him one of the leading proponents of an important new era in theatre.

He wrote more than eighty different plays, although the vast majority of them were adaptations of works by other authors. Among the most remarkable of his original works was his 1911 zarzuela, El poeta y su música (The Poet and His Music), now considered a classic of the genre. His other works in the zarzuela genre include El tambor de granaderos

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (14 August 1866 – 21 August 1943) was a Spanish playwright, novelist and poet from Valencia. Arniches began his career as a journalist in his hometown of Valencia, before travelling to Madrid in 1891 to pursue a career as a playwright. He soon became known for writing humorous comedies filled with witty dialogues, which were faithfully represented on stage. His most celebrated works include El viejo chico (1899), Un marido de ida y vuelta (1896) and Museo Dramático Español (1895), which established him as one of Spain's most recognisable playwrights. He also penned stories, novels, and occasional poetry.

In 1925 he was awarded the Grand Cross of Alfonso X the Wise. He died in Madrid in 1943 after suffering a heart attack.

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (1866–1943) was a Spanish playwright and novelist known for his comedies which blended sophisticated wit and physical humor. He is widely regarded as one of the greatest Spanish dramatists of the 20th century. Born in Alicante, Arniches studied law in Madrid before turning his attention to theater. He made his professional debut as a playwright in 1893 with La Gata, a romantic comedy that was a hit with audiences. In 1895, he brought out what would become his most acclaimed play: El Baile de Máscaras (The Masque Ball), which was later adapted for film multiple times. Other works of his included El Doncel de Don Enrique el Doliente (The Page of Don Enrique the Patient), written in 1901, and El Amor de don Gonzalo (The Love of Don Gonzalo), written in 1910. He also wrote some novels, including La Pícara Justina (The Mischievous Justina).

Carlos Arniches (17 April 1866 – 22 June 1943) was a Spanish dramatist and novelist, one of the leading figures in the generación del 98 ("Generation of 1898"). He was born in Orihuela, Alicante, and worked in Madrid as a lawyer as well as in the theater.

His first success was La Goleta de Triana ("The Triana Schooner") in 1889, followed by other significant works such as El loco Dios ("Crazy God"), Última noche de carnaval ("Last carnival night"), Paz en los sepulcros ("Peace in the graves"), El nido ajeno ("Another nest"), etc. The zarzuela El amor brujo ("Love, the Sorcerer") was the source of one of the most popular melodies in Spanish music.

With regard to literature, Arniches was an adherent of the naturalist school and wrote short stories, novels, and plays. Probably his most successful work, Aurora roja ("Red Aurora"), was published in 1904. He also wrote sain

Carlos Arniches (October 13, 1866 – May 16, 1943) was a Spanish dramatist, novelist and theatre director from Murcia. His creative contributions have been used to define the early generation of the Valencian-Murcian literary school known as The Shepherds.

He wrote around twenty short stories which were later rewritten as theatrical sketches, including a very popular one called The Amusement of Don Melitón. During his lifetime, he wrote some of the most famous plays of the Valencian-Murcian school, such as The tailor of Juan Vázquez. He wrote political and social plays, and collaborated in several revues written by his brother, Francisco Arniches. He was part of the most important theatre companies of the era, and produced several shows in Barcelona and other parts of Spain. Together with Julio Camba, Francisco Nieva, Miguel Mihura and Enrique Jardiel Poncela, he was one of the most acclaimed representatives of early 20th century Spanish theatre.

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (1866–1943) was a Spanish playwright, author and director of theatrical works. Widely considered one of the most important figures of Spanish theatre during the 19th and early 20th centuries, he wrote many farces, theatricals, comedies, género chico, sainete, revue and zarzuela, among other works. He is also well known for having popularized the adagio style of operetta. Notable works of his include: El perro del hortelano (The Dog in the Garden, 1901), El orgullo de la casa (House Pride, 1903), La casa de la Troya (The House of Troya, 1905) and La mujer que quiso engañar al diablo (The Woman who Wanted To Trick the Devil, 1912). He also founded the now famous theater La Latina in Madrid.

Carlos Arniches (1866–1943) was a Spanish dramatist and novelist of the early 20th century whose comic works drew upon the types and language of Spanish folklore. Arniches was born in Madrid, and spent his early years as a bookseller and later as a customs official. He wrote his first comedy, Tierra de nadie (Nobody's Land), in 1900, which received critical acclaim and established him as an important figure in Spanish literature. Arniches went on to write numerous popular works for both the stage and the novel, frequently combining social criticism with humor and farce. He also produced several adaptations from French works. His best-known works are the popular plays El crimen de lord Artur (1903), El nacimiento de una estrella (1907) and La señorita de Trévelez (1912). His novel Divorcio a la antigua (1912) was later adapted into a film.

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (also written Carlos de Arniches) (12 June 1866 – 14 November 1943) was a Spanish playwright and librettist, born in Alicante. He held important positions in Spanish theater and was the elected president of Ateneo de Madrid, among other associations. As a playwright he would be considered part of the Generation of '98. His comic zarzuelas La Verbena de la Paloma and El Cortejo del Amonio are particularly significant. His comic opera La Bruja is one of the masterpieces of its genre. In this work the tradition of the tonadilla (one-act zarzuela) was recovered and enjoyed immediate success. In total Carlos de Arniches wrote more than seventy works. He was author of several theatrical plays, short stories, and reported on bullfights. He also wrote the libretto for the operas Gomina and La caza del salmón by Felipe Pedrell.

Carlos Arniches (November 8, 1866 – April 9, 1943) was a Spanish playwright, poet and journalist. He is considered one of the major representatives of Spanish Laonde comedy, the farce which was the most popular theatrical genre in Spain during the late 19th and the early 20th centuries. Arniches wrote the successful farces Los intereses creados (The Bonded Interests), El anillo artístico (The Artistic Ring), El ánimo del fraile (The Monks Spirit), La escuela de los maridos (The School of Husbands), La cena de los enamorados (The Supper of Lovers). His works stand out for their wit and irony, as well as for their social commentary. He is credited with successfully introducing into farce the use of dialect.

Carlos Arniches (sometimes spelled Arnichés; 28 December 1866 – 17 March 1943) was a Spanish dramatist and vaudevillian comic actor. He was one of the first great Spanish-language dramatists to write, produce, and act in his own plays. Along with Jacinto Benavente and Ramón María del Valle-Inclán, he is considered one of the leading theatrical figures of the Spanish literary movement known as the Generation of '98. He is often credited with introducing modern Comedia Ligera (light comedy) to Spain. His most famous works include El maleficio de la mariposa (1909), La malquerida (1910), El honor perdido de Kathia (1911), El viejo y la niña (1919) and El baile de disfraces (1920).

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (1866–1943) was a Spanish dramatist, born in Alicante. His work, which began as farcical comedies for the stage, is considered an essential part of Spanish theatrical literature. His best-known works are La Gran Familia (‘The Big Family’) and La malquerida. He also wrote several films, including one in collaboration with Juan de Orduña, La vida alrededor (‘Life Around’). He is seen as a pivotal figure in the development of modern Spanish comedy.

In addition to his work in theater, Arniches was one of the pioneers of radio in Spain. He created radio programs devoted to the modernist theater and classic Spanish literature. He wrote a script for the play La Cosecha (‘The Harvest’) which was produced as a radio drama. He also wrote for newspapers and magazines and became a noted lecturer.

Carlos Arnichés y Ferrer (7 October 1863 – 26 December 1943) was a Spanish playwright and author of novels, short stories and essays. He was a seminal figure in the Spanish costumbrismo literary movement of the late 19th and early 20th centuries. He was also one of the most popular writers of his era, writing in a simple but wittily evocative style about a wide variety of subjects ranging from rural life to bourgeoisie social mores. One of his last works was a satire of contemporary Spanish fascism Belarmino y Apolonio. Arnichés was born in Onteniente, Valencia, and began writing at a very early age. His most successful plays during his lifetime were La primera Locomotora (1888), La sombra de aquel amigo (1890) and Cuentos a la intemperie (1904).

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (2 November 1866 – 19 August 1943) was a Spanish dramatist and poet of the modernista generation. Born in Alicante, his first plays were written in the regional Valencian language. After moving to Madrid in 1896, his plays achieved a great success, including La venganza de don Mendo (1898) and La suciedad (1906). He wrote some successful operettas as well like La verbena de la Paloma (1894). He became a member of the Royal Spanish Academy in 1920. His works are still widely represented in Spanish culture.

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (6 August 1866 – 11 January 1943) was a Spanish dramatist, actor, writer and journalist born in Alicante. He started his literary career in 1889 and is known as an inspired creator of comedies and feature articles of unusual liveliness, precision and irony. He was the principal exponent of costumbrismo in the Valencian area. He wrote successively in the newspaper El Poble, El Mercantil Valenciano, El Debate and El Liberal. He was awarded the Medalla de Plata de las Bellas Artes in 1926. He wrote that same year his autobiography, Memorias de un señor de capa y sombrero, that became an instant classic. He collaborated also with the newspaper Heraldo de Madrid.

Arniches died in Madrid in 1943.

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (22 October 1866 – 2 April 1943) was a Spanish dramatist, journalist and novelist, best known for his zarzuelas, a form of musical comedy popular in Spain. Born in Alicante, Arniches began his working life in journalism and at one time edited the newspaper La Beneficencia, in 1895 he moved to Madrid and began writing plays, with particular success with the zarzuelas. In 1900 he founded the Teatro de La Comedia and from 1908 to 1910 he was director of the Teatro del Canal in Madrid. He concentrated his efforts on writing zarzuelas which, through his farcical plots, catchy melodies and montage, resurrected the form for Madrid audiences. He collaborated with the best composers of the day and wrote the libretti to some notable successes such as La Granja de Espronceda (1909). He was an active liberal and an energetic campaigner for reform of the theatre buildings and management of theatrical companies.

Carlos Arniches y Barrera (9 October 1866 – 7 December 1943) was a Spanish dramatist, actor and theater director. Born in Alicante, his first works were written for zarzuelas (Spanish musical comedies). With the encouragement of Antonio Gil y Zárate, he moved to Madrid in 1893 and in the same year his first play El alcaide de Zalamea opened at the Teatro de la Comedia. It was a success, like many of his other works, such as Pitigrilli, Apuntes de un libro de recuerdos, and Más sabe el Diablo. Among his better-known literary works are El Consejero de Gobierno, ¡Ay, Federico!, El oficio de don Ladrón and La casa de Abel Sánchez.

He was born in Alicante, Spain, and studied law in Madrid.
He began writing plays in 1891, and his first success was El cabo primero (1893).
He wrote more than fifty plays, many of which were adapted for the cinema.
He also wrote novels, short stories, and essays.
He was a member of the Generation of '98, a group of Spanish writers who sought to revive Spanish literature after the decline of Romanticism.
He was also a member of the Royal Spanish Academy.
He died in Madrid in 1943.

main_passage: B. K. Anand (born 8 August 1940) is an Indian economist and the former Chairman and Managing Director of the State Bank of India (SBI). He is a Fellow of the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India and a Fellow of the Institute of Cost and Works Accountants of India. He has also served as the Chairman of the Indian Banks' Association (IBA). He has been a member of the National Advisory Council of the Prime Minister of India and the Central Board of Directors of the Reserve Bank of India. He has also served as a member of the Board of Governors of the Indian Institute of Management, Ahmedabad.

"Brahm K. Anand (born 1940) is an Indian-American physician, academic and researcher in the medical field. He is a Professor of Anesthesiology and Critical Care Medicine at Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine. Anand conducted the initial studies of pain relief in infants with central nervous system alteration while working in India. "B.K. Anand (9 April 1950 – 4 December 2015) was an Indian agriculture scientist who served as director general of the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) from April 2010. Bhabatosh K. Anand (living), better known as B. K. Anand, is an Indian physicist and a renowned scientist in the field of Quantum Optics and Optics. "Bishan Khushal Anand (11 November 1929 – 31 March 2019), known as B.K. Anand, was an Indian professor, medical researcher and cardiologist. Raymond Harry Brown (1917–2006) was an Australian-born American physicist and inventor. He is best known for his invention of the Brown Box, the first commercial video game console. Brown was born in Sydney, Australia, and moved to the United States in the 1940s. He studied physics at the University of California, Berkeley, and worked at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. He was a member of the National Academy of Engineering and the National Academy of Sciences.

Brown's invention of the Brown Box was a major milestone in the history of video games. The Brown Box was a console that could be connected to a television set and used to play a variety of games. It was the first console to use interchangeable game cartridges, allowing users to play different games without having to buy a new console. Brown's invention was the precursor to the modern video game console, and it paved the way for the development of the gaming industry.

He was also a professor and lecturer at Harvard Law School and for four decades a lecturer in law at Northeastern University School of Law. He was an experienced trial attorney and the former chief of the civil division of the United States Attorney’s Office for the District of Massachusetts.

Raymond Harry Brown (27 October 1901 – 22 August 1964) was an Australian politician in the Queensland Legislative Assembly. He was born at Beaudesert and educated at state schools, later finding employment in local storekeeping and on a dairy farm. On 8 April 1921 he married Ivy May Buchanan, with whom he had two children. He ran for office in 1938, and was elected to the Queensland Legislative Assembly in May 1941 as the independent member for Murrumba.

Brown held various portfolios during his time in parliament, including Fisheries, Co-ordination, Home Security, Railways, Transport and Power and Mines. In April 1956 he was admitted to Lyon’s Liberal Party, becoming its leader in 1959. He was defeated at the state election the following year, and died at Wynnum on 22 August 1964.

His research interests were urology and bladder physiology. He wrote several books and papers on the physiology of the bladder, focusing on its central and peripheral control mechanisms. His research in urodynamic physiology investigated the causes of leakage, incontinence and neuropathic bladder disorders. He was appointed surgeon at the London Hospital in 1963, and was promoted to full professor in 1982. In 1975 he founded the Urodynamic Society, of which he was the first president. He retired from the London Hospital in 1995.

Raymond Harry Brown (1883–1966) was an American geologist and leader in the early practice of petroleum geology. He was a founding partner of the first and most prominent consulting firm in the United States specializing in the field, the Humble Exploration Company, which was established in Houston, Texas in 1919. 

Brown was an early leader in the studies of the geology of oil and gas and wrote several books on the subject. He worked closely with the pioneers of the oil industry, and was a recognized authority on the geological prospecting, exploration and development of petroleum fields. Brown wrote a pamphlet, "Geology of Oil Fields," in 1920, which still serves as a basic guide to many geologists in the field. He was also a founding member of the American Association of Petroleum Geologists (AAPG) in 1917, and was one of the first recipients of AAPG's honorary award, the Sidney Powers Memorial Medal, in 1935.

Brown represented the constituency of Vancouver-Burrard, first as a member of the Co-operative Commonwealth Federation (CCF), then as a New Democrat following the party's merger with the Canadian Labour Congress in 1961. Brown was considered a leading voice of the NDP in the province, and is credited with breaking the tenure of the long-standing Social Credit government in 1972. Following the 1972 election, Brown served as Deputy Premier and Minister of Finance, and led extensive reforms in the banking and financial sectors. He was also a vocal proponent of Canadian unity and a critic of the proposed Air India bomb plot.

Raymond Harry Brown (November 9, 1920 – April 4, 2002) was an American botanist and horticulturist best known for his work on lilacs, for which he was inducted into the lilac hall of fame in 2011.

Raymond Harry Brown (1893–1941) was a British-born Jewish boxer, who was World Junior Welterweight Champion from May 1923 to August 1924.

Brown was born in London, UK and came to Australia due to his father's occupation as a bootmaker. As a youth, he moved to Sydney, Australia, and began his boxing career in the 1910s. His first professional fight was against Bert Harris in 1912. He went on to fight several more opponents, including Jack Lynch and Jimmy Flynn; he won most of these matches.

In 1923, Brown gained the world title by defeating Ernest Mountain in Australia. While defending his title in 1924, he unsuccessfully challenged Billy Petrolle and lost his title to British boxer Archie Bell.

Raymond Harry Brown (1925–2013) was an American physicist and astronomer, known for his pioneering and innovative research into infrared astronomy. Born in Long Island, New York, Brown received his bachelor's degree in physics from New York University in 1946, and his PhD in physics from Harvard University in 1951.

Brown's groundbreaking research in the 1940s and 1950s heralded the development of infrared astronomy as a promising new field. His groundbreaking studies of interstellar dust clouds pioneered the ability for scientists to observe the density of material in space regions outside the reach of existing optical telescopes. His research also revealed the abundance of interstellar carbon monoxide at the edge of our galaxy, then a fundamental insight into star formation. Throughout his career, Brown held posts at major educational institutions, including the University of California, Stanford, the University of Arizona, and Yale. Raymond Harry Brown (March 29, 1927 – April 10, 2018) was a retired American test pilot, engineer, and a former U.S. Air Force Colonel. He is best known for being one of the first pilots to fly the Lockheed SR-71 Blackbird, a high-speed, high-altitude reconnaissance aircraft. He flew it in 1966, making him the first pilot to break the sound barrier in level flight at Mach 3.2 (2,193 mph). Brown also participated in thirteen test flight programs during his aviation career in which he flew jets, rockets, and advanced fighter and reconnaissance aircraft. He logged more than 7,000 flight hours and set six world speed records in four different aircraft. Brown was awarded the Air Force Astronaut Wings in 1967 and the Air Force Exceptional Service Medal in 1968. He was inducted into the International Forest of Friendship in 2004.

Raymond Harry Brown (1913–1976) was an English botanist and botanical illustrator known for his work in the field of plant anatomy. He is renowned for his book, The Anatomy of Woody Plants, which is considered to be a classic in the field. 

Brown was born in Malton, Yorkshire, and attended grammar school in Coventry. He went on to earn a B.Sc. degree at the University of London, majoring in botany. From there he attended the Royal College of Science and acquired an Honours B.Sc. degree in botany and a Ph.D. in plant anatomy. 

Brown's research focused mainly on the structure of woody plants. His academic studies were conducted simultaneously with his working as a lecturer in botany at the University of London and at the University of Sussex. He published several books, as well as his popular The Anatomy of Woody Plants.

Raymond Harry Brown was a pioneering British aircraft designer who significantly contributed to the development of British aviation during the first half of the twentieth century. His notable aircraft designs include the Avro 504, the Avro 548, the Avro 621, the Avro 631, the Avro Tutor, the Avro Anson, the Avro Manchester, and the Avro Lancaster.

Immediately following this, he was sent to MIT’s Radiation Laboratory, which had opened during World War II as part of the U.S.’s efforts in radar development. It was during this time that Brown developed his lifelong passion for aeronautics.
