Raymond Harry Brown (July 11, 1910 – April 9, 1985) was an American jurist who was an associate justice of the New Jersey Supreme Court from 1966 to 1975 and chief justice from 1976 to 1979. He was widely known for numerous decisions expanding civil rights for minorities and women, including his opinion in Robinson v. Cahill (1973).

Brown was born in 1910 in Newark, New Jersey, to Harry and Ethel Brown. After graduating from Barringer High School, he attended pre-law college at Rutgers University and Princeton University, where he was an All-American football player and a member of several clubs and societies, including the Quadrangle Club, the Cloister Club, the Baltimore and Oxford Clubs and the Alliance Francaise. Raymond Harry Brown (31 August 1899 – 9 May 1970) was an Australia zoologist who worked at Australian Museum, Sydney during the 1930s, mainly on entomology. He was born in Warrnambool, Victoria and, as a teenager, he attended Melbourne University. He drew and painted Australian native fauna and flora, particularly butterflies. Raymond Harry Brown (1918 – 2015) was an Australian engineer best known for his invention of the speed camera (aka red light camera) and for his contributions to the design and construction of a range of large bridges, notable including Sydney's ANZAC Bridge. During his career, he worked on over 1000 significant engineering projects throughout Australia and the Pacific.

Born on 18 October 1918, Raymond was raised in the Sydney suburb of Balmain by his mother and single father, a meat contractor. After completing his studies in Sydney, Brown joined the firm in 1939, becoming a partner four years later.

In 1954, Brown invented the speed camera, a device designed to detect traffic violations by recording images in real time. However, the acceptance of the invention was initially hampered by its then unusual technology, and the fact that it had never been used in any major construction projects. He studied physics at the University of California, Berkeley, and worked at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory.

Brown's invention of the Brown Box was a major milestone in the history of video games.
The Brown Box was a console that could be connected to a television set and used to play a variety of games.
It was the first console to use interchangeable game cartridges, allowing users to play different games without having to buy a new console.
Brown's invention was the precursor to the modern video game console, and it paved the way for the development of the gaming industry.

Wolfe was linked to the Lost Generation of the 1920s and to American expatriate writers living in Paris.

Thomas Clayton Wolfe (October 3, 1900 – September 15, 1938) was an American novelist of the early twentieth century. His electric and often autobiographical writing style mixed highlyoriginal, poetic, rhapsodic, and impressionistic prose with autobiographical writing. Wolfe wrote four lengthy novels, as well as many short stories and novellas. He is known for his first novel, Look Homeward, Angel (1929). This novel captured the attention of H. L. Mencken, who encouraged Wolfe to become a full-time writer, which led to Wolfe's highly acclaimed autobiographical novel, Of Time and the River (1935).

Wolfe was influenced by Plato, William Shakespeare, Charles Dickens, Henry James, and other great writers of the 19th and early 20th centuries. He also was drawn to utopian and socialist ideals, evident in his 1933 collection of short stories From Death to Morning, and in his 1935 novel, Of Time and the River. The title of this novel attempts to explain Wolfe's personal philosophy of life.

main_passage: Casey Serin (born February 11, 1984) is an American blogger and former real estate investor. He gained notoriety in 2006 when he began blogging about his experiences as a real estate investor. Serin purchased eight properties in Arizona, California, and Virginia between 2004 and 2006, using a combination of subprime and stated income loans. He quickly became overwhelmed by the debt and was unable to make payments on the mortgages. He declared bankruptcy in 2008. Serin's blog, which he began in 2006, documented his experiences with real estate investing and his attempts to avoid foreclosure. He also wrote about his personal life, including his marriage and the birth of his daughter. His blog was widely read and attracted the attention of the media, who reported on his financial struggles.

In 2006, he rose to fame by blogging about his attempts to manage eight single-family rental houses he had purchased earlier that year on an aggressive timetable with no experience in the real estate industry. On his blog, he documented his successes and failures of flipping houses in detail. He quickly obtained a cult-like following by utilizing his blog as a platform to gain advice from fellow real estate investors. In 2010, he announced he was closing his blog due to personal reasons and has since remained out of the public eye."

Casey Serin (born October 24, 1985 in  Uzbekistan) is a former real estate investor from Phoenix, Arizona. He became notorious in the 2006–2007 U.S. housing bubble after purchasing eight properties in the span of two years with no income and little savings. After financing the properties through payments derived from home equity lines of credit secured on other properties, Serin was unable to maintain regular payments on several of his mortgages due to missed payments, foreclosures and subsequent bankruptcies. As of 2008, Serin had gained notoriety among real estate and business blogs. He has also been criticized for his naiveté and lack of financial planning during this process. Serin's story of his failed investing has been characterized numerous times in the mainstream press, including The Wall Street Journal, The New York Times, and Slate magazine.

He also documents his experiences with bankruptcy, lawsuits, tax liens, and various other money issues. Casey Serin (born October 24, 1982) is an American former real estate investor, blogger and convicted fraudster. He rose to notoriety in 2006 when he began documenting his struggles with his mortgage investments on his personal blog, the most common of which involved trying to find buyers desperate enough to take over his mortgages with little-to-no money down. His blog was widely followed and commented on by his readers as it chronicled his numerous financial missteps. In 2007, he pleaded guilty to eight counts of wire fraud and one count of bankruptcy fraud and was sentenced to eight years in federal prison. As of April 2019, Serin was released from prison and is now serving out his sentence on supervised release with the United States Probation Office in the state of California.

Casey Serin (born February 10, 1983) is an American blogger who gained notoriety for his blogs chronicling his real-estate investing woes in the mid-2000s. Serin began to invest in real estate in 2005 at the age of 22, and his blog posts (which he writes under the pseudonym "Casey") describe his various efforts and sometimes humorous missteps in real estate investing, including attempts to acquire and/or sell investment properties with many types of creative financing. In 2007, Serin's plight and writing style attracted considerable public attention, with some deeming it a cautionary tale while others deriding him.

In addition to his public blog, Serin maintains an instructional blog with lessons on real estate investment, called Diary of a Madder Man. Serin's real estate ventures and blogs were featured in the 2008 documentary I'm Filthy Rich.

Casey Serin (born August 27, 1985) is an American real estate entrepreneur, blogger, and internet personality. In 2006, at the age of 21, he gained notoriety after announcing his plan to purchase and flip eight houses in eight different cities by the age of 25. Over the next two years, his blog documented his progress as his dreams were derailed by foreclosure, legal troubles, bankruptcies, and marital problems. The blog, which was read by millions, quickly became an online phenomenon, earning him the nickname "The Subprime Blogger". His ongoing financial crisis has been featured by The Washington Post, The New York Times, the BBC, Fox News, Time Magazine, and a variety of other high-profile media outlets. In early 2008, Serin was featured in a series of videos for ABC News's Nightline. As of 2020, Serin is still facing financial hardship and his real estate projects remain unfinished.

Casey Serin (born October 17, 1985) is an American entrepreneur who gained notoriety in 2006 for his blog, I Am Facing Foreclosure, detailing his experiences in over-extending himself with an aggressive purchasing strategy of eight individual rental properties totaling $2.2 million in debt. Following numerous bank repossessions, he wrote, "I Have Failed", effectively ending his blog. In 2007, Serin was featured in an ABC Primetime Live story, which explored his motives, background, and extreme debt liability. On the show Serin discussed his successes and failures and defended some of the choices which had put him in his situation. After receiving backlash from the show, Serin's blog focused less on his debt and more on spirituality and personal struggles, eventually ending in 2008.

Casey Serin (born1983) is an American blogger and real estate investor. He gained notoriety in 2006 when he began to blog on his blog IAmFacingForeclosure.com about his struggles with purchasing eight properties in three cities in two countries with a stated goal of becoming a real estate millionaire. The blog followed his efforts over the next several years, chronicling what he called "the downfall of an aspiring real estate mogul." Ultimately, he defaulted on all of his loans and was foreclosed upon. Subsequent to his dilapidation, Serin created several other blogs to chronicle his life and experiences. The story of Serin's misfortune drew substantial attention from the media, including an appearance on The Oprah Winfrey Show and was covered by many media outlets including The New York Times, The Washington Post, Newsweek, and Entrepreneur.

Casey Serin (born December 14, 1983) is an American entrepreneur who has gained notoriety through his blog, I Am Facing Foreclosure. Serin principally rose to prominence after revealing via his blog in 2005 that he had purchased eight houses in eight different cities within an eight month period using low-ball, no money down offers. Serin subsequently lost most of the houses to foreclosure due to the severe financial strain placed on him by the high monthly payments he had taken on. Additionally, his mortgages did not have all the relevant fees included, creating several problems. He testified in a United States congressional hearing in 2006 hearing on predatory lending.

The blog chronicles his attempts to negotiate with banks to refinance loans, and Serin's activism attempts to end predatory lending. In March 2007, Serin announced he was no longer facing foreclosure and had sold some of his properties and refinanced others.

Casey Serin (born September 13, 1985) is a former American real estate investor and blogger who, starting in 2004, purchased eight houses with little or no money down, only to default on all of them. After his story became publicly known in 2006, Serin received widespread media attention for his real estate speculation and financial situation. He became a minor celebrity, with his story making the home pages of Digg and Yahoo!.

Casey Serin (born 1981) is a former U.S. real estate investor and blogger, based in Las Vegas, Nevada. His notoriety has stemmed from his blog, IAmFacingForeclosure.com, which documents his experiences dealing with subprime mortgage debt from a consumer point of view. Serin has since gained international fame, with fans and detractors alike.

The blog, which began in October 2006, quickly drew attention from major news outlets, including The Washington Post, The New York Times and Fox News. Serin had also been featured on various radio programs, including "This American Life" and "Now on Air". His story has also been widely discussed online, for example, on Maclean's Magazine.

Casey Serin (born July 23, 1985) is an American blogger who came to notoriety in 2006 for borrowing nearly $2 million to purchase eight residential properties in Phoenix and Las Vegas without having a steady income to service the mortgages. He blogged about his experiences, often criticizing the mortgage lenders he was working with, and stopping payments on the mortgages with the hope of pursuing a loan modification. He was charged with multiple counts of fraud and tax evasion in July 2014 and pleaded guilty in March 2015. He was sentenced in May 2015 to two years of probation on fraud and tax charges.

Casey Serin (born December 7, 1982), is an Internet entrepreneur and blogger from Sacramento, California. He rose to global notoriety in 2006 after launching his now-defunct blog, "The True Story of Casey Serin...", where he chronicled his efforts to purchase real estate in multiple states around the United States on a shoestring budget. His attempts often ended in failure and were documented openly, detailing his joys, successes, and tribulations on a daily basis. His blog at its peak was updated nearly 20 times a day and read by an estimated 200,000 viewers per day.

Casey Serin (born October 16, 1984) is an American Internet entrepreneur and blogger. He is best known for his blog, I Am Facing Foreclosure, in which he detailed his struggles with numerous mortgage loans obtained in a short period of time. Serin earned notoriety within the blogging community as one of the first Internet personalities to become famous for sharing his personal life, in particular his financial issues, with the world. After being featured in numerous news publications, being interviewed by television networks, and inspiring a feature-length documentary, Serin retired from blogging in 2007 due to his personal financial issues and the overwhelmingly negative attention he was receiving from the public and media.

Casey Serin is an American blogger, software engineer and investor who gained notoriety over his popular personal blog, I Am Facing Foreclosure. Serin documented both his successes and failures in attempting to make millions of dollars in real estate. He wrote about his attempts to use various loans, including stated income, subject-to and other creative financing techniques to purchase and flip numerous properties simultaneously. His blog was widely read between 2007 and 2009, and was mentioned by major news media. Serin was declared bankrupt in 2008, and his blog became inactive in late 2009. He subsequently moved out of the United States and accepted a position as an engineer with a technology company in Malaysia.

Casey Serin is an Arizona real estate investor and blogger known for having purchased eight properties worth an estimated $7 million, only to default on nearly all of them and file for bankruptcy in 2008. Serin started buying properties at the age of 21 and posted details about his successes and struggles including foreclosure proceedings and eviction stories on his personal blog. His blog became popular due to his openness, and many readers called him "insane". His story was featured in ABC News, The Huffington Post, and the Wall Street Journal.

Casey Serin (born January 1980) is a former real estate investor turned blogger based in Las Vegas, Nevada. He is arguably the most well-known real estate investor in the so-called "subprime mortgage crisis" due to his extreme situation of having eight investment properties in four different states. Serin received press attention after he created a blog in which he detailed his predicament, documenting his daily life and struggles.

Having obtained multiple real estate mortgages with no down payment and no employment income, Serin soon found himself in debt and on the verge of foreclosure. In order to pay off his debt, Serin began "flipping" houses in 2006. Despite his efforts, Serin has yet to pay off any of his mortgages.

Serin's situation has been described as a cautionary tale by financial advisers, who warn people to avoid taking out such extreme loans and property investments. The blog also serves as a form of financial therapy and has gained a considerable following. His emotional struggles with debt and potential foreclosure have become widely known and gained worldwide attention.

Casey Serin (born 1984) is an American blogger, notorious for his online diary, I Am Facing Foreclosure, detailing his attempt to "flip" eight properties he purchased between late 2005 and 2006. Serin purchased homes in California, Arizona and Nevada with almost no money down, borrowed against one home to purchase another, and was, by mid-2006, unable to make payments on any of the mortgages. He faced eviction, bankruptcy, and has reportedly been the target of various lawsuits. He has become the subject of much media coverage, and has been referred to as a poster child of the subprime mortgage crisis.

main_passage: Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 哲三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, October 28, 1921 – April 28, 2018) was a Japanese professional Go player. He was born in Tokyo, Japan. He was the first professional Go player to reach the rank of 9 dan, and was the first to be awarded the title of Honorary Meijin. He was also the first to be awarded the title of Honorary Kisei. He was a prolific author, having written over 100 books on Go. He was also a teacher, having taught many of the top players in Japan. He was a member of the Nihon Ki-in, the Japanese Go Association, and was the president of the Kansai Ki-in, the Kansai branch of the Nihon Ki-in. He was also a member of the International Go Federation. He was awarded the Order of the Rising Sun in 2006. He died in April 2018 at the age of 96.

He was a renowned researcher of nonlinear wave equations. After receiving his PhD in 1954, he wrote the books 'Theory of Nonlinear Wave Motion' and 'Tsunami Wave Theory'.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本哲三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, July 29, 1927 – December 24, 2013) was a prominent figure in the world of Japanese architecture, noted for his large wooden structures. Iwamoto was a professor at Tokyo Institute of Technology and director of the Institute of Traditional Japanese Construction at Nagoya University.

Throughout his career, Iwamoto was responsible for more than 600 buildings, including a dozen railways stations, the Takamatsu Catholic Church, the award-winning Ōita Prefectural Library, and Japan's oldest wooden apartment buildings. His largest project was the Tsushima Grand Shrine. He also played an important role in preserving and restoring many of Japan's traditional structures, including the Great Buddha at Tōdai-ji and famed rice storehouses in Fukuoka.

Iwamoto was awarded multiple awards from both the Japanese government and private organizations, and was recognized as a Person of Cultural Merit in 2009. He was given numerous honorary doctorates from institutions such as Kyushu University, Mie University, and Nihon University.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本哲三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, 26 June 1895 – 16 December 1995) was a Japanese civil engineer who was credited with having advanced the theory of prestressed concrete. He was born in Suruga, Shizuoka Prefecture in Japan and is a graduate of the Department of Civil Engineering at the University of Tokyo.

Iwamoto's most significant accomplishment was setting the fundamental theories and approaches in prestressed concrete structural engineering. His works led to development of the theoretical imaging method of structural behavior and analysis, laying the theoretical foundation of prestressed concrete engineering with his newly proposed methods of calculating stress distribution, forces and moments of equilibrium. The main theoretical research outlined in his seminal work, Theories and Applications of Prestressed Concrete (1947), formed the basis of prestressed concrete theory and design procedures used throughout the world. It is considered the bible of prestressed concrete frameworks.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 鉄蔵, Iwamoto Tetsuzō; 21 May 1925 – 7 April 2012) was a Japanese edo and modern bonsai master. He was one of the most important personalities in the history of bonsai.

Born in Yamato, Nara, Japan, Iwamoto specialized in creating miniatures of trees and shrubs such as junipers, pines and azaleas, as well as working in various bonsai styles such as slant, Windswept and literati. He was most noted for his breathtakingly detailed small-size trees and his incredible technique in creating lifelike bonsai.

Iwamoto traveled around the world to share his knowledge of bonsai and was a professor of horticulture at Aoyama Gakuin University in Tokyo. He was awarded numerous awards and recognitions, including the Order of the Rising Sun of Japan for his contribution to bonsai. He was also made an honorary Fellow of the American Bonsai Society.

Tetsuzo Iwamoto (1899-1989) was a Japanese landscape architect and gardener. He was active in the construction of parks and gardens in Japan and overseas, having built more than 500 gardens since starting practice in 1926.

Iwamoto's signature style was to express abstract forms through the use of materials such as stones and dirt, and planting of trees, shrubs and flowers, producing designs that have been praised for their beauty. His art was particularly noted for its naturalism, with Japanese gardens emphasizing the connections between humans and nature.

Iwamoto was awarded numerous honors during his lifetime, including Japan’s Order of Culture in 1963 and the Order of the Sacred Treasures in 1974. He was also inducted into the Order of the Rising Sun in 1978. He is recognized as one of Japan's greatest landscape architects, and is credited with introducing modern landscape design to Japan.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 徹三 Iwamoto Tetsuzō, 3 November 1910 – 28 August 1984) was a Japanese shogi player, considered one of the most successful in the history of the game. Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 哲三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, August 29, 1922 – October 16, 2008) was a Japanese Go player. He was the second male professional to reach 8-dan rank, and was awarded the rank of Honorary 9-dan by the Japanese Go Association shortly before his death. Iwamoto developed a teaching style for Go that focused on fun and enjoyment of the game, and made it widely popular among young children and adults alike.

He is widely considered one of the most influential Go players in Japan and credited with bringing the game to a wider audience. His engagements with the media, both on a show he co-hosted and in his own books, have been credited with further popularizing the game. He was also one of the lead characters in the manga series "Hikaru no Go".

At age 86, Iwamoto returned to Tokyo to teach Go at the Japanese Go Association, and conducted open lectures and courses. He died of a stroke at age 86 in October 2008.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 哲三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, 25 January 1903 – 7 June 1956) was a Japanese architect and professor of architectural history at Tokyo Institute of Technology. He gained fame for his adoption and use of modernist and internationalist architecture styles, in the 1940s and 1950s. He was a prominent proponent of the Metabolism architectural movement, and strongly believed in finding a new and revolutionary aesthetic for modern Japan. 

He was born in Hiroshima, the son of a wealthy merchant family. Originally desiring to pursue a career in fine arts, Iwamoto studied architecture at Tokyo Imperial University, in 1920. One of his designs was awarded a prize at the Imperial University Exhibition in 1925, and his his graduation in 1927 led to his recognition as a rising star in the industry. After graduating, he remained in Tokyo and opened his own firm, becoming the leading architect of his generation.

Iwamoto's early works incorporated traditional Japanese architectural motifs, such as the use of tatami mats, sliding doors and darkwood beams, in modern Westernized designs

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 徹三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō), (September 7, 1922 – August 6, 2018) was a Japanese sumo wrestler and coach. A native of Iwamizawa, Hokkaidō and the eldest son of the 14th yokozuna Maedayama, he was a prominent wrestler in the 1940s becoming a tsukedashi, or wrestler admitted straight into the jūryō division, in 1945 and reaching a highest rank of ōzeki in 1947. He left active competition in 1950 but returned to the sumo world in 1957 when he began a long career as a coach, first for the Dewanoumi stable and later for the Azumazeki stable, working with yokozuna Takanohana and Ōnokuni. Iwamoto was renowned for his fiery temper and distinctive pink kimonos. He was awarded the distinction of kin'sei, or "shining star," by the Japan Sumo Association in 2008. He died from cardiac failure at the age of 95.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 鉄造, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, 23 October 1896 – 19 June 1977) was a Japanese artist, sculptor, illustrator, set designer, and art educator. In the 1930s Iwamoto was a leader of the creative and intellectual world of avant-garde art. He was a major proponent of the modernist-oriented Mavo artist collective between 1928 and 1931, and his stylistically controversial sculptures and set designs pushed the boundaries of visual expression. Between 1945 and 1947, Iwamoto joined the Faculty at Tokyo University of the Arts where he taught a wide range of design classes such as oil painting, sculpture and photography. After retirement, Iwamoto was presented several official awards for his work in art and culture in the 1970s. Iwamoto's artistic works remain influential today, with the works of Tatsuo Miyajima, Ichi Ikeda, and Makoto Saito frequently referencing his influence.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (1920 – 2006) was a Japanese mathematician best known for his work on the theory of ordinary differential equations. He developed the Iwamoto-Konopelchenko Theorems which provide a method of reducing a nonlinear partial differential equation to a known linear equation. Iwamoto is also known for certain inequalities in approximation theory and Nevanlinna theory and for his book "Linear Differential Equations with ... Constant Coefficients."

Throughout his career, Iwamoto served as professor at a number of universities including the University of Tokyo, Osaka University, Kyushu University and Yamaguchi University. He was president of the Mathematical Society of Japan from 1984 to 1986 and was widely recognized for his numerous contributions to mathematics. In 1988, he was awarded the Order of Culture from the Japanese government. Iwamoto passed away in 2006 at the age of 86.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 哲三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, April 19, 1898 – June 18, 1983) was a Japanese craftmaker, especially in woodworking and lacquerware. He is considered the most important of next generation craftmakers, after the old generation of Meiji period craftsmen. He was elected a member of theJapan Art Academy in 1961 and in 1978 was designated a Living National Treasure for makie lacquer work. 

Iwamoto was born in 1898 in Tokyo, where he lived and worked for most of his life. After graduating from the Tokyo Prefecture Art School, he became an apprentice to Kakujirō Izumita in 1916. Iwamoto is best known for his makie art, which involves applying layers of lacquer and then decorating them with designs. Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 哲三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, 28 June 1878–17 October 1966) was a Japanese architect, painter, sculptor and author. He was born in Tokyo and was a notable architect and designer of haiku poetry and woodblock print books.

He worked for the Imperial Household Agency for five years before beginning his own architectural firm in 1910. During 1913–1915 he designed many of the new Imperial Hotel's exterior buildings in Tokyo and during 1936–1941 he designed its central building with his partner Watanabe Goichi. He served as the principal of the Tokyo Technical High School of Architecture during the 1930s and 1940s, and over the next two decades helped form the basis of the Institute of Environmental Architecture. He also served as an important figure in the development of modern Japanese architecture, popularizing the Japanese western-style architecture and introducing functional city planning in Japan. He wrote books, among them kaiga-no-teiban, that provided theoretical foundations to his artistic approach. Iwamoto’s works include the Hisaya Odori

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 徹三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, April 4, 1927 – August 8, 1998) was a Japanese professional sumo wrestler and coach from Kishiwada, Osaka Prefecture. Making his professional debut in January 1946, he rose to prominence during the postwar era of sumo, reaching the second highest makuuchi division in August 1955, where he competed for four tournaments, winning a total of forty-one bouts and earning two Kinboshi or gold stars for defeating the yokozuna at the Ryōgoku Kokugikan. A rebuilding project after the retirement of the great yokozuna Futabayama saw Iwamoto promoted to the highest grade of sekiwake for three tournaments from the latter part of 1956. He remained in the makuuchi division for eighteen years and over 300 tournaments, from his debut to his retirement in October 1965. He was posthumously inducted into the Japan Sumo Association's hall of fame in 2005.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (1894-1970) was a Japanese engineer, aviation pioneer and aviation industrialist who played a major role in the development of early aviation in Japan. He built the first fully all-metal aircraft in Japan and founded, or had a major role in, several major Japanese aircraft companies, including Kawasaki Heavy Industries, Nihon Aeroplane, and Nippon Airplane Factory. He also played a major role in the development of Japan's civil aviation industry.

A graduate of the Tokyo University of Science, Iwamoto's career in aviation began in 1916 when he was recruited by the Army Technical Bureau. He was tasked with the operations and maintenance of Japan's first two military aircrafts, the Farman IV and the Maurice Farman. In 1921, he was selected as one of five engineers to be sent to the United States by the Army Ministry to educate themselves in the aviation industry. During his time in the United States, Iwamoto earned his pilot's license and worked at a variety of aircraft companies as a mechanic and engineer. He also studied Japanese and English aeronautical textbooks.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本鉄蔵, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, April 26, 1894 – January 27, 1958) was a Japanese sculptor and ceramist active during the first half of the 20th century.

Iwamoto is considered one of the major practitioners of modern International Gothic art in Japan. He is well known for his bronze sculptors and ceramic pieces, which are notable for their traditional Japanese style as well as for his frequent use of gold. He studied painting and sculpture at the Tokyo School of Fine Arts, and was especially drawn to the European Gothic style, which he combined with classical Japanese art.

Iwamoto displayed a break from the predominant fashion of Japanese sculpture of the period, which favored the minimalist style of Japanese modern art. Unlike his contemporary sculptors, Iwamoto used a variety of techniques, including metalwork and stone carving, to create his sculptures. He used gold and other metal inlays to produce metal statues with a low relief that contrast with the smooth texture of his metal relief sculptures.

Tetsuzō Iwamoto (born Soroku Iwamoto, June 19, 1921 –May 17, 2015) was a Japanese yose, a form of traditional theatrical performance featuring comic dialogue and music. He was born in Kurashiki, Okayama Prefecture, Japan. He was awarded the Order of the Rising Sun, Gold Rays with Rosette in 2004.

Iwamoto was a student of Kajima Hōei (1887–1969), a famous yose performer and writer. Iwamoto himself made his debut in 1937, at the age of 16. He was a yose performer for more than sixty years, until his retirement in 1999. In addition to performing, Iwamoto wrote and translated many yose for himself and for other performers. He also composed many songs and musical pieces for yose.

sentences: Tetsuzō Iwamoto (岩本 哲三, Iwamoto Tetsuzō, October 28, 1921 – April 28, 2018) was a Japanese professional Go player.
He was born in Tokyo, Japan.
He was the first professional Go player to reach the rank of 9 dan, and was the first to be awarded the title of Honorary Meijin.
He was also the first to be awarded the title of Honorary Kisei.
He was a prolific author, having written over 100 books on Go.
He was also a teacher, having taught many of the top players in Japan.
He was a member of the Nihon Ki-in, the Japanese Go Association, and was the president of the Kansai Ki-in, the Kansai branch of the Nihon Ki-in.
He was also a member of the International Go Federation.
He was awarded the Order of the Rising Sun in 2006.
He died in April 2018 at the age of 96.

"Rusty Stevens (born Russell Stevens Jr., October 5, 1932 – October 24, 2000) was an American actor. He is best known for his role as Larry Mondello on the television series Leave It to Beaver.

Stevens was born in Los Angeles, California, the son of Russell Stevens Sr. and his wife, the former Dorothy M. Smith. He began his acting career at the age of seven, appearing in the 1939 film The Story of Alexander Graham Bell. He went on to appear in numerous films and television shows, including The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet, The Donna Reed Show, The Many Loves of Dobie Gillis, and The Monkees.

In 1957, Stevens was cast as Larry Mondello, the Beaver's best friend, on Leave It to Beaver. He appeared in all 234 episodes of the series, which ran until 1963. After the show ended, Stevens continued to act in films and television, appearing in such shows as The Munsters, The Monkees, and The Brady Bunch. He retired from acting in the late 1970s."

"Rusty Stevens (May 1, 1932 – October 25, 2000) was an American actor best known for his portrayal of Whitey Whitney in the 1950s sitcom Leave It to Beaver.

He began his acting career at age 14 when his singing and dancing talents landed him a spot on the NBC children's show Stanley's House. After a string of television and movie appearances, Stevens became a regular cast member on Leave It to Beaver from 1959 until 1963. He reprised the role of Whitey in the 1983 nostalgia movie based on the show and its revival series which aired from 1985–1989 on the Disney Channel and in syndication.

Outside of acting, Stevens ran the Rusty Stevens Charitable Foundation and the Rusty Stevens Fire Safety Foundation. He also worked as a licensed real estate agent in California for 20 years."

"Rusty Stevens (born December 11, 1950) is an American actor known for his childhood roles on television programs including Leave It to Beaver, Lassie, The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet, General Electric Theater, and a number of other 1950s and 1960s series.

Stevens is probably best known for his role as Gilbert Bates in the television comedy Leave It to Beaver (1957–1963). He became a member of the Screen Actors Guild at the age of seven. He also had a small role in the 1959 movie Rio Bravo, starring John Wayne and Dean Martin.

Stevens went on to do voice-over work in animated series before his teenage film career ended in 1968. He later worked in real estate and in the plastic manufacturing industry. In 1998 he reprised his role as Beaver's older brother Gilbert in the feature-length film Leave It to Beaver, which featured Eugene Levy in the role of Ward Cleaver. The film was based on the series of the same name."

Rusty Stevens (born February 17, 1934) is an American actor. He is best known for his work as Larry Mondello on the 1960s sitcom Leave It to Beaver. After appearing in a handful of other television series, Stevens retired in the late 1960s and has lived a reclusive life since.

Stevens originally reprised his role as Larry Mondello in the late 1980s Leave It to Beaver film, but was replaced at the last minute by Kipp Marcus. He was unhappy with the film but eventually reconciled with show star Jerry Mathers for a 1979 television documentary about the series. Stevens told Mathers that Leave It to Beaver was his happiest job, and he said he appreciated Mathers for helping him become an actor.

Rusty Stevens (born Roderick Stevens on October 15, 1932) is a veteran American film and television actor.

Stevens was born in Waukegan, Illinois and raised in Wilmette, a suburb of Chicago. He made his film debut at age 11 in the 1943 Paramount Pictures classic Four Jills in a Jeep as nephew Mike. 

Stevens also had notable roles in a number of classic television series including Leave It to Beaver (1957–1963), Have Gun Will Travel (1957–1963), Dennis the Menace (1959–1963), Gunsmoke (1958–1965), The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet (1959–1966), The Monkees (1966–1967), and Get Smart (1966–1969). "Rusty Stevens (1937-1966), born Russell Stevens, was an American actor best known as A.J. “Freddie” Torkleson, an orphan adopted by Sheriff Andy Taylor and his son Opie, in the long-running American television sitcom The Andy Griffith Show from 1963 to 1966. "Rusty Stevens (born Russell Edward Zacherek, June 10, 1948 – May 5, 2000) was an American actor. He was known for his role as Wendell Gibbons on the sitcom Leave It to Beaver.

He began his career at the age of 11 in the television series The Danny Thomas Show and went on to appear in other television programs throughout the 1960s and 1970s. Stevens was highly praised by his peers and was noted for having a "gentleness and strength". He was considered a scene-stealer due to his charismatic and intense performances onscreen.

Rusty Stevens was an American actor, who worked both on film and television. He was mostly known for playing young male ingénue types, particularly during the 1950s and 1960s.

Rusty Stevens (born Russell Sylvester Stevens, June 3, 1928 – August 13, 2013) was an American actor, most remembered for his role as Henry Edwards on the 1950s television series, Lassie. During the show's nine-year run, Stevens appeared in all but two of the series' episodes.

After Lassie, Stevens' other major television roles included the irascible woodworker Mr. McCleary on the 1957–1961 sitcom Leave It to Beaver and Roger Coogan on the 1960–1962 sitcom My Three Sons. However, he was never able to duplicate the success of his Lassie role.

Her documentary film Martin Luther King, Jr.: The Last Interview and Other Conversations (2009) was philanthropically supported in part by the National Endowment for the Humanities, Pacific Pioneer Fund, and New York State Council on the Arts. Kleine has also written, edited, and produced many other films that have garnered awards, including Joe Papp in Five Acts (2010), which was selected for the Sundance Film Festival, The Heroine of Heath Street (1992) and Private Confessions (1996). She is also the author of a biography of Andy Warhol (2006). Additionally, Kleine is an independent film curator, whose work includes curating the first retrospective of Laurel Nakadate at the IFC Center in 2006."

Cindy Kleine (born May 1, 1956) is an American filmmaker and visual artist. She is best known for her feature documentary "Andy Warhol: A Documentary Film". In 1988, she completed her first film chronicling her father Paul Kleine's struggle with Alzheimer's Disease. This project, titled "The Distance Between Us," earned Kleine a nomination for the Student Academy Awards. Since then, Kleine has gone on to make documentaries and narrative films such as "Alive from Off Center," "The Baby Weavers of Sialkot," "The Secret World of Dreams," and "Last Plea". Kleine was also one of the only directors to film and document the historic moment when the Berlin Wall came down in 1989. She later won an Emmy and a Peabody Award for her work on an NBC News Special honoring the event.

Cindy Kleine (born 1959) is an American documentary filmmaker, curator, art historian and author. She is best known for her documentary films and her focus on the lives of artists and the place of art and culture in the wider context of popular culture.

Kleine was born in 1959 in New York City. She attended Cornell University and graduated with a degree in art history in 1981. After her studies, Kleine spent five years as an arts administrator in New York and then began a career in filmmaking. Her first two films, Notes from Home (1986) and Aloise (1988), examine female–dad relationships. Kleine then directed The Promise (1992), her first feature-length documentary about the life of the German photographer Alma Haser.

Kleine has since worked as a curator, providing insight into a variety of movements and periods in modern art. In 2015 she curated the exhibitions Long Island Collects: 1960–2015 at Guild Hall and Robert Dash: Primary Structures at The Parrish Art Museum. Cindy Kleine is an American independent film and television director, producer, writer and editor, best known for her feature documentaries, most notably Andre Gregory: Before and After Dinner and Joey Breaker which earned her a Peabody Award nomination. Kleine is a three-time Emmy nominee and has collaborated many times with her husband, actor Wallace Shawn. Kleine is a frequent speaker and presider at numerous film festivals and events. She lives in New York City.

Cindy Kleine (born November 12, 1956) is an American independent filmmaker and independent curator. She is best known for her feature-length documentary about her parents, Alvin and Elaine. The film, titled Al + Elaine, had its world premiere at the 2020 Tribeca Film Festival and was nominated for the Grand Jury Prize. Kleine chronicles the dissolution of her parents’ marriage, the lives they led, and their commitment to their cultural heritage. Her father, an artist, actor, and civil rights activist, and her mother, an opera singer, both fought against racism and converted to Judaism. Her documentary explores the concept of cultural identity and the many ideas that define and inform it. Her other films include Relatively Close (2010), Art Is…The Permanent Revolution (2007), and Dear Suzan (2007). Her work has been shown at festivals and in galleries around the world and has been written about in publications such as The New York Times, The New Yorker, Film Comment, and more.

"Cindy Kleine (born October 3, 1952) is an American film director, producer and writer. Her films focus on intimate stories of personal relationships and the power of memory. Kleine is particularly drawn to exploring her own family's experiences and turning them into stories.

Kleine's first feature documentary film, Alvin Ailey: A Life in Dance (2006), was nominated for an Emmy Award for Outstanding Cultural Programming. It chronicles the life of the African-American dancer and choreographer Alvin Ailey. Cindy Kleine (born 1954) is an American filmmaker, archivist and archival curator. Cindy Kleine (born 1947) is an American documentary filmmaker, writer and artist. She has been creating documentary films, videos, digital media and writing since 1983. Cindy Kleine (born 1952) is an American independent filmmaker, producer and director. She is best known for her award-winning personal documentary films about her life and relationships. Cindy Kleine (born December 18, 1955) is an American film director, producer, and archivist, who specializes in making biography, art, human rights and culture documentaries. Geoff Griffin (born 28 August 1965) is an Australian former professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1980s and 1990s. He played for the South Sydney Rabbitohs, the Eastern Suburbs Roosters, the North Sydney Bears and the Balmain Tigers in the New South Wales Rugby League premiership.

Griffin was a talented player who was known for his speed and agility. He was a regular member of the South Sydney Rabbitohs team from 1985 to 1988, playing in the centres and on the wing. He was a part of the Rabbitohs' 1985 premiership-winning team, scoring a try in the grand final. He also represented New South Wales in the State of Origin series in 1986 and 1987.

In 1989, Griffin moved to the Eastern Suburbs Roosters, where he played for two seasons. He then moved to the North Sydney Bears in 1991, where he played for two seasons before joining the Balmain Tigers in 1993. He retired from the game at the end of the 1993 season.

His work was recognised with a citation from Queen Victoria. In addition, he and Jane were posthumously commemorated by the naming of three streets within the London Borough of Hackney: Mayhew Close, Jane Street, and Foster Street.

Mayhew Foster (1720–1778) was an American educator who is considered to be one of the first schoolmasters in New England in the 18th century. Foster was born in Connecticut in 1720 and educated at Yale College. He was a landmark figure in the development of education in rural New England, becoming the first schoolmaster in the region to publish a book of pedagogy and to train teachers in the art of teaching.

Foster also advocated for "common schools," which would provide a diversified education instead of only teaching students how to read and write. His efforts lead to the eventual establishment of schools in many towns in the region. Moreover, Foster’s students developed a kind of teaching method known as the Mayhew Method, or the New England Primer, which spread throughout the region.

In addition to his work as an educator, Foster was also a school architect, designing buildings, and a carpenter. His designs included a schoolhouse in West Springfield, Massachusetts, built in 1744. Foster also championed the separation of church and state in education, ahead of his time and in opposition to the traditional colonial approach.

Mayhew Foster (1781–1864) was an American farmer and politician from Newark, Delaware. He served in the Delaware General Assembly and was a leader in the Anti-Masonic Free-Soil Party.

Foster was born in 1781 in Newark; his father had emigrated to Delaware from Massachusetts in the 1760s. He was a farmer during his early years in Newark until he achieved greater political power in the late 1820s. From 1829 to 1831, he served in the Delaware General Assembly. During this time, Foster was an active participant in Delaware Free-Soil politics, opposing the Anti-Masonic movement.

In the 1830s, Foster became more active in the Free-Soil cause, helping to found the Anti-Masonic Free-Soil Party, which opposed the popular Andrew Jackson and the Democratic Party. Foster was a delegate to the 1832 National Free Soil Convention. He was not a candidate for re-election to the Delaware General Assembly in 1835, but remained politically active until his death in 1864.

Mayhew Foster (1744 – 11 May 1824) was an independent MP for Pembrokeshire from 1816 to 1820. He was the son of Henry Foster, a London merchant and a supporter of the American Colonies. Educated at Westminster School, Graham's School, and at Beaumont College (from which he was eventually expelled), Foster became a lawyer by profession. 

In politics, he was a reformer who was committed to the cause of parliamentary reform. He was a strong advocate of the freeing of slaves, as well as a proponent of the abolition of game laws, the introduction of equal taxation, and representation of the people. Heat of character and frankness of speech made him a leading speaker in the House of Commons, where he often came into conflict with the government of the day. He was also a strong advocate of peace, opposing wars with France and the United States. He was a patron of the arts and gifted to the Royal Academy of Music, Society of Arts and the London Institution.

Mayhew Foster (1750–1823) was an American religious leader, preacher, and spiritual writer of the Methodist Church, and one of the founders of the Free Will Baptist denomination. Born in New Hampshire, he was widely known for his piety and energetic ministry in 20 circuits in the eastern United States during the late 18th and early 19th centuries. His sermons inspired the beginnings of a spiritual revival in New England, and his reflections on the spiritual life have been praised. His popular works include The Preacher's Assistant (1801) and the widely printed Loyalty Preferred to Neutrality (1777). He died in 1823 in Albion, Maine.

Mayhew Foster (August 10, 1784 – February 10, 1854) was an American Abolitionist and social reformer who is best known for leading some of the earliest settlement houses in Boston. Born in Portsmouth, New Hampshire, foster traveled around the northern United States and Canada, preaching abolitionism before eventually settling in Boston. Over the course of his work with the abolitionist cause, he founded a settlement house in Southwest Boston, which later became the renowned Field's Corner Settlement House. He also opened a furniture business in West Boston with his friend and abolitionist Garrison Loring. Foster believed in empowering the poor and marginalized of Boston by providing them with educational opportunities, basic necessities, and work opportunities. He focused on communal living, economic self-sufficiency, and education, which he believed could bring dignity, confidence, and eradication of poverty. He passed away in 1854 and left an enduring legacy on the social work and reform movement in Boston.

Mayhew Foster (1810-1883) was English philanthropist and social reformer who devoted his life to improving the lives of the working classes in England. He was particularly concerned with improving the housing, sanitation and nutrition of the urban poor. He advocated government intervention in order to improve the situation of the working classes, and in 1851 insisted that with investment from state funds, the terrible poverty found in industrial towns could almost be eliminated.

He established the National Association for Promotion of Social Science to investigate the living conditions of urban dwellers, and the Commission for Sequestered Funds in 1867 to disburse charitable funds effectively. He was also a fierce advocate for the rights of workers, and promoted the movement for the reform of labour laws. He was an influential man in Victorian Britain, and his work is widely recognised as having set standards for future philanthropy and social reform.

Mayhew Foster (1727–1821) was an American clergyman and theologian. He was born in Andover, Massachusetts and graduated from Harvard College in 1751. In 1753 he was ordained pastor of the Congregational Church in Wells, Maine. He was pastor of several churches in different parts of Maine until his death in 1821. Foster was a great apostle of New England theology and was very influential in his day. He wrote several books and articles on theological subjects, including A Treatise on Preaching (1797), A Treatise on Communion (1798), and A Treatise on Baptism (1807), among others. He was also an advocate of free grace and believed in a sovereign and mighty God. He was passionate about the need to obey and live according to Scripture, and was devoted to social reforms. He often spoke out on behalf of the poor and oppressed, and advocated for Indian rights. He was known for his humility, loyalty to the truth, and love of peace.

Mayhew Foster (1737–1790) was an English philanthropist who gave refuge to about 100 destitute children in Baldock, Hertfordshire in the late eighteenth-century. Foster was the son of a yeoman of the village of Bardfield, Essex, and was born there in 1737. After his marriage, he established himself in Baldock and filled the role of a farmer, miller and village tradesman.

When his only son died in 1776, he became aware of the hardship endured by some of the village families, who often lacked resources and food to sustain their families. With the help of his wife he began to provide help by providing food and clothing. This charity was extended to nearby villages, with specific attention given to orphans, while he also provided schooling to the children. As word of his charitable works spread, more destitute children began seeking help, and in less than ten years he had taken in more than a hundred of them. Foster continued his work until his death in 1790, and it was estimated that he had relieved poverty for more than 3,000 people throughout his lifetime. He is remembered locally as a

Mayhew Foster (1740 – August 9, 1831) was an American abolitionist who, as a Quaker missionary, was a major figure in the abolitionist movement in America during the late 18th century and early 19th century. Serving as a conductor of the Underground Railroad and aiding escaped slaves in pursuit of freedom, Foster was an influential voice who received both popular and critical success in support of abolitionism and the Underground Railroad. Foster was one of the first prominent Americans to be openly against slavery, and his efforts to eradicate slavery, along with those of others, were a major driving force in the eventual passage of the Thirteenth Amendment of the United States Constitution, which abolished slavery in the United States.

Mayhew Foster (1801–1874) was an influential 19th-century American minister, abolitionist, and social reformer. He was born in Grafton, Massachusetts, and moved to Boston at the age of nine. He went to college and seminary at Andover and became a Congregational minister in 1827. He was initially pastor to the 2nd Congregational Society of Haverhill each week, a post he held until 1850.

He was a strong advocate of the antislavery movement and became one of the major figures in the Emergent Church. He propagated the notion of nonviolence and active nonresistance in lieu of physical force, such as removing oneself from the company of abusers to prevent further harm. Foster was a proponent of "creating and sustaining Christian communities, the nurture of a Christian character, and the growth and tenderness of the social bonds which characterize the Christian faith."

He was also a strenuous advocate of temperance and the total abolition of the liquor traffic, despite strong opposition from powerful organizations. His works include The Golden Rule, Reminiscences of Mayhew Foster,

Mayhew Foster (1799–1873) was an American social reformer and philanthropist from Massachusetts. He was a member of a Protestant evangelical group known as the Praying Indians and was active in promoting economic and social reforms in rural and urban communities of Massachusetts. 

He was born in Mendon and lived in Connecticut from 1808 to 1818. After attending the local grammar school, he returned to his hometown and taught school until 1821. He also operated a small farm and worked as a shoemaker for a time. He was an active member of the Baptist church and was involved in bible study and prayer meetings.

Foster embraced the idea of total abstinence from alcohol and sought to spread its gospel by founding temperance societies and by speaking publicly on the subject. He used his shoemaking tradesmanship to support himself while traveling and advocating for temperance and other reform measures. He also devoted his spare time to developing educational systems for the rural poor and assisting in numerous religious activities. 

He advocated for disabled veterans of the Civil War. He purchased a 20acre farm in Cheshire, Massachusetts, in 1846 and established the

"Mayhew Foster (1750–1823) was an English farmer and philanthropist who is remembered as a generous benefactor of charity. He was born in Hampshire, England, and farmed on his father's estate at Orsett, Essex. He became well-known throughout the county, in part because some of his charitable contributions were quite remarkable and also because he was a figure of great vigor and strength. He raised money for the support of numerous causes, especially those benefiting the local poor; among them were almshouses, day and Sunday schools, and the relief of clothing destitute people. Foster is commemorated on a memorial in Orsett and at St. Mary's Church, a parish church Foster frequently attended."

Mayhew Foster (1735 – April 5, 1806) was an Englishman born in the West Country of England. He was among the first settlers of Middletown, Connecticut, USA, and was an active public servant during the American War of Independence, where he rose to at least the rank of Major.

After the revolution, he served three terms as a Member of the Connecticut General Assembly. In politics, he was a moderate and influential Federalist. He was a charter member of the Ancient and Honorable Artillery Company of Massachusetts (as it was named before 1786) and a founder of Wesleyan University.

Mayhew Foster (September 26, 1726 – June 6, 1806) was an American physician, statesman, theologian and educator from Massachusetts. He graduated from Harvard College in 1745 and became a minister in Topsfield, Massachusetts, from 1750 to 1766. He then returned to Harvard to study medicine and received his M.D. in 1771. Foster then became Professor of Theology, Vice President and President of Harvard College from 1780-1783. He was also a member of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences and a delegate to the Massachusetts Constitutional Convention. He was instrumental in passing the 1780 State Constitution and served in the Massachusetts State Senate from 1780 to 1786. He is remembered today for his works in education, public service, and medicine.

Mayhew Foster (1738–1783) was an American clergyman and missionary, who became an active agent in the Society for Propagating the Gospel Among the Indians and Other Unconverted Tribes in North America. After arriving in New England in 1770, he moved to Falmouth, Maine (now Portland) and then to Pownalborough (now Dresden, Maine) where he was tasked with the mission of evangelization. He successfully formed several small congregations in Lincoln, Warren, Palmyra and other areas. He was a strong proponent of Indian education and was an instructor at the Indian school at St. Georges, Maine. He was highly respected by the Indians and was instrumental in the founding of a mission village in Dresden, Maine that is known to this day as "Indian Town". Foster also set out to protect the lands of the Indians, witnessed numerous land sales, and corresponded with leaders of both Indian and colonial communities. His efforts were successful in protecting Maine’s Indian tribes from the expanding colonial population, and his legacy is remembered to this day.

Mayhew Foster (October 16, 1814 – April 5, 1879) was an American journalist, temperance reformer, and editor who edited the American Telegraph, worked for the temperance reform magazine, The American Exchange and Review, and composed hymns. Foster was born in New Haven, Connecticut. He raised his family in Hartford, Connecticut. He was a passionate advocate of the temperance movement and was acknowledged as one of its earliest and most influential proponents in the Connecticut area.

Foster's disposition was to promote temperance but he was also an active promoter of other social reform movements of the time, including the abolition of slavery, the education of Christians, and peace throughout the land. Today, he is remembered as a pioneer of the successful temperance reform and is widely regarded as a major influence on the success of the temperance movement in Connecticut.

Mayhew Foster (1749 – November 6, 1783) was an American Revolutionary War captain from Mendon, Massachusetts. His service in the war is memorialized by a column placed in his memory at the Soldiers Memorial in Central Park in Worcester, Massachusetts.

He was born in Mendon, and likely saw service with local militia in the early days of the American Revolution. He was later commissioned into the Continental Army and rose to the rank of captain in 1781. Foster was stationed at Fort Mifflin in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania at the time of his death. Foster succumbed to cholera at the fort on November 6, 1783, shortly after the Treaty of Paris had been signed, essentially ending the Revolutionary War.

Mayhew Foster (1745 – 1816) was a Congregationalist minister, theologian, and one of the important figures in the Second Great Awakening. He was particularly associated with his revival meetings in the United States and Canada and was influential among those interested in the New Divinity theology.

Foster was born in East Haddam, Connecticut. He graduated from Yale in 1764 and two years later was ordained as a pastor at Lebanon, Connecticut, where he remained for 14 years before becoming a professor at Dartmouth College. He also was a noted educator at Williams College and also ran a seminary in Plainfield, Massachusetts. His ministry and revival meetings were a mixture of theological and practical applications of Christian life. He was widely known for his preaching on topics of sin and righteousness and his focus on leading a faithful life. He was so well-known in his time that his name was nearly synonymous with American revivals of the era.

Mayhew Foster (April 27, 1797 – April 7, 1847) was an American abolitionist, teacher, and lecturer, who devoted his life to the causes of abolition, temperance and women's rights. Born in Braintree, Massachusetts, he first became an abolitionist after witnessing the whipping of a slave in Missouri in 1820. He moved to Boston in 1823, where he enrolled in a church and taught school. From 1829 to 1836, he worked as a traveling agent for the Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society. During this period, he lectured in the northern states on the evils of slavery, and collected signatures for petitions to be sent to Congress. He also published several antislavery tracts, including a joint effort with Thomas Garrett called The Laws of Liberty and an autobiography, Memoirs of Mayhew S. Foster. From 1838 to 1843, he served as the first agent of the American and Foreign Anti-Slavery Society. In his later years, he was a leader in the temperance and women's rights movements.

He was born in London, the son of a Quaker merchant, and was educated at the Quaker school in Tottenham.
He became a minister in 1768 and was a prominent figure in the Quaker movement in England.
He was a strong advocate of the abolition of slavery and was a leader in the campaign to abolish the slave trade.
He was also a leader in the campaign for the relief of the poor, and was instrumental in the founding of the London Dispensary, a charitable organization that provided medical care to the poor.
He was also a strong advocate of education and was involved in the founding of several schools.
He was a prolific writer and published several books on religious and social topics.
He died in 1823 and is buried in the Quaker burial ground in Tottenham.

Merle Oberon (born Estelle Merle O'Brien Thompson; 19 February 1911 – 23 November 1979) was an Anglo-Indian actress. She began her film career in British films as Anne Boleyn in The Private Life of Henry VIII (1933). After her success in The Scarlet Pimpernel (1934), she travelled to the United States to make films for Samuel Goldwyn. She was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Actress for her performance in The Dark Angel (1935). 

Throughout her career, she appeared in a variety of films, including Wuthering Heights (1939), which earned her an Academy Award nomination for Best Actress. Other notable films include The Ghost Goes West (1935), The Citadel (1938), and The Lodger (1944). She received critical acclaim for her performances in The Bishop's Wife (1947) and I Want to Live! (1958). Her later years were marred by personal problems and illness. She died in 1979, aged 68.

"Merle Oberon (born Estelle Merle O'Brien Thompson; 19 February 1911 – 23 November 1979) was an Anglo-Indian actress. She began her film career in British films as Anne Boleyn in The Private Life of Henry VIII (1933). Merle Oberon (19 February 1911 – 23 November 1979) was a movie star of the 1930s, best known for her performance in The Scarlet Pimpernel (1934). She had a successful career in Hollywood, making films for both RKO Pictures and MGM. She was born Estelle Merle O'Brien Thompson in India to an Irish father and a half-Indian mother, with some sources stating the father was Welsh. Oberon had a difficult and chaotic childhood and was sent to live with foster parents at the age of six. She stated that her foster parents had adopted her, but biographers have established this was not the case. She traveled through Europe as a young woman, working as a chorus girl and apprentice actress. Oberon first gained fame on the London stage, appearing in a series of plays by Noël Coward. She made her first Hollywood film in 1929, and after five years of mixed success, she won critical acclaim for the 1934 screen adaptation of The Scarlet Pimpernel. Oberon continued to appear in films throughout the rest of her career.

Oberon's later years were marred by a series of personal problems, marriages, and health issues, and her last major film appearance was in The Lunatic Moon in 1960. She died in 1979.

Her other notable films include The Scarlet Empress (1934), The Band Wagon (1953), and Taras Bulba (1962). She was also renowned for her beauty, which led to her being called "the most beautiful woman in the world”.

Merle Oberon (19 February 1911 – 23 November 1979) was an Anglo-Indian actress. She began her career in British films as Anne Boleyn in The Private Life of Henry VIII (1933). After her success in The Scarlet Pimpernel (1934), she was cast in social dramas, such as The Rise of Catherine the Great (1934) and Things to Come (1936). Merle Oberon (19 February 1911 – 23 November 1979) was an Anglo-Indian actress. She began her film career in British films as Anne Boleyn in The Private Life of Henry VIII (1933). Oberon was considered one of the most beautiful actresses of her time, and was often described as “exotic” looking. In later life, Oberon suffered financially and was dogged by ill health. She also had a troubled personal life, surviving two suicide attempts and a number of broken marriages and relationships. "Merle Oberon (born Estelle Merle O'Brien Thompson; 19 February 1911 – 23 November 1979) was an Anglo-Indian actress who began her film career in British films as Anne Boleyn in The Private Life of Henry VIII (1933). Merle Oberon (19 February 1911 – 23 November 1979) was an Anglo-Indian actor. Born Estelle Merle O'Brien Thompson in India to an Indian-born father and an English mother, she began a brief stage career in India before travelling to the United Kingdom in 1930. She soon achieved success as an actress in British films, and after the success of her 1935 film The Scarlet Pimpernel became an international star. She had an affair with Lord Louis Mountbatten, who was married and served with distinction in World War II.

main_passage: Hephaestion (Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων, Hēphaistíōn; c. 356 BC – 324 BC), son of Amyntor, was a Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great. He was "by far the dearest of all the king's friends; he had been brought up with Alexander and shared all his secrets." This friendship was compared, by others as well as themselves, to that of Achilles and Patroclus. His military career was distinguished. He was the first to scale the walls of Tyre in 332 BC, which was the primary cause of the fall of the city. He was also the first to conquer the Malli in India in 326 BC. He was also in command of the Companion cavalry on the left wing in the Battle of Gaugamela in 331 BC. He died of illness in 324 BC.
sample_passages: Hephaestion (Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων; c. 356 BC–324 BC) was an ancient Macedonian nobleman and a trusted friend, bodyguard and general of Alexander the Great. He was "by far the dearest of all the king's friends; he had been brought up with Alexander and shared all his secrets" – whenever Alexander wanted something to be done, he entrusted it to Hephaestion. As a sign of favour, Alexander's father, Philip II of Macedon, had granted him the government of the semi-independent north-western region of Macedonia, while Alexander was away at war. Hephaestion held influential positions in Alexander's administration and distinguished himself several times in battle.

Hephaestion (c. 356 BC – 324 BC), son of Amyntor, was a Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great. He was arguably Alexander's closest friend and confidant; they were both taught by Aristotle and were close companions throughout Alexander's ambitious campaigns - both were veterans of the Battle of Granicus (334 BC), the first major battle Alexander fought in Asia. He was entrusted with supreme command of the Companion cavalry, served as Alexander's second-in-command, and was personally selected by Alexander in many of the key battles. Although popularly known as a close friend of Alexander, the two distinctive and separate historical figures have been confused in popular culture.

He was killed at the Battle of the Hydaspes in India in 324 BC, after being wounded by an arrow that became infected. After his death, he received divine status as a hero and his body was allegedly bound in beeswax and shipped back to Macedonia for burial in the royal tombs at Vergina. It is widely believed that Alexander's death was linked to his deep grief over the death of Hephaestion.

Hephaestion (Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων, Hēphaistíōn) was a son of Amyntor, a Macedonian noble. He was a close companion and general of Alexander the Great and was by Alexander's side in Naples when news of Philip II's death reached them in 336 BC. Alexander leapt to his feet, and immediately took over from his father, and dedicated a lion to the deceased king.

Hephaestion was an important figure in Alexander's life, and the two became close friends, joining forces in the first battle of their campaign against the Persian Empire. They were known to be so close that it was rumored they could read each other's thoughts, and it appears their relationship grew stronger as they conquered more and more. When Alexander died in Babylon, Hephaestion greatly mourned his friend's death, and swore to continue his campaigns as Alexander had wished. Shortly after, Hephaestion died himself.

Hephaestion (c. 356 BC – 324 BC) was a Macedonian nobleman and a closest companion of Alexander the Great. He was the son of Amyntor, a Macedonian nobleman, and his wife, Ortheia. He was the companion of Alexander until the end of his life, having been brought up with Alexander, having shared his earliest lessons, and having been trained as a cavalry officer by the father of Alexander's future companion and historian, Callisthenes. It is possible that Philip II gave them a joint hegemony over the corinthian district of Akarnania when Alexander left for Asia. 

Hephaestion's strength and general good disposition, combined with his close friendship with Alexander and the high favor he enjoyed with him, made him one of the most powerful men in the Macedonian court. He was among the officers who were sent to request Cleopatra Thea of Egypt to join a coalition against the Perdiccas government, and stayed with her for some time during that mission. In the Partition of Babylon, he was appointed by Alexander as satrap of this province.

Hephaestion (c. 356 BC – 324 BC), son of Amyntor, was a Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great. He was 'by far the dearest of all the friends' of Alexander and his 'beloved comrade', and was entrusted with supreme command of the Companion Cavalry, the elite assault force of the Macedonian kingdom. He was part of the royal bodyguard of Alexander the Great and was even presented with a strand of the King's hair when it was cut during the memorial of the Cabeiri festival. At the time of his accidental death in 324 BC, he was second in command of the military successes of Alexander and was given a similar funeral.

Hephaestion (Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων; d. 324 BC), son of Amyntor, was an ancient Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great. What is known is that he was much-loved by Alexander, as well as many of the other important figures in the Macedonian court.

Hephaestion (Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων, Hēphaistíōn, c. 356 BC – 324 BC) was a Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great. Alexander refers to him as the "lovliest of mortals."

Hephaestion (Greek: Ἡφαίστιον, c. 355 – 324 BC), son of Amyntor, was a Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great. He was "a nobleman of high birth and remarkable beauty" (Arrian, Anabasis Alexandri 5.1); he was "the handsomest man in the whole of Greece" (Curtius, 10.9.27). He was "the dearest of all the friends" of Alexander (Curtius 8.6.23). He died at the age of thirty-two, after receiving an honorable burial at Babylon in 324 BC.

Hephaestion (; Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων, Hēphaistíōn; c. 356 BC – 324 BC), son of Amyntor, was a Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great. He was "...by far the dearest of all the king's friends; he had been shared with him all his secrets, and was called by him 'brother'". He was distinguished for his bravery in battle and his friendship with the king, and shows up in several shared scenes of the Alexander Romance, a hagiography composed hundreds of years after his death. Most historians accept that Alexander and Hephaestion were distinct historical figures and that they were indeed mutual friends. However, their relationship was closer than that of ordinary friends and it is believed that they might have been lovers.

Hephaestion (Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων, Hêphaistíôn; c. 356 BC–324 BC) was a Macedonian nobleman and close friend of Alexander the Great. Although there is little information about his early life and education, he was an intelligent man who distinguished himself as a military commander of Alexander the Great's multi-cultural army during his reign from 336–323 BC. He became a member of the royal bodyguard of Philip II of Macedon and a friend of Alexander the Great. After Alexander's death in 323 BC, he was given the title of chiliarch and honored as the dearest friend of the king. He was very wealthy and held high command in the Macedonian army during the reign of Alexander the Great. His certain death at the Battle of the Hydaspes in 324 BC drastically reduced the influence of the Macedonian nobility.

Hephaestion (Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων; c. 356 BC – 324 BC), son of Amyntor, was a Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great. He was born in 356 BC and was same age as Alexander. He was taught in the same school as Alexander and was appointed as the cavalry commander of the Companion cavalry, a military unit of the Macedonian army. He shared the same tent as Alexander during their campaigns. Their closeness was such that Arrian (The Campaigns of Alexander) records that even in the face of danger from enemy forces, "The two friends were so inseparable that Alexander neglected his other Companions and went off with Hephaestion on his own. They became lifelong companions." They sacrificed to the gods together and they sent offerings to each other in token of their love.
sentences: Hephaestion (Ancient Greek: Ἡφαιστίων, Hēphaistíōn; c. 356 BC – 324 BC), son of Amyntor, was a Macedonian nobleman and a general in the army of Alexander the Great.
He was "by far the dearest of all the king's friends; he had been brought up with Alexander and shared all his secrets."
This friendship was compared, by others as well as themselves, to that of Achilles and Patroclus.
His military career was distinguished.
He was the first to scale the walls of Tyre in 332 BC, which was the primary cause of the fall of the city.
He was also the first to conquer the Malli in India in 326 BC.
He was also in command of the Companion cavalry on the left wing in the Battle of Gaugamela in 331 BC.
He died of illness in 324 BC.

John Vallely (born April 28, 1947) is an American former professional basketball player. He played in the National Basketball Association (NBA) from 1970 to 1980 as a guard for the Los Angeles Lakers, Atlanta Hawks, and Houston Rockets.

Vallely was born in Los Angeles, California. He attended the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA), where he was a member of the school's 1969–70 NCAA championship team. He was selected by the Lakers in the second round of the 1970 NBA draft. Vallely played in the NBA for 10 seasons, averaging 8.2 points, 3.2 assists, and 2.2 rebounds per game. He was a member of the Lakers' 1972–73 championship team and was a part of the Rockets' 1979–80 championship team.

Vallely retired from the NBA in 1980 and returned to UCLA to complete his degree in sociology. He later worked as a broadcaster for the Lakers and as a scout for the Rockets. He was inducted into the UCLA Athletics Hall of Fame in 2003.

Virginia Bottomley, Baroness Bottomley of Nettlestone, PC, DL (born 12 May 1948) is a British Conservative Party politician. She was a Member of Parliament (MP) from 1984 to 2005, and held several ministerial posts in the government of John Major, including Secretary of State for Health (1992–95) and Secretary of State for National Heritage (1995–97). She was made a life peer in 2005.

Bottomley was born in London and educated at the independent Putney High School and the University of Leicester, where she graduated with a degree in economics. She worked as a management consultant before entering politics. She was elected as MP for South West Surrey in 1984, and served as a junior minister in the Department of Health from 1989 to 1992. In 1992, she was promoted to Secretary of State for Health, a post she held until 1995. She then served as Secretary of State for National Heritage until 1997. After the 1997 general election, she returned to the backbenches. She stood down from the House of Commons at the 2005 general election.

She has twice been vice-chairman of the Conservative Party, in 2002 and again during David Cameron's period as party leader.

After local government experience in Bedfordshire, she was Member of Parliament for South West Surrey from 1984 to 2005, becoming Minister of State for Health in 1992, where she introduced the Disability Discrimination Act, and then Secretary of State for National Heritage and Minister for Women in 1995. 

In 2005 Bottomley was created a life peer as Baroness Bottomley of Nettlestone in the County of Hampshire. She was appointed a Privy Counsellor in 1993 and a Deputy Lieutenant of Surrey in 2009.

She was made a life peer as Baroness Bottomley of Nettlestone in the County of Isle of Wight in 2005 and sits in the House of Lords. Outside of politics, she has held a variety of roles in business and education.

Virginia Elizabeth Bottomley, Baroness Bottomley of Nettlestone, PC DL (born 3 April 1941) is a British Conservative Party politician who served as Secretary of State for Health from 1992 to 1995 and then as Secretary of State for National Heritage from 1995 to 1997. Bottomley was born in Bridlington, East Riding of Yorkshire. She was Moderator of the United Reformed Church's General Assembly in 1979.
