Bane is an American hardcore punk band from Washington, D.C. that formed in 1995. The band consisted of Aaron Bedard, Zach "Barney" Defeo, Rob Naylor, and Aaron Dalbec. The band's most prominent member was Bedard, who was credited with forming the band and writing most of their songs.

Bane primarily played straight edge hardcore punk, combining elements of melodic hardcore and youth crew. The band was known for its passionate lyrics, which often tackled social issues, especially animal rights and anti-war themes. In addition, the band was also known for their energetic and cathartic live shows. They toured extensively from 1998 to 2005 and released a string of albums and EPs on Equal Vision, Underestimated and Bridge 9 Records. In 2005, after releasing Do or Die and Final Backpatch, the band broke up. They reunited in 2010 and have been touring and writing music since.

Bane (sometimes stylized as BANE) is an American hardcore punk band from New Jersey. Formed in 1995, the band consists of Aaron Bedard (vocals), Bobby Rich (drums), Rob Moran (guitar and backing vocals), Zach Jordan (guitar), and Damian Michniewicz (bass). The band has released four full-length albums, Thirty Pieces and It All Comes Out, Holding This Moment, The Note and Don't Wait Up, and The Depth Of My Reason, as well as several split EPs and singles. Bane has toured extensively in the United States and Europe, playing as chaperones on Warped Tour and several independent tours including their well-known Last Show tour. The band disbanded in 2013, but has since announced a reunion show in October 2018.

Bane is a punk rock band from East Orange, New Jersey, USA, formed in 1995. The band has played a significant role in the development of the melodic hardcore and post-hardcore genres, and have served as a platform for the initiation of melodic and politically charged riffs, emerging in hardcore punk. The band has toured continuously since their inception, performing at international music festivals such as Warped Tour, Groezrock, Resurrection, and Keep It True. In addition, Bane has released four full-length studio albums and two extended plays, along with four compilations, five videos, and vinyl releases.

Bane is a Hardcore/Metalcore band from Washington, D.C., formed in 1995. The band has featured members of bands such as Ignite, The Suicide File, and Kid Dynamite. Over its lifespan, the band has gone through lineup changes, and its sound has shifted from melodic hardcore to metalcore and back again. Its earliest works, such as The Note and It All Comes Down to This, featured typical "East Coast" hardcore punk/emo-influenced vocal delivery as well as heavier, more metallic-sounding riffs. Bane's later work, especially in their albums Holding This Moment and The Note, incorporate a much more metallic-sounding drumming, and a heavily distorted, dissonant guitar style. Bane has become one of the most influential bands in the hardcore and metalcore genres, inspiring generations of newer hardcore and metalcore bands such as Every Time I Die and Comeback Kid.

Bane is a hardcore punk band formed in 1995 in Washington, D.C. The band disbanded in 2002, but reunited in 2010, playing live again and releasing new material. Musically, Bane has been described as "melodic hardcore" or "emotional hardcore," owing to the band's combination of aggressive punk and metal-influenced hardcore and the heartfelt and often confessional lyrical content. The band has toured and recorded alongside acts such as AFI, Alexisonfire, Comeback Kid, Hot Water Music, Strung Out, He Is Legend, and The Hope Conspiracy. At their shows, Bane is known for their powerful live performance and intense stage diving.

Bane is a hardcore punk band from Fort Lauderdale, Florida. Formed in 1995, the group is composed of vocalist Aaron Bedard, guitarists Zach Jordan and Aaron Dalbec, bassist Damien Moyal and drummer Bill Martin. The band has released four full-length studio albums, nine extended plays, one split EP and a number of compilation and live performances.

Bane's sound has been described as a blend of traditional American hardcore punk and contemporary metallic hardcore. Their lyrics address a variety of topics, including personal relationships, politics, and the hardcore punk ethos. Their 2004 album, The Note, was praised for its anti-war message. The band has maintained a strong DIY aesthetic throughout its career, self-releasing a majority of their material and only signing to Equal Vision Records in 2011 with the release of their final full-length album, Don't Wait Up.

Bane was an American hardcore punk band from Washington, D.C. that is now based in San Francisco, California. Founded in 1995, the group consists of lead vocalist Aaron Bedard, guitarists Aaron Dalbec and Zach Jordan, bass guitarist Jon Eaton, and drummer Bobot Adccionado. The band has released four albums and several singles subject to a loyal and dedicated underground fanbase. Though often associated with hardcore punk, some of their releases received recognition from punk rock and alternative rock publications. They incorporated genres such as punk rock, crust punk, alternative rock, thrash metal, progressive rock, and melodic hardcore into their sound, pushing their style forward from album to album. The band announced its dissolution on January 19, 2017.

Bane is a hardcore punk band from Virginia, formed in 1995. The current members are singer Aaron Bedard, guitarist and founding member Zach Jordan, bassist Aaron Dalbec, and drummer Damien Moyal.

The band has released five studio albums and several singles, and their style and lyrics often tackle issues such as politics, social justice, and personal offerings. They also have a strong D.I.Y ethic and have put out much of their work on their own label, End Hits Records. Though the band has had multiple line-up changes throughout its career, their sound has stayed relatively constant, with strong hardcore and punk leanings.

"Bane (formerly BANE) is an American straight edge hardcore punk band from Massachusetts. It was formed in 1995 by Aaron Bedard, Aaron Dalbec and Damian Genuardi. The band is currently pursuing a passionate groove oriented direction utilizing dark yet melodic song structures while lyrically retaining the aggression and militancy of their hardline roots. Throughout their career, the band has toured extensively throughout Europe, Japan, as well as the United States extensively. The band is credited by some as having spearheaded a musical trend back in the late 1990s which came to be known as "Light Insanity". 

Bane has had a total of three full-length albums, all of which have received predominately positive reviews. Their most recent album is titled "Don't Wait Up", released in 2014. The band has announced a reformation in 2015, but no further information is available yet. Despite the 2015 reformation, the band has not announced any performances, or any new music since their 2014 album."

Bane is a hardcore punk band formed in 1995 in East Tremont, New York. The band consists of lead vocalist Aaron Bedard, guitarists Zach Jordan and Aaron Dalbec, bassist Joe Kilcoyne, and drummer Damian Morris. The band released their debut EP, Holding This Moment, in 1996 and has since released five studio albums: Give Blood (2000), It All Comes Down To This (2001), The Note (2005), The Fire Rises (2010), and Don't Wait Up (2014). The band is known for its intense live performances, which have included members of the band throwing stage equipment around, crowd surfing, and launching audience members off the stage. Bane has been cited as a major influence by many hardcore and punk bands and is often viewed as one of the most influential bands of the genre.

Bane is an American hardcore punk band formed in 1995 in East Bridgewater, Massachusetts. They are considered one of the prominent bands in the mid to late 1990s within the genre. Their final full-length album, Don't Wait Up, was released in 2014. The band has achieved a cult following due to their Confrontation and Give Blood albums and has been cited as a major influence on modern hardcore by Allmusic and Decibel Magazine. With a sound built around heavy guitar riffs and raw, emotional themes of inner-city struggle and defiance, Bane has been cited as musically influential to bands such as Have Heart, Terror, and Shipwreck.

Bane is an American hardcore punk band from East Greenwich, Rhode Island. Founded in 1995, the band consists of Aaron Bedard (vocals), Zach Jordan (guitar/vocals), Damian Schmidt (bass/vocals), and Jake Churton (drums). Over the course of the band's career, they have released four full-length albums, two EPs, and numerous demos, splits and compilations.

Their lyrical contents center around themes of friendship, loyalty, and politics.   Bane has been credited as one of the leaders of the melodic hardcore genre, blending driving fast hardcore with emotional and melodic singing. The band has managed to grow their fanbase through relentless touring, especially in Europe. In 2009, the band toured with Other Plans, Death Before Dishonor, Have Heart, and Bane toured again in 2010 with Very Little.

Bane is a punk rock band from Fairfax, Virginia. Formed in 1995, the band consists of Aaron Bedard - vocals, Zach Jordan - bass, Shaun L crisp - guitar, and Aaron Dalbec - guitar, who also sings. They have released four full-length albums, as well as numerous EPs, splits, compilations, live albums, and DVDs. Their music is often described as a mix of the politically charged punk of their native Washington DC, and of their home state of Virginia. The band has earned a reputation for passionate and energetic live performances, touring both nationally and internationally. Their lyrics are often concerned with human suffering, political oppression, and oppression of the working class.

Bane is a hardcore punk band from East Troy, Wisconsin formed in 1995. Founded by Aaron Bedard, the group developed a large following due to word of mouth, relentless touring and associating with well-known punk acts. Initially heavily influenced by bands like Earth Crisis and Madball, by the time of their 2003 album, The Note, the band had begun to incorporate more melody and structure into their sound. After several EPs, split records, and full-length releases on various independent record labels, they released their sixth studio album, Don't Wait Up, on June 24, 2014.

Edward Synge (1714 – 16 August 1798) was an Irish Anglican prelate who served as the Bishop of Elphin from 1773 to 1778 and the Archbishop of Tuam from 1778 until his death in 1798.

Synge was literate in Irish tax records and local lore, and collected a large number of manuscripts and printed books, some of which remain in the library at Tuam cathedral to this day.

Edward Synge (c. 1700 – 7 November 1762) was an Irish archbishop in the Church of Ireland in the 18th century. He held the office of Archbishop of Tuam from 1742 until his death in 1762.

main_passage:
Sylvain Barrier (born 28 April 1992) is a French motorcycle racer. He is the 2014 FIM Superstock 1000 Cup champion.

Barrier began his career in the French 125cc Championship in 2006, before moving to the European Superstock 600 Championship in 2008. He finished third in the championship in 2009, and second in 2010. In 2011, he moved to the FIM Superstock 1000 Cup, where he finished fourth in the championship.

In 2012, Barrier won the FIM Superstock 1000 Cup championship, becoming the first French rider to do so. He defended his title in 2013, becoming the first rider to win back-to-back championships in the series. In 2014, Barrier won his third consecutive championship, becoming the first rider to win three consecutive titles in the series.

Barrier currently competes in the FIM Superbike World Championship, riding for the Grillini Racing Team.

main_passage: The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified as the reincarnation of the Second Dalai Lama, Gendun Gyatso, and enthroned at the age of seven in 1550. He was a monk of the Gelug school of Tibetan Buddhism, and is credited with unifying the political and spiritual authority of Tibet. He established the Ganden Phodrang government, which governed Tibet until the mid-17th century. He was a prolific writer, and wrote many important works on Tibetan Buddhism, including a commentary on the Kalachakra Tantra. He also wrote a treatise on the unification of Tibet and the Mongolian regions to the east. He was also a great patron of the arts, and was responsible for the construction of many temples and monasteries. He died in 1588 at the age of 45.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588) was a Tibetan religious leader and the third incarnation of the Dalai Lamas in Tibet, the head of the Gelugpa lineage. He was considered to be the most important Tibetan religious leader of the 16th century. Sonam Gyatso was born in 1543 in the Tsang region of Tibet. He was recognized as the third Dalai Lama in 1546 when he was just three years old. He attended the great institutions of learning in Lhasa such as Sera, Drepung and Ganden. He founded a monastery named Drepung Loseling, where he received the most intensive training in Buddhist philosophy and practice. He traveled to Mongolia and placed himself at the court of Altan Khan, from whom he received the title of Dalai Lama. Altan Khan also conferred on him the title of "Lord of the Ocean" as well as precious gifts in recognition of his spiritual attainments. He died in 1588 in Mongolia.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588) was identified by the Mongolian emperor Altan Khan as the third incarnation of Gyalwa Gendün Drup, a disciple of Tsongkhapa and one of the principal founders of the Gelug School of Tibetan Buddhism. Sonam Gyatso was born in Wokhar Monastery, Rakhog, in modern day Qinghai province in China. He had been installed as the abbot of Drepung Monastery in 1551, at the age of eight. He received the full vows of a monk from the First Dalai Lama, Gendün Drup, in 1559 and wrote a commentary on the Kalachakra. After the death of the Third Dalai Lama, the title "Dalai Lama" would only pass from master to student and is still most commonly associated with the Gelug School of Tibetan Buddhism.

Sonam Gyatso received his monastic education at Drepung Monastery, and was considered one of the most important Gelug lamas of the 16th century. He spent most of his life travelling through Tibet and neighbouring countries, spreading Gelug practices and edifying the faith of devoted adherents.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588) was identified as the reincarnation of Sonam Rabten, the second dalai lama, when he was three years old. Gyatso was born in Yulshul, Tibet, to a farming family. He was first recognized as the reincarnation of Sonam Rabten by the leader of Ü and confirmed by the leader of Shigatse. The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified as the reincarnation of Yonten Gyatso and enthroned at Drepung Monastery in 1543. He was recognized as a great scholar and teacher, and one of the greatest reincarnated lamas of his time. He met the Mongol ruler Altan Khan in 1571 and developed a close relationship with him and his successors. He obtained political support from Mongol rulers for the Dalai Lama’s authority and reforms of Tibetan Buddhism. Due to his efforts, the Dalai Lamas received the title of “head of the government of Tibet” from the Mongolian court. During his rule, he routinely made up stories to maintain the peace between Tibet and Mongolia, while at the same time, he waged war against rival sects of Tibetan Buddhism. He also taught Buddhism to Mongolians, wrote several Tibetan and Mongolian language religious texts, and created new monasteries.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (Tibetan: བསོད་ནམས་རྒྱ་མཚོ་, Wylie: bsod-nams-rgya-mtsho; 1543–1588) was the third Dalai Lama and reputedly an incarnation of the historical Buddha Avalokiteśvara (Tibetan: སྤྱན་འཁོར་, Wylie: spyan-'khr). He was identified as the reincarnation of Gedun Gyatso, the second Dalai Lama, by Drogön Chögyal Phagpa, the imperial preceptor of Kublai Khan. The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588) was identified as the reincarnation of the First Dalai Lama at the age of two by the governor of Tsang and the great scholar, Vairotsana. He was enthroned in likeness of a Kashag ruler holding a jewelled ceremony in 1553 at the age of ten. In 1578 he called a national council to extend his authority to all the provinces of Tibet, and the fifth Dalai Lama in a later period declared Sonam Gyatso as the epitome of all three Dalai Lamas. He was a great lover of Peking opera and music, and experienced political relations with China's rulers. In 1571, the Altan Khan of Mongolia gave him religious titles and the distinction Hofogi (Dharma-Heir Apparent). His presence and influence reached far beyond Tibet and Mongolia, and his intervention was sought in various disputes throughout East and Central Asia. He played an important role in the Tibetan success in the battle of Dartsedo in 1578. In 1588 his rule was cut short when he died prematurely.

The Third Dalai Lama Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588) was the third Dalai Lama. He was identified and recognized as the third incarnation of the Dalai Lama by the Mongolian prince Altan Khan in 1578. He furthered relations between Mongolia and Tibet and is credited with the introduction of the Tibetan Mantra, or "Seven Line Prayer," in Mongolia. He was instrumental in the Tibetan ordination of Sönam Choklang, the first abbot of Drepung Monastery in 1577. He established Tashi Lhunpo Monastery in Shigatse as his main seat in Tibet. Known for his wisdom and compassion, the Third Dalai Lama wrote many important works on Buddhist philosophy and was well-known throughout Tibet.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified as the reincarnation of the First Dalai Lama, Gendun Drup, and a descendant of Dromtön, a disciple of Tsongkhapa, the founder of the Gelug school. After several years of meditative and monastic training, during which he took the vows of a novice monk, he was officially installed as the Dalai Lama when he was sixteen. Despite his political and spiritual authority, he withdrew from formal government and instead devoted himself to tantric studies. He was known for his works on Vajrayana Buddhism and for leaving behind an account of his life.

The Third Dalai Lama maintained relationships with rulers of Tibet's surrounding countries, even sending embassies to the Ming court in Beijing. He established diplomatic ties with the Mongol leaders, who had supported the Gelug school since its founding in the seventeenth century. He attempted to maintain a space of political neutrality between China and Mongolia, but also sent armies to fight against various Mongol clans in the 1570s. The third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), is known for having unified Tibet and reconnected it with Mongolia. He founded the Tashi Lhunpo Monastery in Shigatse, and was a major contributor to the Tibetan Buddhist philosophical tradition. During his lifetime, Sonam Gyatso also worked to organize the Gelug tradition of Tibetan Buddhism and to reconcile differences between the two traditions of Tibetan Buddhism, the Gelug and the Sakya. Sonam Gyatso was considered a great political and religious leader. He was also known for his diplomatic skill; he hosted many envoys until his death in 1588. He sent the first Tibetan diplomatic mission to the Ming emperor of China in 1578, which resulted in further recognition of the Dalai Lamas as well as the title of "Dalai Lama."

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified and enthroned as the third Dalai Lama in 1543 at the age of thirteen by Altan Khan, a Mongol military leader and religious figure, and later by the Mongol ruler of Tibet, Guge Minghmar. He became a great political and spiritual leader who established himself and his dynasty as the Tibetan pre-eminent authority. He established a Buddhist theocracy based on a combination of Tibetan Buddhist beliefs and Mongolian shamanism, becoming the absolute religious and temporal power of Tibet and Mongolia. He also sought recognition from Chinese emperors who, though consistent in their acknowledgement of the Dalai Lama's pre-eminent position in Tibet, did not confer any special titles or symbols that would contest the right of the Gelugpa to rule Tibet. His life is documented in a biography named "The Jewel Ornament of Liberation".

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588) was born in the Wono Valley of Ü-Tsang, Tibet, most probably in 1543. He was recognized as the successor to the Second Dalai Lama, Gedun Gyatso, in 1544. Sonam Gyatso was the preceptor of the Zen school of Buddhism, although he embraced all others. He was the first Dalai Lama to be offered and accept the title of 'Dalai Lama'. He also set out the official Tibetan position on the Dzungar people, who had been wreaking havoc upon Tibet. As a religious leader, he issued several disciplinary decreed which threatened followers of sects other than his own with corporal or spiritual punishment. He also convened a large scale ordination ceremony in 1578. He died in 1588 and Lama Zopa Rinpoche considers him the incarnation of Manjushri, the bodhisattva of wisdom.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified as the incarnation of the Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva of Compassion by the Panchen Lama on the basis of an interpretation of various physical signs, dreams, and auspicious coincidences. Sonam Gyatso was the first of the five Dalai Lamas to be recognized as an incarnation of Avalokiteśvara by the spiritual leader of the Gelug school of Tibetan Buddhism, the Panchen Lama as well as one of Tibet's main spiritual figures and a political ally. He was born to a noble family, and was recognised as the reincarnation of the previous Dalai Lama, Gendun Gyatso by Khedrup Drakpa Gyaltsen, the Abott of Drepung Monastery in Amdo, on the basis of dreams, physical signs and ecstasies of deities witnessed by monks. Sonam Gyatso was placed in the care of Abbot Khedrup and thus was ordained as a novice monk. As such, Sonam Gyatso established a long-

The third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified and enthroned in 1578 at the age of 15 to succeed the deceased second Dalai Lama, Gedun Gyatso. He soon went to Mongolia, where Altan Khan granted him the title (Tib. ཏཱ་ལའི་བླ་མ་, Wylie: ta la'i bla ma, Short biographical description of the Third Dalai Lama) "Dalai Lama". He initiated, and after his death his reincarnation was identified and enthroned as the fourth Dalai Lama, Yonten Gyatso (1589–1617).He established the first systematic and recognizable government in Tibet, placing the power in the hands of the Dalai Lamas and their government, the Ganden Phodrang.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588) was an important figure in Tibetan history and was the leader who unified the country under a single spiritual and temporal leader, paving the way for the Dalai Lamas to become holders of both spiritual and secular power. He received both his religious and his secular education at Drepung Monastery, the largest monastery in Tibet, and soon after becoming abbot of Drepung at the age of thirty-one, he was recognized by the Mongol ruler Altan Khan as the reincarnation of the First Dalai Lama and offered his patronage. Sonam Gyatso was maximally successful in unifying Tibet's myriad of tribes, estates, and monasteries, and his administrative structure, focusing on the incarnate lineage of Sakya Pandita as the highest leader of both political and spiritual matters in Tibet, remains largely intact today. He also composed a varied amount of works on Buddhist philosophy and wrote several great volumes on Tibetan historiography.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified as the reincarnation of the Second Dalai Lama at the age of five, and was politically and religiously influential in Tibet. He studied at Drepung Monastery under its abbot, Losang Chökyi Gyaltsen, an abbot of the Gelug school and tutor to the Fourth and Fifth Dalai Lamas. Sonam Gyatso gained an important advantage when Altan Khan, leader of the Tümed Mongols, adopted Buddhism as his faith, enabling the Dalai Lamas to gain political support. Altan Khan conferred titles upon Sonam Gyatso and declared that he was the third incarnation of the Dalai Lama. He also peaceably helped the Tibetans by turning against the Chinese and the Karakhanids.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified and enthroned as the Third Dalai Lama by Altan Khan of the Tümed in the province of Kokonor at the great temple of White Jina in 1578, at the age of fifteen. He subsequently received the Mongolian title of Dalai Lama, which made him a spiritual leader not only for the Mongols, but eventually for all Tibetan Buddhism. He extended the influence of the Tibetans to Mongol realms as far as China, and exerted a strong influence in the field of politics and religion in Central Asia until his death in 1588. He was instrumental in the formation of a clear religious identity among Mongols and Tibetans, unifying them in a unified Mongolic identity under the influence of Tibetan Buddhism. He built bridges between his own people and their powerful neighbours, and progressively developed Tibet's standing as a regional power. Tibetans revere the Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso, as an emanation of Avalokitesvara.

The Third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was identified as the incarnation of the earlier Dalai Lamas and invited to Takebhutan and central Tibet as a guest of the aboriginal ruler of Tibet and established himself as the spiritual leader of Tibet by conquering major fortresses, fortifying the nation, and reviving Lamaist Buddhism. Revered in Tibet, he is known for his efforts to propagate Lamaism, and exerted strong religious and secular authority in Tibet. He is also credited with the revival of the Kalachakra teachings. He wrote several important treatises on sidhy and philosophy, including The Precious Garland of Tenets of the Sect and Forms of Knowledge which summarise important aspects of the doctrines of various tantras.

The third Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso (1543–1588), was born in the Nyethang district of Ü-Tsang (now under Chinese rule as Xigazê Prefecture, Tibet Autonomous Region). Sonam Gyatso was recognized as the third incarnation of the Dalai Lama in 1542, shortly after the death of the second Dalai Lama. He was the first to be accorded this recognition by all major divergent sects of Tibet, and also the first to be recognized by the newly-unified Mongol leader Altan Khan, who awarded the title to Sonam Gyatso.

Sonam Gyatso was a particularly active and literary scholar. He was responsible for revenging the death of Tsangpa Gyare, the founder of the Drukpa Order, and played a major role in developing it as one of the major schools of Tibetan Buddhism. His knowledge and enthusiasm also spread far to other regions like Ladakh and Bhutan. He was a monk of the Gelug school of Tibetan Buddhism, and is credited with unifying the political and spiritual authority of Tibet.
He established the Ganden Phodrang government, which governed Tibet until the mid-17th century.
He was a prolific writer, and wrote many important works on Tibetan Buddhism, including a commentary on the Kalachakra Tantra.
